## Assistant (2026-04-18 09:12:32)

I’m narrowing onto the cubic-leading section itself and the exact hypotheses it currently uses. Then I’ll rewrite it into a form that separates what is proved, what is generic, and what still needs a lemma.

The useful sharpening is this: the scary-looking hypothesis \(B(h)\not\equiv 0\) is not just a vague “genericity” placeholder. It is equivalent to excluding a very special nonlinear ODE, so this part can be made much crisper than it currently is.

_Thought for 3m 2s_

Yes. The current cubic proposition is very close, but it still has two soft spots:

First, it uses hypotheses like \(v(h)\not\equiv 0\) and \(B(h)\not\equiv 0\), then states a pointwise conclusion \([J_3^{\pair}(h)]\neq 0\). That should be converted into a statement on an explicit open dense region \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}=\{h:\,v(h)B(h)\neq 0\}\). Second, the \(B(h)\not\equiv 0\) hypothesis can be sharpened a lot: it is not an opaque genericity assumption, it excludes one very special nonlinear ODE. That is exactly the kind of cleanup this section wants. turn9file5turn9file8

The structural input you already have is enough for this freeze: the cubic value-family is confined to the off-diagonal sector, while the actual cubic jet has diagonal part
\[
\operatorname{diag}J_3^{\pair}(h)
=
v(h)B(h)(x_1-x_2)\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
B(h)=\frac{q(h)^2q'(h)}{4}-\frac{q'''(h)}{48}.
\]
So any point where \(v(h)B(h)(x_1-x_2)\neq 0\) gives an immediate nonzero cubic quotient class. turn9file2

The extra sharpening is:
\[
B(h)=0
\iff
q'''(h)=12q(h)^2q'(h)
\iff
\bigl(q''(h)-4q(h)^3\bigr)'=0.
\]
So \(B\equiv 0\) on a connected interval if and only if
\[
q''=4q^3+C
\]
for some constant \(C\). That makes the cubic hypothesis much cleaner: you are excluding a rigid special ODE, not appealing to vague genericity.

Here is the version I would actually put in the paper.

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Rigidity of the cubic scalar \(B\)]
\label{lem:B-rigidity}
Let \(I\) be a connected parameter interval, and define
\[
B(h):=\frac{q(h)^2q'(h)}{4}-\frac{q'''(h)}{48}.
\]
Then the following are equivalent:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(B\equiv 0\) on \(I\);
\item \(q'''=12q^2q'\) on \(I\);
\item there exists a constant \(C_0\in \mathbf C\) such that
\[
q''=4q^3+C_0
\qquad\text{on }I.
\]
\end{enumerate}
In particular, the hypothesis \(B\not\equiv 0\) is equivalent to excluding
the special cubic ODE \(q''=4q^3+C_0\).
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
By definition,
\[
48\,B = 12q^2q' - q'''.
\]
Hence \(B\equiv 0\) is equivalent to
\[
q'''=(4q^3)'.
\]
Integrating on the connected interval \(I\) gives
\[
q''=4q^3+C_0
\]
for some constant \(C_0\in \mathbf C\). The converse is immediate by
differentiation.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Generic cubic leading quotient order]
\label{prop:cubic-leading-order}
Let \(I\) be a connected one-pair parameter interval, and let
\(J_3^{\pair}(h)\) denote the cubic jet of the normalized one-pair family.

Assume:
\begin{enumerate}
\item the cubic value-family satisfies
\[
V_{\val}^{(3)}
\subset
\left\{
\begin{pmatrix}
0&*\\ *&0
\end{pmatrix}
\right\};
\]
\item the background same-point block is nondegenerate, so that the diagonal
entries of \(X_0=G_0^{-1/2}\) satisfy \(x_1\neq x_2\);
\item the scalar \(v(h)\) is not identically zero on \(I\);
\item \(B(h)\not\equiv 0\) on \(I\), equivalently, by
Lemma~\ref{lem:B-rigidity}, \(q\) does not satisfy
\[
q''=4q^3+C_0
\]
identically on \(I\).
\end{enumerate}

Define the cubic-generic region
\[
\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}
:=
\{\,h\in I:\ v(h)B(h)\neq 0\,\}.
\]
Then \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\) is open and dense in \(I\), and for every
\(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\),
\[
[J_3^{\pair}(h)]\neq 0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathcal J_3/V_{\val}^{(3)}.
\]

More precisely, the image of \(J_3^{\pair}(h)\) in the cubic quotient has
nonzero diagonal-traceless component
\[
v(h)\,B(h)\,(x_1-x_2)
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&-1
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Consequently,
\[
F_h(z)=v_0(h)z^3+v_1(h)z^5+\cdots,
\qquad
v_0(h):=[J_3^{\pair}(h)]\neq 0
\]
for \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Since the first quotient order is
generically trivial, the generic leading quotient order is therefore exactly
\(3\) on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By assumption,
\[
\operatorname{diag}J_3^{\pair}(h)
=
v(h)\,B(h)\,(x_1-x_2)
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&-1
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Because \(V_{\val}^{(3)}\) is confined to the off-diagonal sector, it cannot
cancel any nonzero diagonal component. Hence for every \(h\) with
\(v(h)B(h)(x_1-x_2)\neq 0\), the cubic quotient class
\([J_3^{\pair}(h)]\) is nonzero.

Since \(x_1\neq x_2\) is fixed and both \(v\) and \(B\) are not identically
zero, the set \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\) is nonempty; if the dependence on
\(h\) is real-analytic, its complement is a proper analytic subset, hence
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\) is open and dense. Finally,
Proposition~\ref{prop:first-order-quotient-trivial} shows that the leading
quotient order is at least \(3\), while the present argument shows that it is
equal to \(3\) on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What is genuinely generic here]
\label{rem:cubic-leading-genericity}
The cubic-leading statement depends on three logically distinct inputs:
\begin{enumerate}
\item \(x_1\neq x_2\), a background same-point nondegeneracy condition;
\item \(v\not\equiv 0\), a one-pair nonfreezing condition;
\item exclusion of the special ODE \(q''=4q^3+C_0\), equivalently
\(B\not\equiv 0\).
\end{enumerate}
This separates the structural cubic mechanism from the residual quintic
bookkeeping needed later.
\end{remark}
```

Two small judgment calls are built into that patch.

One is that I changed the conclusion from “generic one-pair region” to the explicit set \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). That makes the logic honest.

The other is that I stated the cubic class has a nonzero diagonal-traceless component, rather than overclaiming that the whole cubic quotient line is literally a fixed diagonal line. What you have proved is enough for the leading-order theorem and for later trace transversality, because the entire cubic class is still trace-free: its diagonal part is traceless and the value-family contribution is off-diagonal. turn9file9

So this freezes the cubic step in a form I’d regard as stable. After this, the next live task is the quintic no-cancellation lemma, but now it sits on top of a cleaner base rather than an overcompressed “generic cubic” proposition.

and the current witness is the \(q^{(5)}\) term inside the linear \(G_3\)-part of \(\gamma-\alpha\), multiplied by the \(\eta_2\)-term in \(v\).

That is already the draft’s own heuristic, and it is the right one. turn14file4

I pushed that internal competitor directly.

Model just the relevant bilinear interaction:
take a diagonal background
\[
G_0=\mathrm{diag}(a^2,b^2),\qquad X_0=G_0^{-1/2}=\mathrm{diag}(1/a,1/b),
\]
an off-diagonal first-order perturbation
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}0&u\\u&0\end{pmatrix}
\]
for the \(\eta_2\)-channel, and a diagonal third-order perturbation
\[
D=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&d\end{pmatrix}
\]
for the \(q^{(5)}\)-channel.

Now expand
\[
X=(G_0+\varepsilon A+\delta D)^{-1/2}
= X_0+\varepsilon U+\delta V+\varepsilon\delta W+\cdots
\]
by solving \(XGX=I\) order by order. The relevant coefficients come out as

\[
U_{12}=-\frac{u}{ab(a+b)},
\qquad
V_{22}=-\frac{d}{2b^3},
\qquad
W_{12}=\frac{du(a+2b)}{2ab^3(a+b)^2}.
\]

Interpretation:
\(U_{12}\) is the \(v\)-channel,
\(V_{22}\) is the \(q^{(5)}\)-piece of \(\gamma-\alpha\),
and \(W_{12}\) is exactly the dangerous bilinear \(G_1\)-\(G_3\) contribution to \(r\).

Then the combined coefficient entering the K1 trace is
\[
(x_1-x_2)r + v(\gamma-\alpha),
\]
and for this bilinear datum it simplifies to
\[
\Bigl(\frac1a-\frac1b\Bigr)W_{12}+U_{12}V_{22}
=
\frac{du}{a^2b^2(a+b)^2}.
\]

That is nonzero.

So, under the current placement of the \(q^{(5)}\) witness in the diagonal \(G_3\)-channel, the dangerous \(r\)-term does **not** cancel the known \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\) witness. It actually combines with it into a clean nonzero coefficient.

\[

which is exactly total odd order \(1+1+3=5\). That is already encoded in the quintic trace witness formula in the draft. 

That observation kills most possible competitors immediately.

A same-point quintic term cannot contribute to \(M\) because it has no \(\kappa\)-factor at all. A \(K_5\)-transport term also cannot contribute, because once transport has already consumed order \(5\), there is no room left for the extra \(\eta_2\) and \(q^{(5)}\) factors. A \(K_3\)-transport term is also out: after spending order \(3\) on transport, only order \(2\) remains, but \(q^{(5)}\) does not appear that early. So the target monomial can only live in the \(K_1\)-sector. That is the conceptual core of the uniqueness argument. turn19file4

Inside the \(K_1\)-sector, the quintic trace is
\[

\]
So the problem is now completely local: which of these three channels can actually carry \(M\)? turn21file8

The \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\) channel definitely carries \(M\). That is the original witness. From the draft,
\[
v(h)=-\frac{1}{4\pi(s_1+s_2)}\eta_2(h),
\]
and the linear \(G_3\)-part of \(\gamma-\alpha\) contains a nonzero \(q^{(5)}\)-term, so \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\) contains \(M\) with nonzero coefficient. 

Now for the other two channels.

The \(\beta(u-w)\) channel is not a real competitor. The reason is matrix placement. The linear \(q^{(5)}\)-piece of \(X_3\) is diagonal, not off-diagonal; that is exactly why it appears in \(\gamma-\alpha\). So \(q^{(5)}\) does not enter \(\beta\) at linear order. Meanwhile, when you combine the off-diagonal first-order \(\eta_2\)-piece with that diagonal \(q^{(5)}\)-piece through the inverse-square-root expansion, the resulting bilinear order-\(4\) correction lands in the off-diagonal entry of \(X_4\), not in the diagonal difference \(u-w\). So \(\beta(u-w)\) misses \(M\) from both sides: \(\beta\) misses the linear \(q^{(5)}\), and \(u-w\) misses the bilinear \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\). That leaves only the \(r\)-channel as a possible internal competitor.

And that is the good news: the \(r\)-channel does not cancel the witness.

If you linearize around a diagonal background
\[
G_0=\operatorname{diag}(a^2,b^2),
\]
take an off-diagonal first-order perturbation
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}0&u\\u&0\end{pmatrix}
\]
for the \(\eta_2\)-source, and a diagonal third-order perturbation
\[
D=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&d\end{pmatrix}
\]
for the \(q^{(5)}\)-source, then in the expansion
\[
X=(G_0+\varepsilon A+\delta D)^{-1/2}
\]
the relevant coefficients are
\[
U_{12}=-\frac{u}{ab(a+b)},\qquad
V_{22}=-\frac{d}{2b^3},\qquad
W_{12}=\frac{du(a+2b)}{2ab^3(a+b)^2}.
\]

Here \(U_{12}\) is the \(v\)-piece, \(V_{22}\) is the \(q^{(5)}\)-piece of \(\gamma-\alpha\), and \(W_{12}\) is exactly the dangerous bilinear \(X_4\)-piece feeding \(r\). The total coefficient entering
\[
(x_1-x_2)r+v(\gamma-\alpha)
\]
then becomes
\[
\Bigl(\frac1a-\frac1b\Bigr)W_{12}+U_{12}V_{22}
=
\frac{du}{a^2b^2(a+b)^2},
\]
which is nonzero.

So the internal \(K_1\)-story is now basically settled:

the only channels that can hit \(M\) are \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\) and \((x_1-x_2)r\), and their combined coefficient is nonzero rather than canceling.

That means the no-cancellation lemma has been reduced to one last narrow bookkeeping check:

you need to confirm from the exact same-point expansion that the \(q^{(5)}\)-piece really first enters through the diagonal \(G_3\)-slice and its induced bilinear \(X_4\)-correction, with no hidden off-diagonal \(q^{(5)}\)-term elsewhere. \eta_2 q^{(5)}\text{ appears bilinearly only in }r,

and then the coefficient computation above closes the monomial-isolation argument.

\[

\]
So the only place a \(q^{(5)}\) term can first appear is in the cubic \(s\)-expansion of the \((2,2)\)-entry, because that is where \(q''(t_\pm)\) contributes \(q^{(5)}(m)s^3\). It does **not** appear in the lower same-point jets \(G_1\) or \(G_2\). 

That is exactly consistent with the formula already in the draft:
the linear \(G_3\)-contribution to \(\gamma-\alpha\) contains the term
\[
-\frac{x_2^3}{2\pi\cdot 576}\,q^{(5)}(h),
\]
so the \(q^{(5)}\)-witness is entering through the diagonal-traceless part of \(X_3\). turn22file4

Now add the matrix-parity viewpoint.

Let \(E\) denote the diagonal subspace and \(O\) the off-diagonal subspace. Relative to the diagonal baseline \(G_0\) and \(X_0=G_0^{-1/2}\), multiplication respects
\[
E\cdot E\subset E,\qquad E\cdot O\subset O,\qquad O\cdot E\subset O,\qquad O\cdot O\subset E.
\]

The \(\eta_2\)-source sits in \(G_1\), hence in \(X_1\), and it is off-diagonal:
\[
X_1^{(\eta_2)}\in O.
\]
The \(q^{(5)}\)-source sits linearly in \(G_3\), hence linearly in \(X_3\), and it is diagonal:
\[
X_3^{(q^{(5)})}\in E.
\]

Once you phrase it that way, the remaining channels become almost forced.

The target monomial \(M=\kappa\,\eta_2\,q^{(5)}\) has transport degree \(K_1\) and same-point degree \(X_1\cdot X_3\). That immediately excludes:

- same-point quintic terms, because they have no \(\kappa\);
- \(K_5\)-terms, because there is no room left for \(X_1\cdot X_3\);
- \(K_3\)-terms, because after using \(K_3\) you only have same-point degree \(2\), but \(q^{(5)}\) first appears at same-point degree \(3\). turn22file8

So only the \(K_1\)-trace block matters:
\[
-2\kappa\bigl((x_1-x_2)r+\beta(u-w)+v(\gamma-\alpha)\bigr).
\]

Now use the parity bookkeeping:

- \(v\) comes from \(X_1\), so \(v\in O\).
- \((\gamma-\alpha)\) is the diagonal-traceless part of \(X_3\), so \((\gamma-\alpha)\in E\).
- Therefore \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\) is exactly the natural \(O\)-times-\(E\) route for the \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\) witness, and this is the one already identified in the file. turn22file8

For \(\beta(u-w)\):

- \(\beta\) is the off-diagonal part of \(X_3\), so a linear \(q^{(5)}\)-term would have to appear in the off-diagonal part of \(X_3\).
- But the \(q^{(5)}\) source enters \(G_3\) through the diagonal \((2,2)\)-entry, and the first Fréchet response of \(G\mapsto G^{-1/2}\) at a diagonal base keeps diagonal input diagonal.
- So there is no linear \(q^{(5)}\) contribution to \(\beta\).

Also, \(u-w\) is diagonal, and the bilinear \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\) correction coming from coupling \(X_1^{(\eta_2)}\in O\) with \(X_3^{(q^{(5)})}\in E\) lands in \(O\), not in \(E\). So \(u-w\) also misses the bilinear \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\) route.

That kills \(\beta(u-w)\) twice over.

That leaves only \(r\), the off-diagonal part of \(X_4\). And here the parity works in the opposite direction:
the bilinear interaction
\[
X_1^{(\eta_2)}\in O,\qquad X_3^{(q^{(5)})}\in E
\]
naturally produces an off-diagonal contribution in \(X_4\), exactly where \(r\) lives. So \(r\) is the only genuine internal competitor to \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\).

That matches the explicit model computation I gave before: inside
\[
(x_1-x_2)r+v(\gamma-\alpha)
\]
the coefficient of the \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\) slice comes out nonzero, not canceled.

So the remaining proof problem is now almost purely formal:

show that on the graded slice \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(5)})\),

1. \(X_3^{(q^{(5)})}\) is diagonal,
2. \(X_4^{(\eta_2 q^{(5)})}\) is off-diagonal,
3. therefore the only \(K_1\)-channels hitting the slice are \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\) and \((x_1-x_2)r\),
4. and their combined coefficient is nonzero.

That is much tighter than the original no-cancellation lemma. It is no longer “check every quintic term.” It is “prove one parity-and-degree isolation statement for the \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(5)})\)-slice.”

My honest view: this is now close enough that the only thing still missing is a clean abstract lemma about how the inverse-square-root expansion preserves diagonal/off-diagonal parity by degree. Once you state that properly, the uniqueness of the quintic witness should mostly fall out.

_Thought for 1m 26s_

Yes. The right lemma is an abstract parity lemma for analytic matrix functions at a diagonal basepoint.

Here is the clean statement.

Let \(E\) be the diagonal \(2\times2\) matrices and \(O\) the off-diagonal ones. Let
\[
f(z)=z^{-1/2},
\qquad
X(H):=f(G_0+H),
\]
with \(G_0=\mathrm{diag}(\lambda_1,\lambda_2)\) positive diagonal. Then every multilinear coefficient in the Taylor expansion of \(X(H)\) has a definite matrix parity:

if a term is multilinear in \(n\) perturbation factors \(H_1,\dots,H_n\), then it lies in \(E\) when an even number of the \(H_j\) lie in \(O\), and it lies in \(O\) when an odd number of the \(H_j\) lie in \(O\).

That is the lemma you want.

Why it is true:

use the holomorphic functional calculus
\[
f(G_0+H)=\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_\Gamma f(z)\,(zI-G_0-H)^{-1}\,dz,
\]
and expand the resolvent by Neumann series:
\[
(zI-G_0-H)^{-1}
=
R(z)+R(z)HR(z)+R(z)HR(z)HR(z)+\cdots,
\]
where \(R(z)=(zI-G_0)^{-1}\). Since \(G_0\) is diagonal, every \(R(z)\) is diagonal, so inserting \(R(z)\) between perturbation factors never changes diagonal/off-diagonal parity. Therefore each word
\[
R H_{i_1} R H_{i_2}\cdots R H_{i_n} R
\]
has the same parity as the product \(H_{i_1}\cdots H_{i_n}\), and in \(2\times2\) matrices that parity is exactly “even number of off-diagonal factors gives diagonal, odd number gives off-diagonal.” Integrating over \(z\) preserves that subspace. So every Taylor coefficient inherits the same parity rule.

That is the full abstract lemma.

Now apply it to your setting.

From the same-point block formula in the draft,
\[
G_{m,\pm}^{\corr}(s)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
2q(t_\pm) & \frac12 q'(t_\pm)\\[1ex]
\frac12 q'(t_\pm) & \frac1{12}(q''(t_\pm)+2q(t_\pm)^3)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
the first appearance of \(q^{(5)}\) is at cubic order in the \((2,2)\)-entry, coming from the Taylor expansion of \(q''(t_\pm)\). So the linear \(q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(G_3\) is diagonal, not off-diagonal. That is exactly consistent with the file’s witness formula for \((\gamma-\alpha)^{\lin}\), where the \(q^{(5)}\) term sits in the diagonal-traceless part of \(X_3\), not in \(\beta\). turn24file0

Also from the file,
\[
v(h)=-\frac{1}{4\pi(s_1+s_2)}\,\eta_2(h),
\]
so the \(\eta_2\)-slice of \(X_1\) is off-diagonal. 

Now the consequences are immediate.

First, since the linear \(q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(G_3\) is diagonal, the linear \(q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(X_3\) is diagonal by the lemma. So \(q^{(5)}\) can contribute to \(\gamma-\alpha\), but not to \(\beta\). That kills the “linear \(q^{(5)}\) inside \(\beta\)” route.

Second, the \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(X_4\) comes from one off-diagonal input and one diagonal input. By the lemma, that slice is off-diagonal. So it can contribute to \(r\), but not to the diagonal difference \(u-w\). That kills the \(\beta(u-w)\) route for the target monomial.

So the parity lemma gives exactly the isolation you need:

for the monomial
\[
M=\kappa\,\eta_2\,q^{(5)},
\]
the only \(K_1\)-trace channels that can possibly see it are
\[
(x_1-x_2)r
\quad\text{and}\quad
v(\gamma-\alpha),
\]
while
\[
\beta(u-w)
\]
cannot contribute to \(M\) at all.

That is the real payoff. Combined with the coefficient computation we already did, the no-cancellation problem is reduced to the sum of those two surviving channels, and that sum came out nonzero.

So the lemma is now doing the exact job you wanted: it turns the quintic witness from a vague “best candidate” into an isolated graded slice.

The only thing still left after this lemma is the tiny bookkeeping statement that \(q^{(5)}\) does indeed first enter only through that diagonal cubic same-point slice, but that is already what the file’s \(G_{m,\pm}\) formula and \((\gamma-\alpha)^{\lin}\) formula are showing. turn24file0

The first one is an order argument. The target monomial has transport tag \(\kappa\), so it must come from a \(K_1\), \(K_3\), or \(K_5\) transport contribution. But \(q^{(5)}\) first appears in same-point degree \(3\), and \(\eta_2\) contributes same-point degree \(1\). So the target slice needs same-point degree \(4\) in total. That is compatible only with \(K_1\). A \(K_3\)-term leaves only same-point degree \(2\), and a \(K_5\)-term leaves degree \(0\). So neither can carry \(\kappa\,\eta_2\,q^{(5)}\). Same-point quintic terms are also out immediately, because they have no \(\kappa\) at all. That closes the non-\(K_1\) issue.

The second one is the parity lemma. Relative to a diagonal base \(G_0\), split matrices into diagonal \(E\) and off-diagonal \(O\). For
\[
f(A)=A^{-1/2},
\]
every Taylor coefficient of \(f(G_0+H)\) preserves parity in the obvious sense: an even number of \(O\)-inputs gives a diagonal output, and an odd number gives an off-diagonal output. This is rigorous from the holomorphic functional calculus expansion
\[
f(G_0+H)=\frac1{2\pi i}\oint f(z)(zI-G_0-H)^{-1}\,dz,
\]
because \(R(z)=(zI-G_0)^{-1}\) is diagonal and so does not change parity.

\[

\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The \(q^{(5)}\)-term first appears only in the cubic expansion of the \((2,2)\)-entry, via \(q''(t_\pm)\). So the \(q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(G_3\) is diagonal. Therefore the linear \(q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(X_3\) is also diagonal, which is exactly why it appears in \(\gamma-\alpha\), not in \(\beta\). Because Taylor coefficients are multilinear in the perturbation directions, to compute the coefficient of the monomial \(\eta_2 q^{(5)}\) inside the whitening expansion, it is legitimate to restrict to the two perturbation directions that actually carry those tags and set all other directions to zero. So the reduced two-parameter family
\[

with the following.

```latex
\begin{lemma}[Parity of the whitening expansion at a diagonal base]
\label{lem:whitening-parity}
Let
\[
G_0=\diag(\lambda_1,\lambda_2),
\qquad \lambda_1,\lambda_2>0,
\]
and let
\[
X(H):=(G_0+H)^{-1/2}
\]
for \(H\) sufficiently small. Write
\[
E:=\left\{
\begin{pmatrix}
*&0\\0&*
\end{pmatrix}
\right\},
\qquad
O:=\left\{
\begin{pmatrix}
0&*\\ *&0
\end{pmatrix}
\right\}.
\]
Then every multilinear Taylor coefficient of \(X(H)\) has definite matrix
parity: a coefficient depending on perturbation factors
\(H_1,\dots,H_n\) lies in \(E\) if an even number of the \(H_j\) lie in
\(O\), and lies in \(O\) if an odd number of the \(H_j\) lie in \(O\).

In particular, diagonal input gives diagonal output at linear order, and a
bilinear coefficient with one diagonal and one off-diagonal input is
off-diagonal.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Use the holomorphic functional calculus
\[
(G_0+H)^{-1/2}
=
\frac{1}{2\pi i}\oint_{\Gamma} z^{-1/2}(zI-G_0-H)^{-1}\,dz,
\]
where \(\Gamma\) encloses the spectrum of \(G_0+H\). Expanding the resolvent,
\[
(zI-G_0-H)^{-1}
=
R(z)+R(z)HR(z)+R(z)HR(z)HR(z)+\cdots,
\]
with
\[
R(z):=(zI-G_0)^{-1},
\]
every factor \(R(z)\) is diagonal because \(G_0\) is diagonal. Hence the
matrix parity of each word
\[
R(z)H_{i_1}R(z)H_{i_2}\cdots R(z)H_{i_n}R(z)
\]
is exactly the parity of the product \(H_{i_1}\cdots H_{i_n}\). Since
\[
E\cdot E\subset E,\qquad
E\cdot O\subset O,\qquad
O\cdot E\subset O,\qquad
O\cdot O\subset E,
\]
the parity rule follows. Integrating over \(z\) preserves the subspaces
\(E\) and \(O\).
\end{proof}

\begin{lemma}[No-cancellation of the quintic trace witness]
\label{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace}
The monomial
\[
\kappa(h)\,\eta_2(h)\,q'''''\!(h)
\]
appearing in Lemma~\ref{lem:quintic-trace-witness} is not produced by any
other quintic contribution to \(\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\). More
precisely, on the graded slice corresponding to
\[
(K_1,\eta_2,q^{(5)}),
\]
the only contributing channels in
\(\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\) are
\[
-2\kappa(h)\,(x_1-x_2)r(h)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
-2\kappa(h)\,v(h)\bigl(\gamma(h)-\alpha(h)\bigr),
\]
and their total coefficient of
\(\kappa(h)\eta_2(h)q^{(5)}(h)\) is nonzero. Consequently
\[
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\not\equiv 0
\]
on the generic one-pair region.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Fix \(h\) and isolate the graded slice
\[
\kappa(h)\,\eta_2(h)\,q^{(5)}(h).
\]

We first exclude all non-\(K_1\) contributions. Same-point quintic terms
carry no \(\kappa\)-factor, so they cannot contribute. A \(K_5\)-transport
term leaves no same-point degree available, and a \(K_3\)-transport term
leaves only same-point degree \(2\), whereas the factor \(q^{(5)}(h)\)
first appears in same-point degree \(3\). Hence the target monomial can
occur only in the \(K_1\)-transport sector.

Now consider the same-point expansion. By the explicit formula
\[
G_{m,\pm}^{\corr}(s)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
2q(t_\pm) & \frac12 q'(t_\pm)\\[.5ex]
\frac12 q'(t_\pm) & \frac1{12}(q''(t_\pm)+2q(t_\pm)^3)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
the factor \(q^{(5)}(h)\) first appears only in the cubic same-point
coefficient \(G_3\), and there only in the diagonal sector, namely through
the cubic Taylor term of the \((2,2)\)-entry coming from \(q''(t_\pm)\).
Therefore, by Lemma~\ref{lem:whitening-parity}, the linear
\(q^{(5)}\)-contribution to \(X_3\) is diagonal. In particular it contributes
to \(\gamma-\alpha\), but not to \(\beta\).

Likewise, the \(\eta_2(h)\)-slice of \(X_1(h)\) is off-diagonal, because
\[
v(h)=-\frac{1}{4\pi(s_1+s_2)}\,\eta_2(h).
\]
Applying Lemma~\ref{lem:whitening-parity} again, the mixed
\((\eta_2,q^{(5)})\)-slice of \(X_4\) is off-diagonal. Hence it contributes
to \(r\), but not to the diagonal difference \(u-w\).

It follows that on the graded slice
\((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(5)})\), the term
\[
-2\kappa(h)\,\beta(h)\bigl(u(h)-w(h)\bigr)
\]
does not contribute at all. Thus the only surviving \(K_1\)-channels are
\[
-2\kappa(h)\,(x_1-x_2)r(h)
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
-2\kappa(h)\,v(h)\bigl(\gamma(h)-\alpha(h)\bigr).
\]

To compute their combined coefficient, it suffices by multilinearity to
restrict to the two perturbation directions carrying the relevant tags:
an off-diagonal first-order perturbation \(A\) for the \(\eta_2\)-slice and
a diagonal third-order perturbation \(D\) for the \(q^{(5)}\)-slice.
Accordingly, write
\[
G_0=\diag(a^2,b^2),
\qquad
A=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&u\\u&0
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
D=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&0\\0&d
\end{pmatrix},
\]
and expand
\[
X(\varepsilon,\delta):=(G_0+\varepsilon A+\delta D)^{-1/2}
=
X_0+\varepsilon U+\delta V+\varepsilon\delta W+O(\varepsilon^2,\delta^2).
\]
Solving the identity
\[
X(\varepsilon,\delta)\,(G_0+\varepsilon A+\delta D)\,X(\varepsilon,\delta)=I
\]
order by order gives
\[
U_{12}=-\frac{u}{ab(a+b)},
\qquad
V_{22}=-\frac{d}{2b^3},
\qquad
W_{12}=\frac{du(a+2b)}{2ab^3(a+b)^2}.
\]
Here \(U_{12}\) is the \(\eta_2\)-slice of \(v\), \(V_{22}\) is the
\(q^{(5)}\)-slice of \(\gamma-\alpha\), and \(W_{12}\) is the mixed
\((\eta_2,q^{(5)})\)-slice of \(r\). Therefore the total coefficient of the
target slice inside
\[
(x_1-x_2)r+v(\gamma-\alpha)
\]
is
\[
\left(\frac1a-\frac1b\right)W_{12}+U_{12}V_{22}
=
\frac{du}{a^2b^2(a+b)^2},
\]
which is nonzero.

Thus the monomial
\(\kappa(h)\eta_2(h)q^{(5)}(h)\) occurs in
\(\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\) with nonzero total coefficient and is not
canceled by any other quintic contribution. Hence
\[
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\not\equiv 0.
\]
\end{proof}
```

## Assistant (2026-04-18 10:20:39)

I’m checking the exact proposition that line belongs to, because the answer depends on whether it’s the local trace lemma or the residual-class proposition built on top of the cubic line.

_Thought for 32s_

Not in that form.

At this point, for the **first residual class proposition**, you do still need the **conclusion** of the cubic-leading theorem — namely that on the cubic-generic region the cubic quotient coefficient \(v_0(h)\) is nonzero and defines the leading line \(\ell_h\). The proof of the proposition uses exactly that, then uses the nonzero quintic trace to show \(v_1(h)\notin \ell_h\). 

But you do **not** need to restate the full raw hypotheses of Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order} there if that proposition has already been proved. So this

```latex
Assume the generic nondegeneracy hypotheses of Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}.
```

is stronger and more awkward than necessary. The cleaner statement is to work **on the cubic-generic region** from Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, or equivalently to assume \(v_0(h)\neq 0\) so that \(\ell_h\) is defined. The file’s own proof only uses that downstream conclusion, not the whole hypothesis list again. turn30file8

So I would change the proposition opening to something like:

```latex
On the cubic-generic region \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\) of Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order},
assume Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace}.
Then the first residual class is nonzero:
...
```

or even more directly:

```latex
Assume \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), so that \(v_0(h)\neq 0\) and \(\ell_h\) is defined.
Assume also Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace}.
Then ...
```

That is the honest dependency structure. The cubic proposition supplies the region and the line; the quintic lemma supplies transversality. You no longer need to drag the whole cubic hypothesis list into the residual proposition.

Once the no-cancellation lemma is **proved**, it should not appear as an “assumption” in the proposition statement. And the cubic proposition should only enter through its **output** \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), not by restating its hypothesis list. The current proof of the residual proposition already uses exactly those downstream facts: from the cubic proposition, \(v_0(h)\neq 0\) and \(\ell_h\) is defined; from the no-cancellation lemma, \(\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\not\equiv 0\). 

So the proposition statement should be something like:

```latex
\begin{proposition}[Nontrivial first residual class]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-nontrivial}
For \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), the first residual class is nonzero:
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\quad\text{in }Q/\ell_h.
\]
Equivalently, the quintic quotient coefficient is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
\[
v_1(h)\notin \ell_h.
\]
Hence the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant.
\end{proposition}
```

And then the proof begins:

```latex
\begin{proof}
By Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, for \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\)
the cubic quotient coefficient
\[
v_0(h)=[J_3^{\pair}(h)]
\]
is nonzero and defines the leading line \(\ell_h\).
By Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace},
\[
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\not\equiv 0.
\]
...
\end{proof}
```

That is the clean dependency structure.

So: yes, say it as **following from** \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), and use Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace} in the **proof**, not in the theorem statement. ```latex
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Nontrivial first residual class]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-nontrivial}
Assume the generic nondegeneracy hypotheses of Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}
and the no-cancellation statement of Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace}.
Then the first residual class is nonzero:
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\quad\text{in }Q/\ell_h

on the generic one-pair region.
Equivalently, the quintic quotient coefficient is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
\[
v_1(h)\notin \ell_h.
\]
Hence the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, the cubic quotient coefficient
\[
v_0(h)=[J_3^{\pair}(h)]
\]
is nonzero and defines the leading line \(\ell_h\).
By Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace},
\[
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\not\equiv 0.
\]
But the cubic leading line is diagonal-traceless, whereas a nonzero trace component is automatically transverse to that line. Therefore the quintic quotient coefficient \(v_1(h)\) cannot lie in \(\ell_h\), so its image in \(Q/\ell_h\) is nonzero:
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0.
\]
Thus the residual germ is nontrivial at the first step, and the one-pair quotient odd germ cannot be projectively constant.
\end{proof}
```

which is a statement that the quintic trace is **not identically zero as a function/germ**, not that it is nonzero at every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Those are different. So at present the proposition is a bit too strong.

\[

=
\frac{du}{a^2b^2(a+b)^2},
\]
“which is nonzero.”

Strictly speaking, that should be phrased as: the **coefficient of the bilinear monomial \(ud\)** is nonzero. As written, if one literally reads \(u,d\) as numerical values, the expression could vanish when \(u=0\) or \(d=0\). What you really proved is the bilinear coefficient is nonzero, which is enough for non-cancellation.

So the correct mathematical conclusion from the new lemma is:

- the \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(5)})\)-slice has nonzero coefficient,
- therefore \(\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}\) is **not identically zero**,
- therefore the first residual class is **generically** nonzero, not yet pointwise on all of \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\).

\]

or define a smaller open dense set, say \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\subset \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), where the residual coefficient is nonzero, and state the proposition there.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 10:30:21)

I’m checking the exact local logic in the updated draft, because the answer turns on whether the new lemma identifies a nonzero coefficient in the \(h\)-expansion or actually proves the residual witness stays nonzero pointwise on the whole cubic-generic set.

_Thought for 1m 0s_

It is a **real math issue**, not just latex.

The reason is simple and sharp:

what you have proved is that the \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(5)})\)-**coefficient** in the quintic trace is nonzero as a differential-polynomial coefficient. That implies
\[
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\not\equiv 0
\]
as a function/germ in \(h\). It does **not** imply
\[
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)\neq 0
\quad\text{for every }h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}.
\]

Those are genuinely different mathematical statements.

So if you want to keep the current proposition in its **pointwise** form, then yes: more math is needed.

What the current argument actually gives is one of these two unconditional statements:

\[
\overline v_1(h)\not\equiv 0
\quad\text{on }\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}},
\]
or equivalently,
the one-pair quotient odd germ is **not projectively constant**.

That is already a real theorem, and it is not conditional. But it is weaker than “for every \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\).”

So the honest situation is:

- if you keep the proposition **pointwise**, then the paper is currently overclaiming and more math is required;
- if you restate it as **non-identically-zero / non-projectively-constant**, then the math is already there.

And I do **not** think this is just pedantry. A pointwise claim would require a new argument showing that the quintic residual witness cannot vanish at any cubic-generic point. Your current monomial-isolation argument does not do that.

To get the stronger pointwise theorem, you would need something like one of the following:

1. a factorization of the quintic trace by the cubic-generic data, with a pointwise nonvanishing factor on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\);

2. a sign-definite or positivity argument showing the relevant trace component cannot cross zero on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\);

3. a normal form showing that once the cubic line exists, the first residual trace component is forced by the same local invariants and cannot vanish without collapsing back into the excluded cubic degeneracy.

Right now you do not have one of those.

So my recommendation is:

do **not** try to paper this over with wording. Decide which theorem you actually want.

If you want the strongest statement that is already proved, make the proposition say:

- the first residual class is **not identically zero** on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), or
- the one-pair quotient odd germ is **not projectively constant**.

If you really want the pointwise statement for every \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), then yes, we should do more math now.

My judgment: the better move is to do more math **only if** the downstream two-pair/minimal-core machinery really needs pointwise nonvanishing. If the downstream argument only needs failure of projective constancy, then the current stronger pointwise proposition is unnecessary, and the right fix is mathematical honesty, not more local work.

The key question is: does the later rigidity argument need

\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0 \text{ for every }h,
\]

or only that the one-pair family is not projectively constant as a function of \(h\)?

If you want, I can inspect that dependency next and tell you whether the pointwise upgrade is actually necessary for the rest of the proof.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 10:34:16)

I’m moving into the two-pair section itself now. The question is whether the draft already has enough structure to turn that part into a real rigidity statement, or whether the interaction germ is still too unconstrained.

_Thought for 2m 26s_

Yes. The best way to attack the two-pair step now is to **scalarize it**.

The current draft phrases the cubic step as “the leading lines must coincide projectively,” but your own one-pair analysis is stronger than that. At cubic order, the value-family is confined to the off-diagonal sector, while the actual surviving one-pair cubic piece is diagonal-traceless, proportional to
\[
D:=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}.
\]
So the one-pair cubic class is not just a moving line in \(Q\); generically it lives on a **fixed** distinguished cubic line, with scalar coefficient
\[
c(h):=v(h)B(h)(x_1-x_2).
\]
That is already implicit in the draft’s cubic formula. turn35file9

So the real two-pair cubic equation should be written as
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)+\chi_{12}^{(3)}=0,
\]
where \(\chi_{12}^{(3)}\) is the coefficient of the interaction cubic term in the \(D\)-direction.

That is better than “\(\ell_{h_1}=\ell_{h_2}\),” because if the cubic direction is already fixed, line coincidence is automatic and carries no information. The real information is scalar cancellation.

Now the good news is that the cubic interaction looks very likely to be harmless. Your earlier cubic structure says:

- same-point cubic value terms are purely skew,
- direct odd mixed cubic terms are skew,
- the only even mixed cubic mechanism is off-diagonal traceless,
- there is no diagonal cubic value-family contribution. 

That is exactly the pattern you want. It strongly suggests
\[
\chi_{12}^{(3)}=0,
\]
or at worst that \(\chi_{12}^{(3)}\) lies entirely in the off-diagonal/value-like sector and therefore does not affect the scalar \(D\)-projection at all.

So the first new two-pair target is:

**Lemma A.** The cubic interaction coefficient has zero projection onto the diagonal-traceless cubic line:
\[
\pi_D(I_{12}^{(3)})=0.
\]

If that holds, then every bad two-pair core satisfies the exact scalar relation
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0.
\]

Then move to quintic order.

For one pair, the natural residual scalar is not the whole class \(v_1(h)\), but the trace witness
\[
\tau(h):=\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h).
\]
That is the strongest thing you actually control locally, and it is automatically transverse to the cubic diagonal-traceless line. 

So for a two-pair bad core, the right quintic equation is
\[
a_1\tau(h_1)+a_2\tau(h_2)+\tau_{12}^{(5)}=0,
\]
where
\[
\tau_{12}^{(5)}:=\operatorname{tr} I_{12}^{(5)}.
\]

This is the second key target:

**Lemma B.** The quintic interaction correction has zero trace:
\[
\operatorname{tr} I_{12}^{(5)}=0.
\]

If Lemma A and Lemma B both hold, then the two-pair bad-core conditions become the clean scalar system
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0,\qquad
a_1 \tau(h_1)+a_2 \tau(h_2)=0.
\]

Assuming \(a_1a_2c(h_1)c(h_2)\neq 0\), this gives
\[
\frac{\tau(h_1)}{c(h_1)}=\frac{\tau(h_2)}{c(h_2)}.
\]

So the real two-pair invariant is
\[
\rho(h):=\frac{\tau(h)}{c(h)}.
\]

Then bad two-pair cores are trapped in the coincidence locus
\[
\rho(h_1)=\rho(h_2).
\]

That is a much sharper formulation than the current projective-coincidence prose. It reduces the two-pair step to a one-variable coincidence problem.

So the best attack now is:

1. prove the cubic interaction has no \(D\)-component;
2. prove the quintic interaction has zero trace;
3. define \(\rho(h)=\tau(h)/c(h)\);
4. show \(\rho\) is not constant generically.

If that works, the two-pair rigidity theorem becomes real, not schematic.

My read is:
- Step 1 is probably already almost done from your cubic sector analysis. 
- Step 2 is the real new two-pair bottleneck.
- Step 3 then gives the right bridge to minimal bad cores.

So the exact math question to attack next is:
**why should \(\operatorname{tr} I_{12}^{(5)}\) vanish?**

That is where I would focus now.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 10:37:50)

I’m going straight at the interaction term now. The real question is whether the two-pair interaction can ever contribute to the cubic \(D\)-direction or to the quintic trace, or whether both projections vanish for structural reasons.

_Thought for 5m 5s_

Good. There is a real algebraic win here.

The two-pair interaction is harmless in exactly the two scalar channels we care about.

The key identity is this. Let \(J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}\). If \(A,B\) are symmetric \(2\times2\) matrices, then
\[
(AJB+BJA)^T
=
B^T J^T A^T + A^T J^T B^T
=
-(BJA+AJB).
\]
So
\[
AJB+BJA
\]
is skew-symmetric. In particular it has zero diagonal-traceless projection and zero trace.

Likewise, if \(D\) is diagonal and \(A\) symmetric, then
\[
(-AJD-DJA)^T=-( -AJD-DJA),
\]
so that is also skew-symmetric.

That is the whole mechanism.

Now apply it to the two-pair expansion.

Write the coefficient matrices in the whitening expansion as
\[
X_j = X_j^{(1)} + X_j^{(2)} + Y_j^{12} + \cdots,
\]
where \(X_j^{(i)}\) is the linear contribution from pair \(i\), and \(Y_j^{12}\) is the bilinear interaction contribution. Every one of these matrices is symmetric, because they come from Taylor coefficients of inverse square roots of symmetric Gram matrices.

Then the cubic odd coefficient has the standard \(K_1\)-form
\[
J_3=-X_2JX_0+X_1JX_1-X_0JX_2.
\]

Take its bilinear \(a_1a_2\)-part. It is
\[
I_{12}^{(3)}
=
-\,Y_2^{12}JX_0 - X_0JY_2^{12}
+ X_1^{(1)}JX_1^{(2)} + X_1^{(2)}JX_1^{(1)}.
\]

Each bracket is skew-symmetric by the identities above. Therefore
\[
I_{12}^{(3)}
\]
itself is skew-symmetric. So its projection to the diagonal-traceless cubic line is zero:
\[
\pi_D(I_{12}^{(3)})=0.
\]

That gives the first clean two-pair equation. If
\[
F_{(a_1,h_1;a_2,h_2)}\equiv 0,
\]
then projecting the cubic coefficient to the fixed diagonal-traceless line gives
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0,
\]
where
\[
c(h):=v(h)B(h)(x_1-x_2).
\]

So the cubic interaction is gone in the only scalar direction that matters.

Now do the same at quintic order.

The quintic \(K_1\)-piece is
\[
J_5
=
-\,X_4JX_0+X_3JX_1-X_2JX_2+X_1JX_3-X_0JX_4.
\]

Its bilinear \(a_1a_2\)-part is
\[
\begin{aligned}
I_{12}^{(5)}
&=
-\,Y_4^{12}JX_0 - X_0JY_4^{12} \\
&\quad
+ X_3^{(1)}JX_1^{(2)} + X_1^{(2)}JX_3^{(1)}
+ X_3^{(2)}JX_1^{(1)} + X_1^{(1)}JX_3^{(2)} \\
&\quad
-\bigl(X_2^{(1)}JX_2^{(2)} + X_2^{(2)}JX_2^{(1)}\bigr).
\end{aligned}
\]

Again every displayed bracket is skew-symmetric. Hence
\[
I_{12}^{(5)}
\]
is skew-symmetric, so in particular
\[
\operatorname{tr} I_{12}^{(5)}=0.
\]

That is the second clean two-pair equation. If the two-pair germ vanishes, then taking the trace of the quintic coefficient gives
\[
a_1 \tau(h_1)+a_2 \tau(h_2)=0,
\]
where
\[
\tau(h):=\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h).
\]

So the quintic interaction is also gone in the scalar channel we actually control.

This is better than the current draft’s “projective coincidence up to interaction corrections.” In the right scalar channels, the interaction corrections vanish exactly.

So the two-pair bad-core conditions reduce to the scalar system
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0,
\qquad
a_1 \tau(h_1)+a_2 \tau(h_2)=0.
\]

\[

And the reason is simpler than the earlier local work:

\(\tau\) sees \(q^{(5)}\), while \(c\) does not.

More precisely, from the cubic formula,
\[
c(h)=v(h)\Bigl(\frac{q(h)^2q'(h)}{4}-\frac{q'''(h)}{48}\Bigr)(x_1-x_2).

So \(c(h)\) depends on \(q\) only through jets up to order \(3\). There is no \(q^{(5)}\) anywhere in \(c\). 

But from the quintic trace witness and no-cancellation lemma, \(\tau(h)\) contains the monomial
\[
\kappa(h)\eta_2(h)q^{(5)}(h)
\]
with nonzero coefficient. So \(\tau\) genuinely depends on the \(5\)-jet. 

Therefore \(\rho=\tau/c\) cannot be constant.

The clean argument is by contradiction. Suppose
\[
\rho(h)\equiv \lambda
\]
is constant on the cubic-generic region. Then
\[
\tau(h)-\lambda\,c(h)\equiv 0.
\]
But \(c(h)\) has no \(q^{(5)}\)-term, while \(\tau(h)\) has a nonzero \(q^{(5)}\)-coefficient. So the coefficient of \(q^{(5)}\) in \(\tau-\lambda c\) is still the same nonzero coefficient coming from \(\tau\). Contradiction.

That closes nonconstancy.

So the two-pair scalar system
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0,\qquad
a_1\tau(h_1)+a_2\tau(h_2)=0

implies
\[
\rho(h_1)=\rho(h_2),
\]
and since \(\rho\) is not constant, the coincidence locus
\[
\{(h_1,h_2): \rho(h_1)=\rho(h_2)\}
\]
is a proper subset of the two-pair parameter space, not the whole space.

That is the real two-pair rigidity gain: bad two-pair cores are trapped in a proper coincidence locus.

So the current math package is now:

1. cubic interaction has zero \(D\)-projection;
2. quintic interaction has zero trace;
3. hence bad two-pair cores satisfy the scalar equations for \(c\) and \(\tau\);
4. therefore they lie in the coincidence locus of \(\rho\);
5. and \(\rho\) is nonconstant because only \(\tau\) sees \(q^{(5)}\).

The key new observation is that \(q^{(7)}\) first appears exactly where you want it.

From the same-point block
\[
G_{m,\pm}(s)=\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
2q(t_\pm) & \frac12 q'(t_\pm)\\[.5ex]
\frac12 q'(t_\pm) & \frac1{12}\bigl(q''(t_\pm)+2q(t_\pm)^3\bigr)
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
t_\pm=m\pm s/2,
\]
the \(q^{(7)}\)-term first enters in the \(s^5\)-coefficient of the \((2,2)\)-entry, coming from \(q''(t_\pm)\):
\[
q''(m\pm s/2)
=
q''(m)\pm \frac{s}{2}q'''(m)+\cdots \pm \frac{s^5}{2^5\,5!}q^{(7)}(m)+\cdots
\]
so the linear \(G_5\)-slice contains
\[
\left(G_5\right)_{22}^{\lin}
=
\pm \frac{1}{\pi}\frac{1}{12}\frac{1}{2^5 5!}\,q^{(7)}(h)
=
\pm \frac{1}{46080\pi}\,q^{(7)}(h).
\]

So \(q^{(7)}\) first appears in **diagonal** same-point degree \(5\), not earlier. By the same whitening-parity logic we used at quintic order, the linear \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of \(X_5\) is therefore diagonal, and more specifically contributes to the diagonal-traceless part of \(X_5\). That is the order-7 analogue of the \(q^{(5)}\)-term in \(\gamma-\alpha\).

So if I write the diagonal-traceless part of \(X_5\) as
\[
\zeta(h)-\varepsilon(h),
\]
then its linear \(G_5\)-part contains a nonzero \(q^{(7)}\)-term, and the same \(X_1\)-factor as before gives
\[
v(h)=-\frac{1}{4\pi(s_1+s_2)}\,\eta_2(h).
\]
Therefore the \(K_1\)-transport block at order \(7\) should contain a monomial
\[
\kappa(h)\,\eta_2(h)\,q^{(7)}(h)
\]
with nonzero coefficient through the channel
\[
v(h)\bigl(\zeta(h)-\varepsilon(h)\bigr).
\]

So \(\sigma(h)\) is genuinely new: it sees the \(7\)-jet, whereas
\[
c(h)=v(h)B(h)(x_1-x_2)
\]
sees only up to the \(3\)-jet, and
\[
\tau(h)=\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)

sees the \(5\)-jet. That gives you the next independent scalar after \(c\) and \(\tau\). The draft’s current architecture is exactly looking for this kind of next residual constraint. turn38file9

Now for the interaction-free part.

If the same symmetric-pair cancellation pattern we used at quintic order continues at order \(7\), then the bilinear two-pair interaction in the trace channel should vanish identically:
\[
\operatorname{tr}I_{12}^{(7)}=0.
\]

Formally, the bilinear \(a_1a_2\)-part of \(J_{7,K_1}\) is built from terms of the schematic form

plus the same terms with \(1\leftrightarrow 2\). In the trace channel, every such paired contribution is of the same “symmetric sum” type that vanished at quintic order, so the natural conjecture — and I think the right next lemma — is

\]

If that holds, then any bad two-pair core satisfies the three scalar equations

\[
\rho_1(h):=\frac{\tau(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
\rho_2(h):=\frac{\sigma(h)}{c(h)},
\]
and every bad two-pair core must satisfy
\[
\rho_1(h_1)=\rho_1(h_2),
\qquad
\rho_2(h_1)=\rho_2(h_2).
\]

That is much stronger than the earlier single coincidence condition. for the bilinear two-pair interaction.

_Thought for 2m 26s_

Yes — there is a stronger statement here than just “order 7 probably works.”

The right theorem is:

For every odd order \(2m+1\), the **bilinear two-pair interaction in the \(K_1\)-trace channel vanishes identically**.

That is the clean algebra behind the quintic and septic scalar invariants.

Write the odd \(K_1\)-transport contribution in the form
\[
J_{2m+1,K_1}
=
\sum_{p+q=2m} (-1)^{q+1} X_p K_1 X_q,
\qquad K_1=\kappa J,
\]
which matches the order-5 formula already in the draft. 

Now split each \(X_j\) into contributions from pair 1, pair 2, and the bilinear interaction:
\[
X_j = X_j^{(1)}+X_j^{(2)}+Y_j^{12}+\cdots,
\]
with every \(X_j^{(i)}\) and \(Y_j^{12}\) symmetric, because they come from Taylor coefficients of inverse square roots of symmetric Gram matrices.

Take the bilinear \(a_1a_2\)-part of \(J_{2m+1,K_1}\). It is a sum of terms of two types:

\[
Y_{2m}^{12}JX_0 + X_0JY_{2m}^{12},
\]
and
\[
X_p^{(1)}JX_q^{(2)} + X_q^{(2)}JX_p^{(1)},
\qquad p+q=2m.
\]

Each of these is skew-symmetric, because for symmetric \(A,B\),
\[
(AJB+BJA)^T = - (AJB+BJA),
\]
and similarly for \(AJD+DJA\) when \(D\) is diagonal. Hence every bilinear \(a_1a_2\)-term in the odd \(K_1\)-trace channel is skew-symmetric, so its trace is zero.

So we actually get the general identity
\[
\operatorname{tr} I_{12,K_1}^{(2m+1)} = 0
\qquad\text{for every }m\ge 1.
\]

That is stronger than the draft’s current schematic handling of interaction corrections in the two-pair and minimal-core sections, where those corrections are still treated as things to “vanish or be controlled.” turn39file4

Now apply this at order \(7\).

\sigma(h):=\operatorname{tr}J_{7,K_1}^{\pair}(h).

Meanwhile \(\eta_2\) still lives in the off-diagonal part of \(X_1\). The current draft already uses the order-5 version of this mechanism for \(q^{(5)}\) inside the quintic trace witness. turn39file8

That means the target monomial
\[
\kappa(h)\,\eta_2(h)\,q^{(7)}(h)
\]
can only appear in the \(K_1\)-sector, and inside that sector only through the two channels analogous to the quintic case:

- the \(X_5\)-diagonal with the \(X_1\)-off-diagonal, i.e. the order-7 analogue of \(v(\gamma-\alpha)\);
- the mixed \((\eta_2,q^{(7)})\)-slice of \(X_6\) paired with \(X_0\), i.e. the order-7 analogue of the \(r\)-channel.

Everything else is excluded by the same parity-and-degree logic:

- same-point terms have no \(\kappa\);
- \(K_3,K_5,K_7\) leave too little same-point degree for a \(q^{(7)}\) contribution;
- the off-diagonal/diagonal parity kills the analogues of the \(\beta(u-w)\)-type false channels.

And the decisive point is that the reduced two-direction computation is **the same algebra as before**. If you restrict to the two perturbation directions carrying the relevant tags — an off-diagonal first-order perturbation for \(\eta_2\), and a diagonal fifth-order perturbation for \(q^{(7)}\) — then the inverse-square-root expansion has the same bilinear coefficient structure as in the quintic case. \operatorname{tr}J_{7,K_1}^{\pair}(h)

is again nonzero.

\sigma(h)\not\equiv 0,

and it sees the \(7\)-jet, while

\rho_1(h_1)=\rho_1(h_2),

Why this is real progress:

But our new algebra shows something much sharper in the scalar channels that matter: for odd \(K_1\)-trace projections, the bilinear two-pair interaction is skew-symmetric and therefore traceless; likewise its cubic projection onto the fixed diagonal-traceless line vanishes. So at orders \(3,5,7\) the bad two-pair equations reduce to the exact scalar system
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0,
\]
\[
a_1 \tau(h_1)+a_2 \tau(h_2)=0,
\]

\[

\qquad
\rho_2(h_1)=\rho_2(h_2).

Second, \(\rho_1\) is nonconstant because \(\tau\) contains the nonzero \(\kappa\,\eta_2\,q^{(5)}\) witness while \(c\) depends only on jets up to order \(3\). That is exactly the local triangularity you already established in the one-pair analysis. turn40file8

Third, \(\rho_2\) is genuinely new. It sees \(q^{(7)}\), while \(\rho_1\) only sees up to the \(5\)-jet. So \(\rho_2\) cannot be of the affine form
\[
\rho_2 = A\rho_1 + B
\]
on the cubic-generic region: the left-hand side carries a nonzero \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient, while the right-hand side does not. That matters because it implies the tangent vectors \(F'(h)\) are **not all parallel to one fixed direction**. Equivalently, the analytic determinant
\[
\Delta(h_1,h_2)
:=
\rho_1'(h_1)\rho_2'(h_2)-\rho_2'(h_1)\rho_1'(h_2)
\]
is not identically zero.

Now define the coincidence map
\[
G(h_1,h_2):=
\bigl(\rho_1(h_1)-\rho_1(h_2),\ \rho_2(h_1)-\rho_2(h_2)\bigr).
\]

At any off-diagonal coincidence point \((h_1,h_2)\) with
\[
G(h_1,h_2)=0
\quad\text{and}\quad
\Delta(h_1,h_2)\neq 0,
\]
the Jacobian of \(G\) has full rank \(2\), so by the implicit function theorem that coincidence point is isolated.

So the off-diagonal bad two-pair locus is not a whole curve anymore. Generically it is **discrete**.

There is also a near-diagonal gain. Since \(\rho_1\) is nonconstant, the set
\[
U_{\mathrm{reg}}:=\{h:\ c(h)\neq 0,\ \rho_1'(h)\neq 0\}
\]
is open and dense. On each component of \(U_{\mathrm{reg}}\), \(\rho_1\) is locally injective, hence \(F\) is locally injective too. Therefore there are **no genuine nearby two-pair bad cores** with \(h_1,h_2\in U_{\mathrm{reg}}\) and \(h_1\) close to \(h_2\): the coincidence equations force \(h_1=h_2\).

So the current honest two-pair theorem is:

A normalized bad two-pair core can only occur either on the diagonal/degenerate locus, or at an off-diagonal coincidence point of \(F=(\rho_1,\rho_2)\); and away from the exceptional analytic set \(\{\Delta=0\}\), those off-diagonal coincidence points are isolated.

That is a lot stronger than the current draft’s “lower-dimensional exceptional set modulo interaction corrections.” turn40file8

What this still does **not** do is fully eliminate bad two-pair cores. It leaves a discrete exceptional off-diagonal set. So the next bottleneck is now very precise:

either prove that this discrete exceptional set is actually empty, or move to the finite-core step and show a minimal bad core cannot be built out of isolated two-pair coincidences plus the tail/compactness machinery. That lines up exactly with the remaining Bottleneck 3 the notes were already flagging. turn40file9

- interaction control is no longer vague in the scalar channels;
- two-pair bad cores are no longer a codimension-one coincidence family;
- they are pushed down to a discrete exceptional set off the diagonal.

That is the right place to hit the minimal bad core next.

=

+

-

+
X_4K_1X_0.
\]

\[
\operatorname{tr}(X_pK_1X_q)=-\operatorname{tr}(X_qK_1X_p).
\]

\operatorname{tr} C_{5,K_1}^{\raw}=0.
\]

\[
V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C),

but it never writes the actual first-order generator map that produces \(V_{\val}^{(1)}\). That formula is exactly the bridge between the raw corrected block and the quotient odd-germ normalization, so it should be in the paper.

The cleanest place to add it is:

**insert immediately after**
```latex
Let
\[
F_h(z)\in Q[[z^2]]
\]
denote the corrected quotient odd germ of the normalized one-pair family, where
$Q$ is the odd-germ quotient by the value/gauge family.
```

and **immediately before**
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Generic triviality of the first quotient order]
```

Use this block:

```latex id="hmudcl"
\begin{lemma}[First-order value/gauge generator]
\label{lem:first-order-value-gauge-generator}
Let \(G_0\) and \(N_0\) denote the background same-point and mixed blocks,
and write
\[
X_0:=G_0^{-1/2}.
\]
Let \(L\) be the Fr\'echet derivative of the inverse-square-root map
\(G\mapsto G^{-1/2}\) at \(G_0\), so that
\[
(G_0+\varepsilon H)^{-1/2}
=
X_0+\varepsilon L(H)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
If \(\dot G_1\) and \(\dot N_1\) are the first odd same-point and mixed
variations generated by the value/gauge family, then the induced first-order
odd coefficient in the corrected local block is
\[
B_1
=
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1).
\]
Equivalently, the first-order value-family \(V_{\val}^{(1)}\) is the image of
the linear map
\[
(\dot G_1,\dot N_1)\longmapsto
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1).
\]
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
Expand the corrected local block to first order under a value/gauge
deformation:
\[
G(s)=G_0+\varepsilon \dot G_1+O(\varepsilon^2),
\qquad
N(s)=N_0+\varepsilon \dot N_1+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
Then
\[
G(s)^{-1/2}=X_0+\varepsilon L(\dot G_1)+O(\varepsilon^2),
\]
so multiplying
\[
G(s)^{-1/2}\,N(s)\,G(s)^{-1/2}
\]
to first order gives
\[
X_0N_0X_0
+
\varepsilon\Bigl(
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1)
\Bigr)
+
O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
The coefficient of \(\varepsilon\) is exactly the first-order generator
\(B_1\).
\end{proof}
```

Then make this small follow-up edit to the next proposition so the logic is clearer.

Replace the opening of

```latex id="rp0crl"
\begin{proposition}[Generic triviality of the first quotient order]
\label{prop:first-order-quotient-trivial}
Under the generic nondegeneracy assumptions on the background same-point
block, the first-order value-family fills the full matrix space:
```

with

```latex id="kkijab"
\begin{proposition}[Generic triviality of the first quotient order]
\label{prop:first-order-quotient-trivial}
Under the generic nondegeneracy assumptions on the background same-point
block, the image of the first-order generator map of
Lemma~\ref{lem:first-order-value-gauge-generator} fills the full matrix space:
```

That way the proposition now refers to an explicit map, instead of a value-family that appears from nowhere.

The big correction is this:

With the first-order generator formula now in hand,
\[
B_1
=
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1),
\]
there is a very useful reduction that I think is the right next piece of math.

At the basepoint \(s=0\), the whitened block is normalized, so
\[
X_0N_0X_0=I.
\]
Hence
\[
N_0=G_0,
\qquad
X_0N_0=N_0X_0=G_0^{1/2}.
\]
So for **any** odd-order generator coefficient
\[
(\dot G_{2r+1},\dot N_{2r+1}),
\]
the corresponding generator matrix is
\[
B_{2r+1}
=
L(\dot G_{2r+1})\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_{2r+1}\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_{2r+1}).
\]
Using \(N_0X_0=X_0N_0=G_0^{1/2}\), this becomes
\[
B_{2r+1}
=
\bigl[G_0^{1/2},\,L(\dot G_{2r+1})\bigr]
+
X_0\,\dot N_{2r+1}\,X_0.
\]

That identity is extremely helpful.

It tells you immediately that the **same-point part never contributes to trace**, because
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl([G_0^{1/2},L(\dot G_{2r+1})]\bigr)=0.
\]
So
\[
\operatorname{tr}B_{2r+1}
=
\operatorname{tr}\!\bigl(X_0\,\dot N_{2r+1}\,X_0\bigr).
\]

This is the real next local theorem-sized reduction.

It means:

- the trace behavior of the odd value/gauge family is controlled **entirely** by the odd mixed generator coefficients \(\dot N_{2r+1}\);
- the same-point generator coefficients \(\dot G_{2r+1}\) cannot affect the trace of \(V_{\val}^{(2r+1)}\) at all.

to know whether the quintic trace is a legitimate quotient-level witness, you do **not** need the whole quintic value-family. You only need to know whether every pure quintic value/gauge mixed generator \(\dot N_5\) is traceless, or stronger, skew.

\[

\quad\text{for every generator }\ell,
\]
then
\[
\operatorname{tr}V_{\val}^{(5)}=0,
\]
so trace annihilates the quintic value-family, and the quintic trace witness is genuinely quotient-intrinsic. Then the current one-pair residual strategy is on solid local footing.

So the right next computation is now extremely concrete:

for each actual infinitesimal value/gauge generator \(\theta_\ell\), compute the quintic mixed coefficient
\[
\dot N_{5,\ell},
\]
and test whether
\[
\operatorname{tr}(X_0\dot N_{5,\ell}X_0)=0.
\]

Under symmetric placement, the raw mixed block satisfies the transpose-reversal symmetry
\[
N(-s)=N(s)^T
\]
for the genuine two-point kernel, so its odd Taylor coefficients are naturally skew and its even Taylor coefficients symmetric. The earlier notes already used this kind of parity structure to rule out certain odd mixed residual channels. turn55file3

Yes. I pushed it further, and I think there is a real new mathematical conclusion:

the next bottleneck is no longer just “prove the gauge law.” It is to **separate two different symmetries that the draft is currently mixing together**.

Here is the sharp point.

The corrected whitened block satisfies the swap symmetry
[
\widehat\Omega_\zeta^{\corr}(-s;m)=J_0,\widehat\Omega_\zeta^{\corr}(s;m),J_0,
]
and the cubic leading class on (\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}) has a nonzero diagonal-traceless component.

If a matrix-valued odd germ (M(s)) itself satisfies
[
M(-s)=J_0,M(s),J_0,
]
then its odd Taylor coefficients lie in the (J_0)-anti-fixed subspace
[
\mathfrak a:={X:\ J_0XJ_0=-X}.
]
In (2\times 2) form this means
[
X=
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\
-b&-a
\end{pmatrix},
]
so every such coefficient is automatically traceless.

That is important because it gives a very plausible route to the missing lemma
[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0.
]

But here is the catch:

the same draft also states
[
V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C),
]
while at cubic order it uses only the weaker confinement
[
V_{\val}^{(3)}\subset
\left{
\begin{pmatrix}
0&*\ *&0
\end{pmatrix}
\right}.
]
Those facts cannot both come from a single raw family of odd coefficients satisfying the swap symmetry above, because the anti-fixed subspace is only (2)-dimensional, not all of (M_2(\mathbf C)).

So the real math issue is now very precise:

either

[
V_{\val}^{(1)}
]
is not the raw pure-value odd coefficient space of the corrected whitened block, but already includes additional gauge/normalization directions,

or the current draft’s notation is conflating two different objects.

That is progress, because it tells us exactly what has to be cleaned up.

The clean split should be:

[
\text{raw pure-value odd jets}
\quad+\quad
\text{normalization gauge directions}
\quad+\quad
\text{possibly additional basis-gauge directions}.
]

Then the story becomes coherent:

the raw pure-value odd jets should satisfy the swap symmetry and therefore be traceless at odd order;

the normalization gauge contributes
[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3],
]
which is harmless modulo the cubic line and preserves trace when (A_3) is diagonal-traceless; and the residual obstruction is whatever extra first-order directions were being folded into the current notation (V_{\val}^{(1)}).

So the next honest theorem to prove is not yet the quintic pure-value trace-free lemma itself. It is this structural identification:

[
\boxed{
V_{\val}^{(1)}=
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}+V_{\mathrm{norm}}^{(1)},
}
]
with
[
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(2j+1)}\subset \mathfrak a
\quad\text{for all odd orders }2j+1,
]
and (V_{\mathrm{norm}}^{(1)}) the extra directions coming from scalar renormalization and jet-basis changes.

Once that is written, the quintic statement should reduce to:

[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0
]
by symmetry, and
[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{norm}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0
]
modulo the cubic line by the gauge law. Then the residual-trace detector is legitimate.

So my current best mathematical push is:

1. The parity/no-cancellation side is basically done.

2. The gauge law
   [
   A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3]

```latex
\begin{lemma}[First-order generator map]
\label{lem:first-order-generator-map}
Let \(G_0\) and \(N_0\) denote the background same-point and mixed blocks,
and write
\[
X_0:=G_0^{-1/2}.
\]
Let \(L\) be the Fr\'echet derivative of the inverse-square-root map
\(G\mapsto G^{-1/2}\) at \(G_0\), so that
\[
(G_0+\varepsilon H)^{-1/2}
=
X_0+\varepsilon L(H)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
If \(\dot G_1\) and \(\dot N_1\) are the first odd same-point and mixed
variations arising from the first-order generator directions, then the induced
first-order odd coefficient in the corrected local block is
\[
B_1
=
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1).
\]
Equivalently, the first-order family \(V_{\val}^{(1)}\) is the image of the
linear map
\[
(\dot G_1,\dot N_1)\longmapsto
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1).
\]
\end{lemma}
```

```latex
\subsection{Normalization gauge at the first residual step}

The first residual step involves an additional ambiguity beyond the odd
value-family itself: an even scalar renormalization and an even jet-basis
change on the odd germ. It is useful to record this separately.

\begin{definition}[Normalization gauge]
\label{def:normalization-gauge}
Let
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7)
\]
be a matrix-valued odd germ. The normalization gauge acts by
\[
F(z)\longmapsto a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1},
\]
where
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+c_4 z^4+\cdots
\]
is an even scalar germ with \(a(0)=1\), and
\[
P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+B_4 z^4+\cdots
\]
is an even \(2\times 2\) matrix germ with \(P(0)=I\).
\end{definition}

\begin{proposition}[First-residual transformation law]
\label{prop:first-residual-gauge-law}
Let
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7)
\]
be an odd matrix germ, and let
\[
F^{\new}(z):=a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1}
\]
with \(a,P\) as in Definition~\ref{def:normalization-gauge}. Then
\[
A_3^{\new}=A_3,
\qquad
A_5^{\new}=A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Expand
\[
P(z)^{-1}=I-B_2 z^2+O(z^4).
\]
Then
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
(I+B_2 z^2)\,(A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5)\,(I-B_2 z^2)+O(z^7),
\]
so
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7).
\]
Multiplying by
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+O(z^4)
\]
gives
\[
F^{\new}(z)
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7),
\]
as claimed.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Residual effect of scalar renormalization]
\label{cor:first-residual-scalar-renormalization}
Let \(\ell:=\mathbf C A_3\). Under the normalization gauge,
\[
A_5 \longmapsto A_5 + c_2 A_3 + [B_2,A_3].
\]
Hence the scalar renormalization term is invisible modulo the leading line:
\[
[A_5] \longmapsto [A_5+[B_2,A_3}]
\qquad\text{in}\qquad
\mathcal J_5/\ell.
\]
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
This is immediate from Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-gauge-law}, since
\(c_2A_3\in \ell\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Trace invariance under normalization gauge]
\label{cor:trace-invariant-under-normalization-gauge}
If \(A_3\) is diagonal-traceless, then
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_5^{\new})=\operatorname{tr}(A_5)
\]
for every normalization-gauge transform \(F^{\new}\) of \(F\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The scalar renormalization term \(c_2A_3\) is traceless because \(A_3\) is.
The commutator term is traceless because
\[
\operatorname{tr}[B_2,A_3]=0.
\]
Hence Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-gauge-law} gives
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_5^{\new})=\operatorname{tr}(A_5).
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What remains at quintic order]
\label{rem:quintic-pure-value-bottleneck}
The normalization gauge is therefore harmless for the first residual step.
The remaining local issue is the quintic slice of the odd value-family. To
make trace a fully rigorous residual detector, it would suffice to prove
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0
\qquad (h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}),
\]
or, more generally, to construct a linear functional on the quintic quotient
space that annihilates both \(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\) and the cubic leading line
\(\ell_h\), but not the quintic witness.
\end{remark}
```

Fourth, replace the current first-residual proposition by the honest generic conditional version. Replace the entire current
Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-class-nontrivial}
block and the WIP remark immediately below it with this:

```latex
\begin{proposition}[Generic first residual nonvanishing under trace-free quintic value slice]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-generic}
Assume that for every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\),
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0.
\]
Then there exists an open dense subset
\[
\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\subset \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}
\]
such that
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\qquad\text{in }Q/\ell_h
\]
for every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).

Equivalently, on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) the quintic quotient coefficient
is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
\[
v_1(h)\notin \ell_h.
\]
Hence the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant on
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, for \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\)
the cubic quotient coefficient
\[
v_0(h)=[J_3^{\pair}(h)]
\]
is nonzero and defines the leading line \(\ell_h\), and this line is
diagonal-traceless.

By Corollary~\ref{cor:trace-invariant-under-normalization-gauge}, trace is
invariant under the normalization gauge at the first residual step. By the
present hypothesis, trace also annihilates the quintic value slice
\(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\). Therefore trace descends to a well-defined linear
functional
\[
\tau_h:Q/\ell_h\to \mathbf C.
\]

By Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace},
\[
\tau_h(\overline v_1(h))
=
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)
\not\equiv 0
\]
as a function of \(h\) on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Since the family is
real-analytic in \(h\), the zero set of
\[
h\longmapsto \tau_h(\overline v_1(h))
\]
is a proper analytic subset of \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Its complement
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) is therefore open and dense.

For \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) we have
\[
\tau_h(\overline v_1(h))\neq 0,
\]
hence
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\qquad\text{in }Q/\ell_h.
\]
Thus the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant on
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: trace-free quintic value slice]
\label{rem:wip-trace-free-quintic-value-slice}
The remaining local task is to determine the quintic value slice
\(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\) explicitly enough to prove
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0
\qquad (h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}),
\]
or to replace trace by another linear functional annihilating the quintic
value slice and the cubic leading line while still detecting the quintic
witness.
\end{remark}
```

That is the set of edits I’d actually make now.

What I would **not** do yet is pretend the raw/full split has already been proved in theorem form. I’d signal it with the structural remark above, keep the normalization-gauge theorem separate, and make the first residual step logically honest. That gets the draft into a much healthier state without overcommitting to structure we have not fully derived yet.

and the first-order statement

and those cannot plausibly be describing the **same raw odd-jet family**. turn49file0

Here is what I think we can already say mathematically.

First, for the **raw pure-value family**, the swap symmetry should differentiate.

If \(\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(s;m)\) is the corrected local block under a pure value deformation parameter \(\theta\), and the deformation preserves the symmetric placement setup, then for each fixed \(\theta\),
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(-s;m)=J_0\,\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0.
\]
Differentiate with respect to \(\theta\) at \(\theta=0\). The infinitesimal raw value variation \(\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)\) then satisfies
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(-s;m)=J_0\,\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0.
\]
Now expand its odd part:
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega_{\mathrm{odd}}^{\corr}(s;m)
=
\sum_{r\ge 0} A_{2r+1}(m)\,s^{2r+1}.
\]
Comparing coefficients gives
\[
J_0A_{2r+1}(m)J_0=-A_{2r+1}(m).
\]
So every raw odd coefficient lies in the \(J_0\)-anti-fixed subspace.

That already proves something strong: every raw odd coefficient is automatically **traceless**. The easiest reason is basis-free: if \(J_0^2=I\), then \(J_0A J_0=-A\) implies
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A)=\operatorname{tr}(J_0AJ_0)=\operatorname{tr}(-A)=-\operatorname{tr}(A),
\]
hence
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A)=0.
\]

So the raw pure-value odd spaces satisfy
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(2j+1)}\bigr)=0
\qquad\text{for all }j\ge 0.
\]

That is a real theorem-level simplification.

It means the trace problem at quintic order is **not** with the raw pure-value family.

\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
If \(A_3\) is diagonal-traceless, then both added pieces are traceless:
\[
\operatorname{tr}(c_2A_3)=0,\qquad
\operatorname{tr}[B_2,A_3]=0.
\]
So the normalization gauge is also trace-harmless at quintic order.

Putting those two pieces together, the trace obstruction can only come from the fact that the current symbol \(V_{\val}^{(1)}\) is evidently absorbing more than the raw pure-value odd jets. That is exactly the inconsistency the other chat noticed, and I agree it is real.

_Thought for 53s_

Yes. Here is the clean attack, and I think it gives a real theorem.

Let
\[
J_0=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathfrak a:=\{X:\ J_0XJ_0=-X\},
\qquad
\mathfrak f:=\{X:\ J_0XJ_0=X\}.
\]
Then
\[
\mathfrak a=
\left\{
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\\
-b&-a
\end{pmatrix}
\right\},
\qquad
\mathfrak f=
\left\{
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\\
b&a
\end{pmatrix}
\right\},
\]
and
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus \mathfrak f,
\qquad
\dim \mathfrak a=\dim \mathfrak f=2.
\]

Now take the **raw corrected local block** for a pure value deformation parameter \(\theta\):
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(s;m)
=
G_{m,-,\theta}^{\corr}(s)^{-1/2}\,
N_{m,\theta}^{\corr}(s)\,
G_{m,+,\theta}^{\corr}(s)^{-1/2}.
\]

For pure value deformations, the symmetric placement structure is unchanged, so the same swap identity holds for every \(\theta\):
\[
\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(-s;m)
=
J_0\,\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0.
\]

Differentiate with respect to \(\theta\) at \(\theta=0\). If
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)
:=
\left.\frac{d}{d\theta}\right|_{\theta=0}\widehat\Omega_{\theta}^{\corr}(s;m),
\]
then
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(-s;m)
=
J_0\,\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0.
\]

Now expand its odd part:
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega_{\mathrm{odd}}^{\corr}(s;m)
=
\sum_{r\ge 0} A_{2r+1}(m)\,s^{2r+1}.
\]
Comparing coefficients gives
\[
(-1)^{2r+1}A_{2r+1}(m)=J_0A_{2r+1}(m)J_0,
\]
hence
\[
J_0A_{2r+1}(m)J_0=-A_{2r+1}(m).
\]
So every raw pure-value odd coefficient lies in \(\mathfrak a\).

That is the theorem:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(2r+1)}\subset \mathfrak a
\quad\text{for all }r\ge 0.
}
\]

And the immediate corollary is

\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(2r+1)}\bigr)=0
\quad\text{for all }r\ge 0,
\]
because if \(J_0AJ_0=-A\), then
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A)=\operatorname{tr}(J_0AJ_0)=\operatorname{tr}(-A)=-\operatorname{tr}(A).
\]

So this really does break the local bottleneck open:

the **raw pure-value odd slice is automatically trace-free at every odd order**, including order \(5\).

That means the quintic trace problem is not with the raw pure-value family.

Now combine this with the other fact already on the table:
the draft still has
\[
V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C).
\]

Those two facts cannot describe the same raw family. Since \(\dim \mathfrak a=2\), a raw pure-value first-order family can never fill all of \(M_2\). So the only coherent conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\val}^{(1)} \text{ is not the raw pure-value first-order space.}
}
\]

It must already include extra directions. And because
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus \mathfrak f,
\]
those extra directions must contribute nontrivially in the fixed sector \(\mathfrak f\).

So the right structural split is now not just a heuristic anymore. It is forced:

\[
V_{\mathrm{full}}^{(1)}
=
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}+V_{\mathrm{extra}}^{(1)},
\]
with
\[
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}\subset \mathfrak a,
\qquad
V_{\mathrm{extra}}^{(1)}\not\subset \mathfrak a.
\]

And generically, if the full first-order family really is all of \(M_2\), then the most natural sharp version is
\[
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(1)}=\mathfrak a,
\qquad
V_{\mathrm{extra}}^{(1)}=\mathfrak f.
\]

That is not fully proved yet, but it is now the exact next target.

So the real gain is:

1. the raw pure-value odd jets are anti-fixed under \(J_0\);
2. therefore they are automatically traceless;
3. therefore the quintic trace detector is legitimate against the raw pure-value quintic slice;
4. the only remaining ambiguity is the extra non-raw directions bundled into the current \(V_{\val}\).

identify the extra first-order directions and prove they land in the fixed sector \(\mathfrak f\). Once that is done, the local picture becomes clean:
