_Thought for 1m 19s_

I pushed the structural split one step further, and there is a real correction plus a real gain.

The correction first, because it matters:

I earlier rewrote the first-order generator as if the same-point part were a **commutator** with \(G_0^{1/2}\). That was wrong. Since
\[
N_0=G_0,\qquad X_0=G_0^{-1/2},
\]
the first-order generator is
\[
B_1
=
L(\dot G_1)\,G_0^{1/2}
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
G_0^{1/2}L(\dot G_1),
\]
so the same-point contribution is an **anticommutator**
\[
\{\,G_0^{1/2},\,L(\dot G_1)\,\},
\]
not a commutator. So there is no automatic trace cancellation from the same-point part alone. That earlier shortcut was wrong.

Now the gain.

Even without knowing the full source of the extra directions, we can prove a clean structural decomposition at the **target space** level using the swap involution.

Let
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak a}(A):=\frac12\bigl(A-J_0AJ_0\bigr),
\qquad
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A):=\frac12\bigl(A+J_0AJ_0\bigr),
\]
so that
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus \mathfrak f,
\]
with \(\mathfrak a\) the \(J_0\)-anti-fixed subspace and \(\mathfrak f\) the fixed subspace.

For the **raw pure-value** odd coefficients, the swap symmetry gives
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(2r+1)}\subset \mathfrak a
\qquad(r\ge 0).
\]
That part is solid: differentiating the raw swap identity with respect to a pure value parameter forces every odd raw coefficient into the anti-fixed sector. Hence
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\rm raw}^{(2r+1)}\bigr)=0
\quad\text{for all }r.
\]

Now use the full first-order theorem from the draft:
\[
V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C).
\]
Whatever the source of the extra directions is, this immediately forces a complementary fixed-sector contribution, because \(\mathfrak a\) is only \(2\)-dimensional. In particular,
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(1)}\bigr)=\mathfrak f.
\]

So without yet identifying the origin of those directions, we already get a rigorous first-order split at the image level:
\[
V_{\val}^{(1)}
=
\pi_{\mathfrak a}(V_{\val}^{(1)})+\pi_{\mathfrak f}(V_{\val}^{(1)}),
\]
with
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(1)}\subset \pi_{\mathfrak a}(V_{\val}^{(1)}),
\qquad
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(V_{\val}^{(1)})=\mathfrak f.
\]

That is weaker than proving
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(1)}=\mathfrak a,
\]
but it is already enough to sharpen the local picture:

- the raw pure-value part lives in the traceless anti-fixed sector;
- the extra first-order directions needed to fill all of \(M_2\) necessarily live, at least after projection, in the fixed sector.

So the contradiction has been reduced from “the notation is inconsistent” to a very precise statement:

> the current \(V_{\val}^{(1)}\) is a bundled first-order family whose anti-fixed part contains the raw pure-value directions and whose fixed part accounts for the extra normalization/basis directions.

That is real structure.

Now, what does this buy us for the quintic residual step?

It means the raw quintic value slice is already harmless for trace:
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]

So the only remaining local obstruction to using trace as a quintic detector is the **quintic shadow of the extra first-order fixed-sector directions**. That is much narrower than “understand all of \(V_{\val}^{(5)}\).”

In other words, the real remaining problem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{How do the fixed-sector first-order directions propagate to order }5?
}
\]

And here the normalization-gauge law becomes the right target again. If those extra first-order directions act on the odd germ only through even scalar renormalization and even basis changes, then at the first residual step they contribute exactly
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3],
\]
which is trace-harmless when \(A_3\) is diagonal-traceless. So the whole quintic trace argument would be vindicated.

So the attack has become very focused:

1. raw pure-value odd jets are anti-fixed and traceless;
2. extra first-order directions are necessarily fixed-sector;
3. the only remaining question is whether their order-5 effect is exactly the normalization-gauge action.

That is the next exact theorem I would try to prove.

So we have made real progress:
the quintic trace bottleneck is no longer “compute all of \(V_{\val}^{(5)}\).” It is “show that the fixed-sector complement acts by the normalization-gauge law at the first residual step.”

_Thought for 58s_

Here is the next honest push.

I think the math has now reduced to a very small representation-theoretic problem.

Write the swap involution as
\[
\iota(A):=J_0AJ_0,
\qquad
J_0=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\]
and decompose
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus \mathfrak f
\]
into the \((-1)\)- and \((+1)\)-eigenspaces of \(\iota\):
\[
\mathfrak a=\{A:\iota(A)=-A\}
=
\span\!\left\{
D:=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},
\ 
J:=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}
\right\},
\]
\[
\mathfrak f=\{A:\iota(A)=A\}
=
\span\!\left\{
I:=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},
\ 
S:=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}
\right\}.
\]

That is the right basis.

The raw pure-value odd jets live in \(\mathfrak a\). The extra first-order directions needed to make
\[
V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C)
\]
must therefore contribute in \(\mathfrak f\). So the remaining local problem is:

> how does a fixed-sector first-order ambiguity propagate to the quintic residual coefficient?

Now the key algebra is tiny.

Assume the cubic leading class has been normalized to lie on the diagonal-traceless line:
\[
A_3=cD.
\]
This is exactly the cubic situation on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\).

Take any even normalization gauge
\[
F(z)\mapsto a(z)P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1},
\qquad
a(z)=1+c_2z^2+\cdots,
\qquad
P(z)=I+B_2z^2+\cdots.
\]
At the first residual step,
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]

Now if the extra directions are fixed-sector, then at the relevant order we may take
\[
B_2\in \mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.
\]
Write
\[
B_2=\alpha I+\beta S.
\]
Then
\[
[B_2,A_3]
=
[\alpha I+\beta S,\ cD]
=
\beta c[S,D].
\]
But
\[
[S,D]=2J.
\]
So
\[
[B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J.
\]

This is a very strong simplification:

for a cubic leading coefficient \(A_3\) on the \(D\)-line, every fixed-sector basis gauge affects the quintic coefficient only by
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+\underbrace{c_2A_3}_{\in \mathbf C D}
+\underbrace{\beta' J}_{\in \mathbf C J}.
\]

So the entire fixed-sector normalization shadow at order \(5\) lies in the \(2\)-plane
\[
\span\{D,J\}=\mathfrak a.
\]
And more specifically:

- the scalar renormalization contributes only along the cubic line \(\mathbf C D\);
- the non-scalar fixed-sector basis change contributes only in the \(J\)-direction.

That already tells you a lot.

First, trace kills both automatically:
\[
\operatorname{tr}(D)=\operatorname{tr}(J)=0.
\]
So **if** the extra first-order directions propagate through the residual step exactly as fixed-sector normalization gauge, then they are completely harmless for the quintic trace detector.

Second, modulo the cubic line, the only possible residual effect is the \(J\)-direction. So even without using trace, the entire normalization ambiguity at the first residual step is at most one-dimensional modulo \(\ell_h\).

That is a much stronger structural statement than we had before.

So the next theorem to prove is now extremely precise:

> The fixed-sector complement of \(V_{\val}^{(1)}\) acts on the first residual coefficient through the normalization-gauge law with \(B_2\in\mathfrak f\).

If that is true, then the quintic trace detector is rigorously valid, because:

1. raw pure-value quintic jets are already traceless by anti-fixed symmetry;
2. fixed-sector normalization contributions are also traceless, since they lie in \(\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J\);
3. therefore trace annihilates the entire quintic ambiguity.

That is the real shape of the remaining local problem.

So the attack has reduced to this one exact question:

Can we identify the extra first-order directions with fixed-sector even basis changes?

And the good news is that even before proving that, we now know exactly what such a theorem would buy: it would close the local quintic trace issue completely.

So my strongest next step is:

- isolate the fixed-sector complement \(\pi_{\mathfrak f}(V_{\val}^{(1)})\),
- choose a basis \(\{I,S\}\) for it,
- and compute the induced order-5 shadow of those two directions on the odd germ.

If the shadows come out as \(D\) and \(J\), the local residual-trace story is done.

_Thought for 1m 59s_

Here is the next real theorem chain. I think this is the cleanest local attack now.

Set
\[
J_0=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
\mathfrak a:=\{A:\ J_0AJ_0=-A\},
\qquad
\mathfrak f:=\{A:\ J_0AJ_0=A\}.
\]
Then
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\!\left\{
D:=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},
\ 
J:=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}
\right\},
\qquad
\mathfrak f=\span\!\left\{
I:=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},
\ 
S:=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}
\right\}.
\]

The right local statement is not just “raw pure-value odd jets are traceless.” It is stronger:

\[
\boxed{
\text{all quintic ambiguity lives in } \mathfrak a.
}
\]

If that is true, then trace is automatically a valid residual detector, because
\[
\operatorname{tr}(\mathfrak a)=0.
\]

Here is the proof structure.

First, raw pure-value odd coefficients.

If the raw corrected local block under a pure value deformation satisfies the swap symmetry
\[
\widehat\Omega_\theta^{\corr}(-s;m)=J_0\,\widehat\Omega_\theta^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0,
\]
then differentiating in the value parameter gives for the infinitesimal raw variation
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(-s;m)=J_0\,\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0.
\]
So every odd raw coefficient \(A_{2r+1}\) satisfies
\[
J_0A_{2r+1}J_0=-A_{2r+1},
\]
hence
\[
A_{2r+1}\in \mathfrak a.
\]

That already gives
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(2r+1)}\subset \mathfrak a
\quad\text{for all }r.
\]

Now the new point is the normalization gauge.

Suppose
\[
F(-z)=J_0F(z)J_0
\]
and we apply a gauge
\[
F(z)\mapsto F^{\new}(z):=a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1}.
\]
Ask when this preserves the same swap symmetry:
\[
F^{\new}(-z)=J_0F^{\new}(z)J_0.
\]

For that to hold for every such \(F\), it is enough and essentially necessary that
\[
a(-z)=a(z),
\qquad
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]

Now expand
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+\cdots,
\qquad
P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+\cdots.
\]
Then the symmetry condition on \(P\) gives
\[
B_2=J_0B_2J_0,
\]
so
\[
B_2\in \mathfrak f.
\]

This is the key new reduction: a symmetry-preserving first residual basis gauge has its order-2 coefficient in the fixed sector, not arbitrarily in \(M_2\).

Now assume the cubic leading term is
\[
A_3=cD,
\]
which is exactly the cubic-leading situation on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). The first residual gauge law says
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]

Since \(A_3\in \mathbf C D\subset \mathfrak a\) and \(B_2\in \mathfrak f\), write
\[
B_2=\alpha I+\beta S.
\]
Then
\[
[B_2,A_3]=\beta c[S,D].
\]
But
\[
[S,D]=2J,
\]
so
\[
[B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J\subset \mathfrak a.
\]
Also
\[
c_2A_3\in \mathbf C D\subset \mathfrak a.
\]

Therefore every normalization-gauge shadow at quintic order lies in \(\mathfrak a\).

So we now get the full local conclusion:

- raw pure-value quintic ambiguity lies in \(\mathfrak a\);
- symmetry-preserving normalization-gauge quintic ambiguity also lies in \(\mathfrak a\).

Hence the **entire quintic ambiguity** at the first residual step lies in \(\mathfrak a\).

That is the real theorem:
\[
\boxed{
V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}+V_{\rm norm}^{(5)}\subset \mathfrak a.
}
\]

And then the payoff is immediate:
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}+V_{\rm norm}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]

So trace is a legitimate quotient functional on the quintic residual coefficient, provided the split between raw pure-value directions and symmetry-preserving normalization-gauge directions is the correct one.

This is better than the earlier vague target
\[
\operatorname{tr}(V_{\val}^{(5)})=0,
\]
because it explains **why** it should be true: not by brute-force enumeration, but because all quintic ambiguity sits in the anti-fixed subspace.

The remaining issue is now extremely specific:

the current draft’s symbol \(V_{\val}\) still bundles more than one thing. What we have really shown is that, after the correct structural split,
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}+V_{\rm norm}^{(5)}\subset \mathfrak a.
\]
So the next exact task is to prove that the extra first-order directions needed for
\[
V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C)
\]
are precisely these symmetry-preserving normalization directions, not something larger.

But mathematically, this is already a strong attack result:
once that identification is made, the quintic trace detector is rigorously justified.

\[

with

a(z)=1+c_2z^2+c_4z^4+\cdots,\qquad

F^{\new}(-z)=a(-z)\,P(-z)\,F(-z)\,P(-z)^{-1}
=a(-z)\,P(-z)\,J_0F(z)J_0\,P(-z)^{-1}.
\]
On the other hand,

J_0F^{\new}(z)J_0
=
a(z)\,(J_0P(z)J_0)\,J_0F(z)J_0\,(J_0P(z)^{-1}J_0).

So for these to agree for all \(F\), it is enough, and in this setting effectively the right condition, that
\[
a(-z)=a(z),\qquad P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]
Since \(a\) is already even, the real condition is
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.

\[
I+B_2z^2+\cdots = J_0(I+B_2z^2+\cdots)J_0,
\]
hence
\[
B_2=J_0B_2J_0.

\[
A_3=cD,\qquad D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix}.

\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
Write
\[
B_2=\alpha I+\beta S.
\]
Then
\[
[B_2,A_3]=\beta c[S,D].
\]
Since
\[
[S,D]=2J,\qquad J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\]
we get
\[
[B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J.
\]
Also
\[
c_2A_3\in \mathbf C D.
\]
Therefore the entire quintic normalization shadow lies in
\[
\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J=\mathfrak a.
\]

So if the extra directions really are symmetry-preserving normalization directions, then at quintic order they are automatically traceless.

Now combine that with the raw pure-value result:
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}\subset \mathfrak a.
\]
So both pieces land in \(\mathfrak a\), hence
\[
V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}+V_{\rm norm}^{(5)}\subset \mathfrak a,
\]
and therefore
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\rm raw}^{(5)}+V_{\rm norm}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.

That is exactly what the quintic trace detector needs.

A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3],

That question can be reduced to a 2-dimensional check.

Let
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak a}(A)=\frac12(A-J_0AJ_0),
\qquad
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A)=\frac12(A+J_0AJ_0).
\]
Since \(\dim \mathfrak a=\dim \mathfrak f=2\), the first-order fullness theorem forces
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(1)}\bigr)=\mathfrak f.

- the \(S\)-direction produces the commutator shadow proportional to \(J\),

\span\{D,J\}=\mathfrak a,
\]
hence is traceless, and the trace detector is rigorously valid.

So the most aggressive next mathematical step is to compute those two shadows directly.

You do **not** need the full machinery of the corrected block for this check. At the residual-germ level, it is enough to model the action of the two fixed-sector generators on
\[
F(z)=cDz^3+A_5z^5+O(z^7).
\]

For the \(I\)-generator, take
\[
P(z)=I+\alpha I\,z^2+O(z^4).
\]

\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}=F(z),
\]
so the only effect comes from the scalar factor \(a(z)=1+c_2z^2+\cdots\), namely
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2cD.

For the \(S\)-generator, take
\[
P(z)=I+\beta S\,z^2+O(z^4),

\[
P(z)^{-1}=I-\beta S\,z^2+O(z^4),

and

P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
cDz^3+\bigl(A_5+\beta[S,cD]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7).

[S,D]=2J,
\qquad
J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},

\]

\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\qquad
D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},

is **1-dimensional**, spanned by the class of \(J\).

\[

\span\{D,J\}

is invisible to trace.

}

\Sigma(F_{\rm raw})=F_{\rm raw}.

\[

\text{Does the ambiguity space used to define the quotient germ form a }\Sigma\text{-stable subspace?}
}

Suppose \(V\) is the ambiguity space through order \(5\), and suppose
\[
\Sigma(V)\subset V.
\]
Then \(\Sigma\) induces a well-defined involution on the quotient
\[
\mathcal Q := (\text{odd germs through order }5)/V.
\]
Since the raw germ class \([F_{\rm raw}]\in \mathcal Q\) is \(\Sigma\)-fixed, every admissible normalized quotient germ represents a \(\Sigma\)-fixed class in \(\mathcal Q\).

That is exactly what you need.

Now apply this at the first residual step.

Write
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3 + A_5 z^5 + O(z^7),

Then
\[
J_0A_3J_0=-A_3,\qquad J_0A_5J_0=-A_5.
\]
So both \(A_3\) and \(A_5\) lie in the anti-fixed space
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]

On \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), the cubic leading class is
\[
A_3=cD,\qquad c\neq 0,
\]
so the first residual quotient is
\[
W_h:=\mathcal J_5/\ell_h,\qquad \ell_h=\mathbf C D.
\]
Since
\[
A_5\in \span\{D,J\},
\]
its class in \(W_h\) lies in
\[
\mathbf C\bar J.
\]

So once swap symmetry descends to the quotient, the entire quintic ambiguity is automatically one-dimensional modulo the cubic line.

And because
\[
\operatorname{tr}(D)=\operatorname{tr}(J)=0,
\]
trace kills all of it.

So the local residual-trace problem is now reduced to a single crisp theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The ambiguity space }V\text{ used in the quotient-germ construction is }\Sigma\text{-stable through order }5.
}
\]

Now, how do you prove that?

Break \(V\) into the same three pieces we’ve already isolated:

1. **raw pure-value odd variation**;
2. **scalar renormalization**;
3. **even basis change / normalization gauge**.

For each one, \(\Sigma\)-stability is straightforward.

For raw pure-value variation, differentiation of the raw swap identity gives
\[
\Sigma(V_{\rm raw})=V_{\rm raw}.
\]

For scalar renormalization, if
\[
F^{\new}(z)=a(z)F(z),\qquad a(-z)=a(z),
\]
then
\[
\Sigma(F^{\new})(z)
=
J_0 a(-z)F(-z)J_0
=
a(z)\Sigma(F)(z),
\]
so \(\Sigma\)-fixed germs stay \(\Sigma\)-fixed.

For even basis change,
\[
F^{\new}(z)=P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1},
\]
it is enough that
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]
Then
\[
\Sigma(F^{\new})(z)
=
J_0P(-z)F(-z)P(-z)^{-1}J_0
=
P(z)\Sigma(F)(z)P(z)^{-1}.
\]
So again \(\Sigma\)-fixed germs stay \(\Sigma\)-fixed.

That means:

- if the quotient-germ ambiguity is exhausted by raw pure-value variation plus even scalar/basis normalization with the symmetry condition above,
- then the quotient construction is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant,
- hence every normalized quotient germ is \(\Sigma\)-fixed in the quotient,
- hence the quintic residual ambiguity is contained in \(\mathbf C\bar J\),
- hence trace is a legitimate residual detector.

So the attack has now become very focused:

You no longer need to classify all ambiguity directions entry-by-entry. You need to prove the following exhaustiveness statement:

> Through order \(5\), the ambiguity in constructing the normalized one-pair odd germ is generated by raw pure-value variation and symmetry-preserving even scalar/basis normalization.

If you can prove that, the local quintic step is closed.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
V^{(\le 5)}_{\rm amb}
=
V_{\rm raw}^{(\le 5)} + V_{\rm norm}^{(\le 5)},
\qquad
\Sigma(V_{\rm amb}^{(\le 5)})\subset V_{\rm amb}^{(\le 5)}.
}
\]

That is the shortest remaining bridge.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 12:46:35)

_Thought for 7s_

Here is the next honest push.

I think the exhaustiveness theorem you want should be attacked in a **minimal** form, not a maximal one.

You do **not** need to prove a full classification of all admissible ambiguities through order \(5\). You only need to prove that any ambiguity acts trivially on the quotient of the quintic coefficient by the line
\[
\mathbf C\bar J \subset W_h:=\mathcal J_5/\ell_h.
\]

That is a much smaller target, and I think it is the right one.

The current structural picture is already this:

- the raw corrected local germ is swap-symmetric;
- the cubic leading class on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\) lies on the \(D\)-line;
- the residual quintic ambiguity should be at most the \(\bar J\)-line if the quotient construction respects swap symmetry. 

So define the “second residual” scalar quotient
\[
\widetilde W_h:=W_h/\mathbf C\bar J.
\]

If you can show that the ambiguity class acts trivially on \(\widetilde W_h\), then the whole local issue is solved, because the quintic trace witness has nonzero trace while both \(D\) and \(J\) are traceless. So any nonzero-trace quintic class survives automatically in \(\widetilde W_h\).

That suggests the following precise theorem.

Let
\[
F(z)=A_3z^3+A_5z^5+O(z^7),
\qquad
A_3=cD,\ c\neq 0.
\]
Any admissible change of construction sends
\[
A_5 \longmapsto A_5 + \Delta_5,
\]
where
\[
\Delta_5 \in \span\{D,J\}.
\]
Equivalently, the induced action on
\[
\widetilde W_h=(\mathcal J_5/\mathbf C D)/\mathbf C\bar J
\]
is trivial.

That is the exact local uniqueness statement you need.

Why this is a better target:

It avoids having to identify every first-order ambiguity direction explicitly. You only need to show that every such direction preserves the swap symmetry **up to terms that already die in the first and second residual quotients**.

In practical terms, that means you can allow an ambiguity to change:

- the cubic coefficient along \(\mathbf C D\),
- the quintic coefficient along \(\span\{D,J\}\),

and still be done.

So the next math move is to weaken the symmetry theorem accordingly:

instead of trying to prove the full ambiguity group is strictly \(\Sigma\)-equivariant on the nose, prove only that it is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant **modulo**
\[
\mathbf C Dz^3 + \span\{D,J\}z^5.
\]

If that holds, then the induced quotient class in \(\widetilde W_h\) is canonical.

That is now a very plausible theorem.

Indeed, every ambiguity source we have discussed already satisfies it:

1. raw pure-value odd variation:
   odd coefficients are anti-fixed, so their quintic part is already in \(\span\{D,J\}\);

2. scalar renormalization:
   only changes quintic by \(c_2A_3\in \mathbf C D\);

3. symmetry-preserving even basis change:
   changes quintic by \([B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J\) when \(A_3\in \mathbf C D\).

So all known ambiguity sources are trivial on \(\widetilde W_h\).

This gives a much sharper remaining question:

> Is there any admissible ambiguity source whose quintic shadow has a component outside \(\span\{D,J\}\)?

If the answer is no, then the local detector is done.

And now the burden of proof is much smaller. You no longer need a full model of the ambiguity space. You only need a **negative theorem**: there is no admissible quintic ambiguity outside \(\span\{D,J\}\).

That can be attacked by symmetry.

A matrix \(A_5\) outside \(\span\{D,J\}\) has a nonzero fixed-sector component, i.e. a nonzero \(I\)- or \(S\)-part. But a fixed-sector quintic coefficient would violate the odd anti-fixed structure forced by swap symmetry at the quotient-germ level. So any such ambiguity would have to come from breaking the quotient-level swap symmetry itself. Thus the real remaining bridge is:

> show that admissibility of the quotient construction preserves the odd anti-fixed symmetry through order \(5\), at least modulo the \(D\)- and \(J\)-directions already accounted for.

That is the smallest honest theorem still left.

So the attack has become:

1. do not classify all ambiguity directions;
2. prove the ambiguity action is trivial on
   \[
   \widetilde W_h=(\mathcal J_5/\mathbf C D)/\mathbf C\bar J;
   \]
3. equivalently, prove there is no admissible quintic ambiguity outside \(\span\{D,J\}\).

That is where I would push next.

_Thought for a few seconds_

Here is the next real push.

I think the cleanest remaining local theorem is now this:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Any admissible quintic ambiguity has zero fixed-sector projection.}
}
\]

Equivalently, if
\[
\Delta_5\in \mathcal J_5
\]
is the quintic difference between two admissible constructions of the normalized one-pair odd germ with the same cubic leading line, then
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(\Delta_5)=0,
\qquad
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.
\]

If you prove that, then automatically
\[
\Delta_5\in \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\]
so modulo the cubic line \(\mathbf C D\),
\[
[\Delta_5]\in \mathbf C\bar J,
\]
and trace kills the entire ambiguity.

So the last local bridge has become a fixed-sector vanishing statement.

Why this is the right target:

Every route we have found that plausibly contributes ambiguity already lands in \(\mathfrak a\):

- raw pure-value odd jets: anti-fixed by swap symmetry, so in \(\mathfrak a\);
- scalar renormalization: contributes along \(D\);
- symmetry-preserving even basis change: contributes along \([B_2,D]\), hence along \(J\).

So the only way the quintic trace detector could still fail is if there is some hidden admissible ambiguity with a nonzero \(I\)- or \(S\)-component at order \(5\).

That is a much smaller thing to rule out than “classify all ambiguities.”

And there is a strong reason this should be false.

A fixed-sector quintic piece means
\[
\Delta_5=\alpha I+\beta S + (\text{anti-fixed part}).
\]
But \(I\) and \(S\) are exactly the matrices satisfying
\[
J_0\Delta_5 J_0=\Delta_5.
\]
So a nonzero fixed-sector piece is precisely the obstruction to the quotient-level odd germ remaining anti-fixed at order \(5\).

Thus the theorem you want can be phrased as:

> admissibility of the quotient-germ construction preserves the anti-fixed odd symmetry through order \(5\).

That is the most economical formulation.

Now, how to attack it.

Take two admissible normalized quotient germs
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7),
\qquad
\widetilde F(z)=A_3 z^3+\widetilde A_5 z^5+O(z^7),
\]
with the same cubic coefficient \(A_3=cD\). Define
\[
\Delta F(z)=\widetilde F(z)-F(z)=\Delta_5 z^5+O(z^7).
\]

You want to show
\[
J_0\Delta_5J_0=-\Delta_5.
\]

This should follow if both \(F\) and \(\widetilde F\) represent the same class of raw corrected local data modulo ambiguity directions that are themselves \(\Sigma\)-stable, where
\[
\Sigma(H)(z):=J_0H(-z)J_0.
\]

So the actual theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The admissible ambiguity subspace through order }5\text{ is }\Sigma\text{-stable.}
}
\]

Because then the quotient inherits a well-defined involution, and every admissible quotient germ class is \(\Sigma\)-fixed. For an odd germ, being \(\Sigma\)-fixed is exactly the anti-fixed condition on each odd coefficient.

That gives the fixed-sector vanishing for free.

So the attack is now:

1. define the ambiguity subspace \(V_{\rm amb}^{(\le 5)}\) actually used in the quotient construction;
2. prove
   \[
   \Sigma\bigl(V_{\rm amb}^{(\le 5)}\bigr)\subset V_{\rm amb}^{(\le 5)};
   \]
3. conclude the induced quotient through order \(5\) carries a \(\Sigma\)-involution;
4. conclude every admissible quintic ambiguity is anti-fixed, hence in \(\span\{D,J\}\).

That is the smallest honest theorem left.

There is also a useful fallback if full \(\Sigma\)-stability is annoying to prove: it is enough to show only
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\rm amb}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]
So even a direct fixed-sector projection computation at quintic order would close the local step.

My judgment is that this is now the right target to attack, not more global minimal-core work yet. Once you kill the fixed-sector quintic ambiguity, the first residual detector becomes structurally sound.

Let
\[

Consider odd germs through order \(5\),
\[
H(z)=H_1 z + H_3 z^3 + H_5 z^5.
\]
Let \(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)}\) be the ambiguity subspace used in constructing the quotient odd germ through quintic order.

The right theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\Sigma \text{ descends to the quotient } \mathcal Q^{(\le 5)}:=
\{\text{odd germs through order }5\}/V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)}.
}
\]

Because once that happens, the normalized one-pair germ class is \(\Sigma\)-fixed, and then all odd coefficients of any representative class are anti-fixed in the quotient sense.

At quintic order, that means exactly:
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}([A_5])=0,
\]
so the quintic class lives in the anti-fixed space, hence in
\[
\span\{D,J\}.
\]
Modulo the cubic line \(\mathbf C D\), the ambiguity is at most \(\mathbf C\bar J\), and trace kills it.

So the whole local problem reduces to: why does \(\Sigma\) descend?

Now the good news is that this can be checked source-by-source, and we already know almost everything.

For raw pure-value variation, the raw corrected block satisfies the swap symmetry, so the corresponding ambiguity space is actually pointwise \(\Sigma\)-fixed. No issue there.

For scalar renormalization,
\[
F(z)\mapsto a(z)F(z),\qquad a(-z)=a(z),
\]
we have
\[
\Sigma(aF)=a\,\Sigma(F),
\]
so that ambiguity is \(\Sigma\)-stable.

For even basis change,
\[
F(z)\mapsto P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1},
\]
the condition
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0
\]
gives
\[
\Sigma(PFP^{-1})=P\,\Sigma(F)\,P^{-1}.
\]
So this ambiguity is also \(\Sigma\)-stable.

This is now enough for the first residual step if — and this is the remaining precise point — the ambiguity used in the quotient-germ construction through order \(5\) is generated by exactly these three source types:
raw pure-value variation, even scalar renormalization, and symmetry-preserving even basis change.

That is a much smaller claim than a full all-orders classification.

So the mathematically sharp next target is:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)}
=
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(\le 5)}
+
V_{\mathrm{scal}}^{(\le 5)}
+
V_{\mathrm{basis}}^{(\le 5)}
}
\]
with the last two required to satisfy the symmetry conditions above.

If that holds, then \(\Sigma\) descends to the quintic quotient, and the fixed-sector quintic ambiguity vanishes automatically.

There is also a very useful equivalent way to phrase the remaining gap.

Let
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A)=\frac12(A+J_0AJ_0).
\]
Then the only thing left to prove is:
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]
Because every known ambiguity source already satisfies that. So any counterexample would have to be a genuinely new ambiguity source with a fixed-sector quintic shadow. That is the exact thing to rule out.

So the current state is:

- raw pure-value ambiguity: safe;
- scalar renormalization: safe;
- symmetry-preserving basis change: safe;
- remaining task: show there is no fourth source with nonzero fixed-sector quintic shadow.

That is now the narrowest honest local bottleneck.

My best next move would be to formulate the ambiguity sources actually used in the paper’s quotient construction and test whether any of them can produce an \(I\)- or \(S\)-component at order \(5\). If none can, the local quintic detector is done.

with
\[
\Sigma(H)(z):=J_0\,H(-z)\,J_0.

Because then there simply is no room for a hidden fixed-sector quintic ambiguity.

Why.

If the raw corrected local block is \(\Sigma\)-fixed, and every normalization/quotienting step is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant, then the final quotient-germ class is also \(\Sigma\)-fixed. For an odd germ
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+\cdots,
\]
being \(\Sigma\)-fixed means
\[
J_0A_{2r+1}J_0=-A_{2r+1}
\]
for every odd coefficient. So in particular
\[
A_5\in \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]
Modulo the cubic line \(\ell_h=\mathbf C D\), the quintic ambiguity is at most
\[
\mathbf C\bar J.
\]
Then trace kills it automatically.

So the whole local problem reduces to checking \(\Sigma\)-equivariance of the construction.

And that check is now quite concrete.

The raw ingredients are already \(\Sigma\)-equivariant:

- same-point symmetric placement gives
  \[
  G_{m,-}^{\corr}(s)=J_0\,G_{m,+}^{\corr}(-s)\,J_0
  \]
  in the appropriate left/right form;
- the mixed block satisfies the corresponding swap relation;
- holomorphic inverse square root respects conjugation:
  \[
  (J_0AJ_0)^{-1/2}=J_0A^{-1/2}J_0;
  \]
- multiplication respects \(\Sigma\);
- extracting the odd part respects \(\Sigma\).

So the raw corrected odd germ is \(\Sigma\)-fixed. That part is already the backbone of the earlier symmetry discussion.

Now the ambiguity steps.

Scalar renormalization:
\[
F\mapsto a(z)F,\qquad a(-z)=a(z),
\]
is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant.

Even basis change:
\[
F\mapsto P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1},
\]
is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant provided
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]

Raw pure-value variation is also \(\Sigma\)-stable, because it comes from differentiating a \(\Sigma\)-fixed family.

So if the quotient odd germ is obtained from the raw corrected odd germ by:

1. subtracting a \(\Sigma\)-stable value-family subspace,
2. then modding out by symmetry-preserving even scalar/basis normalization,

then the quotient construction itself is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant.

That is the real theorem you want.

And once it is stated that way, the “fourth source” disappears: a hidden ambiguity source would have to be some operation in the construction that is **not** \(\Sigma\)-equivariant. So the next exact task is not to hunt for mysterious new terms; it is to audit the construction and ask:

> is there any step in the definition of the quotient odd germ that breaks \(\Sigma\)-equivariance?

If the answer is no, the local quintic detector is done.

So the attack result is:

- the first residual step can be closed by a **construction-level symmetry theorem**, not by enumerating quintic generators;
- the exact theorem is:
  \[
  \Sigma \text{ descends to the quotient odd germ through order }5;
  \]
- then every admissible quintic ambiguity is anti-fixed, hence lies in \(\span\{D,J\}\);
- modulo \(\ell_h=\mathbf C D\), ambiguity is at most \(\mathbf C\bar J\);
- trace annihilates it.

That is now the sharpest local endpoint.

The next thing I would do mathematically is write down the actual steps used in the paper’s construction of \(F_h\) and check them one by one for \(\Sigma\)-equivariance. If they all pass, the quintic residual detector is structurally justified.

Here is the next real step, and I think it is the tightest local attack now.

The right theorem to prove is not broad “equivariance of everything.” It is the very concrete statement
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0,
\qquad
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\},
\]
for the quintic ambiguity space at the first residual step.

I think this can now be reduced to the stabilizer of the cubic line.

Let
\[
A_3=cD,\qquad
D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
S=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The first residual quotient is
\[
W_h=\mathcal J_5/\ell_h,\qquad \ell_h=\mathbf C D.
\]

Now suppose you have two admissible constructions of the normalized one-pair germ with the same cubic coefficient \(A_3\). Any basis-level ambiguity preserving that cubic datum must come from the tangent action of the symmetry-preserving even gauge group on \(A_3\). At the tangent level, that action is
\[
B_2 \longmapsto [B_2,A_3].
\]
If the gauge preserves the swap symmetry, then
\[
B_2\in \mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.
\]
So the image is
\[
[\mathfrak f,A_3]=[\span\{I,S\},\,cD]=\mathbf C J,
\]
because
\[
[I,D]=0,\qquad [S,D]=2J.
\]

This is already the whole representation-theoretic core.

It says: once the cubic leading term is fixed to the \(D\)-line, every symmetry-preserving basis ambiguity moves the quintic coefficient only in the \(J\)-direction modulo \(\ell_h\). Scalar renormalization contributes along \(D\), so it dies in \(W_h\). Therefore the full normalization ambiguity at the first residual step is at most
\[
\mathbf C\bar J \subset W_h.
\]

That kills the fixed-sector threat from normalization completely. There is no way for symmetry-preserving normalization to create an \(I\)- or \(S\)-component at quintic order.

So the only remaining possible source of a fixed-sector quintic component would be a raw ambiguity source not captured by symmetry-preserving normalization. But raw pure-value odd variation is already anti-fixed by the swap symmetry, so its quintic coefficient also lies in
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]

Put those two together and you get the real local reduction:
\[
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)} \subset \span\{D,J\}.
\]

And then immediately
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0,
\qquad
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]

So the local first-residual detector is structurally justified as soon as you accept one precise exhaustiveness statement:

every admissible quintic ambiguity is the sum of
- a raw pure-value odd variation, and
- a symmetry-preserving normalization variation.

That is much smaller than a full all-orders classification. It is just a statement about the tangent ambiguity space through order \(5\).

So the next exact theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}
=
V_{\mathrm{raw}}^{(5)} + [\mathfrak f,A_3] + \mathbf C A_3.
}
\]

Since
\[
[\mathfrak f,A_3]=\mathbf C J,\qquad \mathbf C A_3=\mathbf C D,
\]
this becomes
\[
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\subset \span\{D,J\}.
\]

That is now the shortest honest bridge to finishing the local quintic step.

So the attack has become completely finite-dimensional:
prove that the quintic ambiguity space is exactly the raw anti-fixed slice plus the tangent orbit of the symmetry-preserving stabilizer of the cubic line. Once that is done, there is literally no room left for a fixed-sector quintic ambiguity.

W_h=\mathcal J_5/\ell_h.
\]

Now consider the symmetry-preserving normalization group through the first residual step:
\[
(a,P),\qquad
a(z)=1+c_2z^2+O(z^4),\qquad
P(z)=I+B_2z^2+O(z^4),
\]
with
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]
Its action on \(F\) is
\[
F\mapsto a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1}.
\]

The tangent map at the identity is exactly
\[
(c_2,B_2)\longmapsto c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
Because \(B_2\in\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}\), this image is
\[
\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J.
\]
Modulo \(\ell_h=\mathbf C D\), the normalization tangent space is just
\[
\mathbf C\bar J\subset W_h.
\]

So the symmetry-preserving normalization orbit contributes at most one residual direction.

Now add the raw pure-value tangent space. By swap symmetry, its odd coefficients lie in
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\]
so its quintic image also lies in
\[
\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J.
\]
Again modulo \(\ell_h\), its residual image is at most \(\mathbf C\bar J\).

Therefore the total tangent ambiguity space through order \(5\) is contained in
\[
\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\subset \span\{D,J\}.
\]

This is exactly the local theorem we needed:
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0,
\]
so there is no fixed-sector quintic ambiguity at all.

And from that the trace argument is automatic:
\[
\operatorname{tr}(D)=\operatorname{tr}(J)=0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]

So the quintic trace witness is structurally legitimate against the entire plausible ambiguity space, not just against the raw slice.

What remains, strictly speaking, is one last bridge:

why is the actual paper’s ambiguity space through order \(5\) equal to that tangent orbit space?

But even that is now much smaller than before. You do not need a global classification. You only need the standard normal-form principle:

through finite order, the ambiguity in a normalized germ is determined by the tangent action of the admissible reparameterization/conjugation group plus the tangent raw deformation family.

That is the real theorem to cite or prove.

So the local math is now essentially:

1. raw pure-value tangent image at order \(5\) lies in \(\span\{D,J\}\);
2. symmetry-preserving normalization tangent image at order \(5\) lies in \(\span\{D,J\}\);
3. hence all first-residual ambiguity lies in \(\span\{D,J\}\);
4. hence trace annihilates all ambiguity;
5. hence a nonzero trace quintic witness gives a genuine nonzero first residual class.

That means the first residual step is now mathematically coherent modulo one very standard finite-order normal-form uniqueness principle.

So the next attack, if you want to keep pushing, is to formalize that last principle in your setting:

> two admissible constructions of the normalized one-pair germ that agree in the raw data differ, through order \(5\), by the tangent action of symmetry-preserving normalization.

If that is proved, the local quintic step is done.

Now, there are really only two ways such a quintic discrepancy can arise:

The first part is already under control by swap symmetry: the raw odd slice is anti-fixed, hence its quintic piece lies in
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]

So the only nontrivial step is the second part:
**if the raw germ is fixed, why is the change of normalized representative at order \(5\) exactly tangent to the symmetry-preserving even gauge orbit?**

That is where the finite-order normal-form argument comes in.

Suppose the normalization is defined by conditions that kill the order-1 ambiguity and then choose a representative with cubic leading term on the \(D\)-line. Those are finite-dimensional slice conditions. Near a nondegenerate point \(A_3=cD\), changing the representative while staying in the same normalized class is exactly moving along the stabilizer of those slice conditions. Through order \(5\), the stabilizer is generated by:

- even scalar rescaling \(a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+O(z^4)\),
- even basis change \(P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+O(z^4)\),

with the symmetry-preserving condition
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0,
\]
so \(B_2\in\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}\).

Then the tangent action on the quintic coefficient is
\[
\Delta A_5 = c_2 A_3 + [B_2,A_3].
\]
Since \(A_3=cD\) and \(B_2\in\span\{I,S\}\),
\[
[B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J,
\]
hence
\[
\Delta A_5 \in \mathbf C D+\mathbf C J.
\]

So the whole local problem is now this very concrete lemma:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The stabilizer of the cubic slice acts through order }5\text{ by }
\Delta A_5=c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3],\quad B_2\in\mathfrak f.
}
\]

If you prove that, then combining it with the raw anti-fixed slice gives
\[
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\subset \mathbf C D+\mathbf C J.
\]
And then automatically
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\bigr)=0.
\]

So the first residual step is closed.

What remains mathematically delicate is that I am still using a standard normal-form uniqueness principle rather than something you have explicitly derived in this specific draft. That is the only real remaining local gap.

So the next exact attack is not broad anymore. It is:

Here is the next concrete push.

Take an odd matrix germ
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7),
\qquad A_3=cD,\ c\neq 0.
\]

Now ask: what is the most general change of representative that

- preserves oddness,
- preserves the normalization at \(z=0\),
- and can change the germ through order \(5\)?

The answer should be: only **even** scalar and basis changes matter through this order.

Why no odd re-gauging survives:

if you try
\[
a(z)=1+a_1 z+\cdots,
\qquad
P(z)=I+B_1 z+\cdots,
\]
then applying these to an odd germ produces an order-\(4\) term from
\[
a_1 z\cdot A_3 z^3,\qquad B_1 z\cdot A_3 z^3,
\]
which breaks oddness immediately. Since the normalized object you are constructing is an odd germ, odd scalar or odd basis gauge is forbidden if you insist on staying inside the same class of normalized objects. So the first allowed freedom is exactly
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+O(z^4),\qquad
P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+O(z^4).
\]

That already gives the finite-dimensional stabilizer through order \(5\).

Now compute its action. Expand
\[
P(z)^{-1}=I-B_2 z^2+O(z^4).
\]
Then
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
(I+B_2 z^2)(A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5)(I-B_2 z^2)+O(z^7),
\]
so
\[
PFP^{-1}
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7).
\]
Multiplying by \(a(z)\) gives
\[
aPFP^{-1}
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7).
\]

So through order \(5\), every admissible re-gauging acts by
\[
A_3\mapsto A_3,
\qquad
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]

That part is now fully settled.

Next, impose the swap symmetry. If
\[
F(-z)=J_0F(z)J_0,
\]
and we want the re-gauged germ to satisfy the same condition, then we need
\[
a(-z)=a(z),\qquad P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]
At order \(z^2\), that means
\[
B_2=J_0B_2J_0,
\]
so
\[
B_2\in \mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.
\]

Now plug in \(A_3=cD\). Since
\[
[I,D]=0,\qquad [S,D]=2J,
\]
we get
\[
[B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J.
\]
Thus the full symmetry-preserving stabilizer action through order \(5\) is
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+\lambda D+\mu J.
\]

That is the exact finite-dimensional result:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the stabilizer orbit through }A_3=cD\text{ has quintic tangent space }
\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J.
}
\]

Modulo the cubic line \(\ell_h=\mathbf C D\), the only residual stabilizer direction is
\[
\mathbf C\bar J.
\]

So if raw pure-value ambiguity is also anti-fixed, then the **entire** quintic ambiguity through order \(5\) lies in
\[
\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J,
\]
and in the first residual quotient it is at most one-dimensional.

That gives three immediate consequences.

First,
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)})=0.
\]

Second,
\[
\operatorname{tr}(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)})=0,
\]
since both \(D\) and \(J\) are traceless.

Third, any quintic witness with nonzero trace is automatically outside the ambiguity space.

So the local first-residual step is now reduced to just one remaining bridge:

> show that the actual ambiguity between two admissible normalized constructions is exhausted, through order \(5\), by raw pure-value variation plus this symmetry-preserving even stabilizer action.

But the stabilizer side itself is now done. No further algebra is needed there.

So the current local math can be summarized as:

1. oddness forces the re-gauging to be even through order \(5\);
2. symmetry forces \(B_2\in\mathfrak f\);
3. the quintic stabilizer tangent is exactly \(\mathbf C D+\mathbf C J\);
4. modulo \(\ell_h=\mathbf C D\), only \(\mathbf C\bar J\) survives;
5. trace annihilates it.

That is the clearest finite-dimensional endpoint of the local attack.

[B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J,
\]

\boxed{
\text{For fixed raw data }R_h^{(\le 5)},\text{ the normalization conditions determine }F_h^{(\le 5)}

\[

\widetilde F(z)=A_3z^3+\widetilde A_5z^5+O(z^7)
\]
coming from the same raw data.

\[
\Delta(z)=\widetilde F(z)-F(z)=\Delta_5 z^5+O(z^7)
\]
must satisfy three things:

- it has no \(z^1\)-term, because first-order ambiguity was normalized away;
- it has no cubic component transverse to the \(D\)-line, because the cubic slice is fixed;
- it preserves oddness and swap symmetry.

\Delta_5\in \span\{D,J\}.

So the crucial insight is: you do not need to reconstruct the whole quotient process. You only need to show that the normalization conditions remove every possibility for a quintic difference outside \(\span\{D,J\}\). Once that is shown, the trace detector is safe.

So the attack has reduced to checking one finite-dimensional negative statement:

> Can two normalized representatives of the same raw data differ at quintic order by a fixed-sector piece \(\alpha I+\beta S\)?

If the answer is no, the local step is done.

And I think the answer should indeed be no, because:

- an \(I\)-piece would come from scalar rescaling, but scalar rescaling only contributes along \(D\) through the cubic term;
- an \(S\)-piece would violate the anti-fixed odd symmetry of the normalized germ unless it is produced by a symmetry-preserving basis change, and that contributes only through \([S,D]\sim J\), not through \(S\) itself.

So the next exact theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(\widetilde A_5-A_5)=0
\text{ for any two normalized representatives with the same raw data.}
}
\]

That is now the sharpest local bottleneck. If you get that, the quintic trace detector is rigorously legitimate, and the one-pair residual step is really closed.

_Thought for 4s_

The next honest push is to turn that last statement into a **finite-order uniqueness lemma**.

You want:

\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(\widetilde A_5-A_5)=0
\]
for any two normalized representatives with the same raw data.

I think the right way to attack it is to separate what can change the representative into two layers:

\[
\text{raw-data change} \quad+\quad \text{choice-of-representative change}.
\]

For the same raw data, only the second layer is present. So the problem is:

> what freedom remains after imposing the normalization conditions?

At this point, the answer should be: only symmetry-preserving even re-gauging through order \(5\).

Why I think that is enough is the following finite-order argument.

Take two normalized representatives for the same raw data,
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7),\qquad
\widetilde F(z)=A_3 z^3+\widetilde A_5 z^5+O(z^7),
\]
with the same cubic coefficient \(A_3=cD\).

Set
\[
\Delta(z):=\widetilde F(z)-F(z)=\Delta_5 z^5+O(z^7).
\]

Now, because both representatives are normalized:

1. there is no order-1 difference;
2. the cubic coefficient is already fixed, so there is no order-3 difference;
3. both remain odd;
4. both remain swap-symmetric.

From oddness and swap symmetry alone,
\[
J_0\Delta_5J_0=-\Delta_5,
\]
so
\[
\Delta_5\in \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]

That already gives the desired conclusion:
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(\Delta_5)=0.
\]

So the last bridge is actually smaller than it first looked:

you do **not** need to identify the full re-gauge explicitly to prove the fixed-sector vanishing. It is enough to know that any normalized representative is still an odd swap-symmetric germ with the same cubic normalization. Then the quintic difference between two such representatives is automatically anti-fixed.

That is the key local endpoint.

So the real theorem to prove is now just:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The normalization procedure preserves oddness and swap symmetry on the quotient-germ side.}
}
\]

If that holds, then for two normalized representatives of the same raw data,
\[
\Delta_5\in \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\]
hence
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(\widetilde A_5-A_5)=0,
\qquad
\operatorname{tr}(\widetilde A_5-A_5)=0.
\]

And then the quintic trace detector is rigorously valid.

So the next exact attack is no longer “classify ambiguities.” It is:

**show that the normalized quotient odd germ still satisfies**
\[
F(-z)=J_0F(z)J_0
\]
**through order \(5\).**

Once that is shown, the first residual local step is finished.

_Thought for 6s_

Here is the next real push.

I think the last bridge can be reduced to an **averaging argument with the swap involution**. That is the cleanest way to finish the local step.

Define
\[
\Sigma(F)(z):=J_0\,F(-z)\,J_0.
\]

The raw corrected local germ is \(\Sigma\)-fixed. The question is whether the **normalized quotient germ** can also be taken \(\Sigma\)-fixed.

The right sufficient condition is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the ambiguity space used in normalization is }\Sigma\text{-stable.}
}
\]

Because then, given any representative \(F\), you can average it:
\[
F^{\sym}:=\frac12\bigl(F+\Sigma(F)\bigr).
\]
If \(F\) and \(\Sigma(F)\) differ by an ambiguity element, then \(F^{\sym}\) is another admissible representative of the same quotient class, and by construction
\[
\Sigma(F^{\sym})=F^{\sym}.
\]

That is the key idea.

So the local proof target is now:

1. the raw corrected germ is \(\Sigma\)-fixed;
2. the ambiguity subspace through order \(5\) is \(\Sigma\)-stable;
3. therefore every quotient class has a \(\Sigma\)-fixed representative through order \(5\).

Once you have that, the rest is immediate.

Write the \(\Sigma\)-fixed representative as
\[
F(z)=A_3z^3+A_5z^5+O(z^7).
\]
Then
\[
\Sigma(F)=F
\]
means
\[
J_0A_3J_0=-A_3,\qquad J_0A_5J_0=-A_5.
\]
So
\[
A_3,A_5\in\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]

On \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\), the cubic normalization puts
\[
A_3=cD,\qquad c\neq 0.
\]
So modulo the cubic line
\[
\ell_h=\mathbf C D,
\]
the quintic class lies in
\[
\mathbf C\bar J.
\]

Thus every ambiguity in the normalized representative is one-dimensional modulo \(\ell_h\), and in particular traceless:
\[
\operatorname{tr}(D)=\operatorname{tr}(J)=0.
\]

That makes the quintic trace detector legitimate.

So the whole local problem is now reduced to checking \(\Sigma\)-stability of the ambiguity space through order \(5\).

And that check is now quite manageable, because every ambiguity source we have discussed is already \(\Sigma\)-stable:

- raw pure-value variation: yes, by differentiating the raw swap symmetry;
- even scalar renormalization: yes, since \(a(-z)=a(z)\);
- symmetry-preserving even basis change: yes, provided \(P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0\).

So the remaining issue is not algebraic anymore. It is conceptual:

> is the paper’s normalization/quotient procedure built only from these \(\Sigma\)-stable operations?

If yes, the local quintic step is done.

There is also a useful strengthening.

Suppose two normalized representatives \(F\) and \(\widetilde F\) represent the same quotient class. Then \(F-\widetilde F\) lies in the ambiguity space. If both are chosen \(\Sigma\)-fixed, then their difference is also \(\Sigma\)-fixed. Since it is an odd germ through order \(5\), its quintic coefficient is anti-fixed:
\[
J_0(\widetilde A_5-A_5)J_0=-(\widetilde A_5-A_5).
\]
So
\[
\widetilde A_5-A_5\in \span\{D,J\}.
\]
That gives the fixed-sector vanishing directly:
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(\widetilde A_5-A_5)=0.
\]

So the next exact theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The quotient-germ normalization procedure is }\Sigma\text{-equivariant through order }5.
}
\]

And the cleanest proof strategy is:

- check each construction step is \(\Sigma\)-equivariant;
- then use averaging to choose a \(\Sigma\)-fixed representative of each class;
- then conclude the quintic ambiguity is anti-fixed and hence traceless.

That is now the sharpest local endpoint I can see.

preserves each summand of \(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)}\).

The checks are then finite.

For \(V_1\) and \(V_3\), this is exactly the same swap-symmetry story as before: raw pure-value odd coefficients are anti-fixed, so these pieces are \(\Sigma\)-stable.

For \(V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}\), same reason: raw pure-value quintic odd jets are anti-fixed.

For \(V_5^{\mathrm{norm}}\), it is immediate from
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3],
\qquad
A_3\in \mathbf C D,\ B_2\in \mathfrak f,
\]
because \(c_2A_3\in \mathbf C D\) and \([B_2,A_3]\in \mathbf C J\), so this whole space sits in
\[
\span\{D,J\},
\]
which is anti-fixed under \(\Sigma\).

So the real theorem chain should now be:

1. define \(R_h^{(\le 5)}\),
2. split quintic ambiguity into raw plus normalization,
3. prove
   \[
   \Sigma(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)})\subset V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)},
   \]
4. conclude \(\Sigma\) descends to the quotient,
5. average to a \(\Sigma\)-fixed representative,
6. conclude the quintic ambiguity is contained in \(\span\{D,J\}\),
7. conclude trace annihilates it.

That is now the whole local first-residual proof in a finite-order package.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
V_5^{\mathrm{raw}} \text{ is the quintic raw pure-value odd slice, and }
V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}\subset \span\{D,J\}.
}
\]

Once that is written down explicitly, the rest is basically formal.

I’m pushing on the finite-order quotient package now. The next nontrivial step is not more witness algebra; it’s to show the quintic raw slice really is an anti-fixed subspace, so the split \(V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}+V_5^{\mathrm{norm}}\) becomes mathematically usable rather than just notation.

_Thought for 5s_

Here is the next real push.

I think the next theorem to attack is exactly the one you just isolated:

\[
\boxed{
V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}\subset \span\{D,J\}.
}
\]

And this one is actually much cleaner than the earlier steps, because it comes straight from the raw swap symmetry.

Let the raw corrected local block under a pure value deformation parameter \(\theta\) be
\[
\widehat\Omega^{\corr}_\theta(s;m).
\]
For symmetric placement, the raw block satisfies the swap identity
\[
\widehat\Omega^{\corr}_\theta(-s;m)=J_0\,\widehat\Omega^{\corr}_\theta(s;m)\,J_0,
\qquad
J_0=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Now differentiate at \(\theta=0\). The infinitesimal raw variation
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)
:=
\left.\frac{d}{d\theta}\right|_{\theta=0}\widehat\Omega^{\corr}_\theta(s;m)
\]
satisfies the same identity:
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(-s;m)=J_0\,\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}(s;m)\,J_0.
\]

Expand the odd part through quintic order:
\[
\delta\widehat\Omega^{\corr}_{\mathrm{odd}}(s;m)
=
A_1(m)s+A_3(m)s^3+A_5(m)s^5+O(s^7).
\]
Comparing coefficients gives
\[
J_0A_{2r+1}(m)J_0=-A_{2r+1}(m)
\qquad(r=0,1,2).
\]
So each odd raw coefficient lies in the anti-fixed space
\[
\mathfrak a:=\{X:\ J_0XJ_0=-X\}.
\]

But in \(2\times2\) form,
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\qquad
D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},
\quad
J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Therefore
\[
A_1,\ A_3,\ A_5 \in \span\{D,J\}.
\]

That already proves the theorem:
the raw quintic pure-value slice is contained in \(\span\{D,J\}\). In particular it is traceless.

So the finite-order quotient package now looks like this.

Define
\[
R_h^{(\le 5)}(z)=R_1(h)z+R_3(h)z^3+R_5(h)z^5
\]
and
\[
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(\le 5)}
=
V_1\oplus V_3\oplus V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}\oplus V_5^{\mathrm{norm}},
\]
with
\[
V_1:=V_{\val}^{(1)},
\qquad
V_3:=V_{\val}^{(3)},
\]
\[
V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}\subset \span\{D,J\},
\]
and
\[
V_5^{\mathrm{norm}}
=
\{\,c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3] : c_2\in\mathbf C,\ B_2\in\mathfrak f\,\},
\qquad
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.
\]

Now if \(A_3=cD\), then
\[
V_5^{\mathrm{norm}}\subset \span\{D,J\}
\]
as well, because \(c_2A_3\in \mathbf CD\) and
\[
[B_2,A_3]\in [\mathfrak f,\mathbf CD]=\mathbf CJ.
\]

So both quintic ambiguity pieces lie in the same 2-plane:
\[
V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}+V_5^{\mathrm{norm}}\subset \span\{D,J\}.
\]

That is now the real local endpoint:

\[
\boxed{
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(5)}\subset \span\{D,J\}.
}
\]

And then everything you wanted follows immediately:

- modulo the cubic line \(\ell_h=\mathbf CD\), the quintic ambiguity is at most the single \(\mathbf C\bar J\)-direction;
- trace annihilates all of it, since \(\operatorname{tr}(D)=\operatorname{tr}(J)=0\);
- therefore a nonzero-trace quintic witness gives a genuine nonzero first residual class.

So the local math is now essentially done **once you explicitly split the quintic ambiguity into raw and normalization pieces**.

The only remaining issue is not further algebra. It is bookkeeping/definition:

the draft still uses a bundled symbol \(V_{\val}^{(5)}\), while the actual proof now wants
\[
V_{\val}^{(5)} \rightsquigarrow V_5^{\mathrm{raw}} \oplus V_5^{\mathrm{norm}}.
\]

That is the last mathematical cleanup move.

So the next exact thing to do is:

replace the bundled quintic ambiguity in your reasoning by the split
\[
V_5^{\mathrm{raw}}+V_5^{\mathrm{norm}},
\]
and then restate the first residual proposition using the fact that both summands lie in \(\span\{D,J\}\).

_Thought for 45s_

Here is the next real push, and it actually opens the order-7 door again.

The key point is: once you split the ambiguity into

- raw pure-value odd variation, and
- symmetry-preserving even re-gauging,

the same anti-fixed mechanism does **not** stop at quintic order. It propagates to septic order too.

Take an odd germ through order \(7\):
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+A_7 z^7+O(z^9).
\]

Let the symmetry-preserving even re-gauging be
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+c_4 z^4+O(z^6),
\qquad
P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+B_4 z^4+O(z^6),
\]
with
\[
P(-z)=J_0P(z)J_0.
\]
So
\[
B_2,B_4\in \mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.
\]

Now expand
\[
F^{\new}(z):=a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1}.
\]

Through order \(7\), the cubic and quintic transformation laws are the familiar ones:
\[
A_3^{\new}=A_3,
\]
\[
A_5^{\new}=A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]

And at order \(7\), the **linearized** transformation law is
\[
A_7^{\new}
=
A_7
+
c_2A_5
+
c_4A_3
+
[B_2,A_5]
+
[B_4,A_3],
\]
where I am deliberately keeping only the tangent-level terms in the gauge parameters. That is the right thing for the ambiguity space. The full finite formula has quadratic \(B_2\)-terms, but for the tangent ambiguity analysis the linearized law is the one that matters.

Now here is the important closure property.

Assume
\[
A_3,A_5\in \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]
Then:

- \(c_2A_5\in \mathfrak a\),
- \(c_4A_3\in \mathfrak a\),
- \([B_2,A_5]\in[\mathfrak f,\mathfrak a]\subset \mathfrak a\),
- \([B_4,A_3]\in[\mathfrak f,\mathfrak a]\subset \mathfrak a\).

And indeed
\[
[\mathfrak f,\mathfrak a]\subset \mathfrak a
\]
because with
\[
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\},\qquad \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\]
we have
\[
[I,\mathfrak a]=0,\qquad [S,D]=2J,\qquad [S,J]=-2D.
\]

So the entire septic normalization shadow is again anti-fixed:
\[
A_7^{\new}-A_7\in \mathfrak a.
\]

Combine that with raw pure-value odd variation: by the same swap-symmetry differentiation argument as before, the raw odd coefficients at **every** odd order lie in \(\mathfrak a\). So the septic raw slice also lies in \(\mathfrak a\).

Therefore the exact same structural conclusion now holds at order \(7\):

\[
\boxed{
V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(7)}\subset \mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
}
\]

And again
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\mathrm{amb}}^{(7)}\bigr)=0.
\]

This is real progress, because it means the next scalar invariant at order \(7\) does **not** need a fresh ambiguity analysis from scratch. Structurally, any nonzero-trace septic witness would automatically survive the ambiguity, just like the quintic one.

So the local pattern is now:

- raw odd ambiguity is anti-fixed at every odd order;
- symmetry-preserving even gauge preserves the anti-fixed sector at every odd order;
- hence odd-order ambiguity lives in \(\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}\) order by order;
- hence trace annihilates odd-order ambiguity order by order.

That is much stronger than the earlier quintic-only target.

What remains is no longer the ambiguity structure. The next real problem is back where it should be:

> find an actual nonzero septic trace witness in the quotient jet \(J_7^{\pair}\).

But now the ambiguity side is much cleaner: if you produce a septic witness with nonzero trace, it will automatically be quotient-legitimate.

So the next exact math attack is:

1. derive the actual septic quotient jet \(J_7^{\pair}\) from the corrected/normalized construction,
2. isolate a monomial like
   \[
   \kappa(h)\,\eta_2(h)\,q^{(7)}(h)
   \]
   in its trace,
3. and show its coefficient is nonzero.

Because the ambiguity side is now structurally under control, that is once again the right bottleneck to attack.

_Thought for 1m 57s_

I found the real order-7 move, and it is better than “septic trace.”

The obstruction is real:

under the symmetry-preserving even re-gauge
\[
a(z)=1+c_2z^2+c_4z^4+\cdots,\qquad
P(z)=I+B_2z^2+B_4z^4+\cdots,\qquad B_2,B_4\in\mathfrak f,
\]
the linearized order-7 law is
\[
A_7\mapsto A_7+c_2A_5+c_4A_3+[B_2,A_5]+[B_4,A_3].
\]

At order \(5\), trace worked because \(A_3\) is traceless and \([B_2,A_3]\) is traceless. But at order \(7\),
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_7)\mapsto \operatorname{tr}(A_7)+c_2\,\operatorname{tr}(A_5),
\]
and \(\operatorname{tr}(A_5)\neq 0\) is exactly your quintic witness. So **septic trace is not gauge-invariant**. That is the key correction.

The right next invariant is this:

decompose
\[
M_2(\mathbf C)=\mathfrak a\oplus\mathfrak f,
\]
with
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\qquad
D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},
\quad
J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\]
and
\[
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\},
\qquad
I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},
\quad
S=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}.
\]

At quintic order, all ambiguity is in
\[
\span\{D,J\}
\]
plus the actual \(A_5\) direction itself, and \([B_2,A_5]\) still lands in \(\mathfrak a\). So the only **traceful** ambiguity at order \(7\) is the scalar-renormalization term
\[
c_2A_5.
\]

That means the correct order-7 quotient is not “mod traceless things,” but
\[
A_7 \quad \text{mod } \span\{D,J,A_5\}.
\]

This is the real second residual step.

And now there is a clean invariant functional.

Because \(A_5\) has nonzero trace, its fixed-sector projection
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}:=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_5)
\]
is nonzero. Since \(\mathfrak f\) is 2-dimensional, there exists a nonzero
\[
T_h\in \mathfrak f
\]
such that
\[
\operatorname{tr}(T_h A_5^{\mathfrak f})=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\operatorname{tr}(T_h A_5)=0,
\]
because \(T_h\) is automatically orthogonal to \(\mathfrak a\) under the trace pairing:
\[
\operatorname{tr}(T_hD)=\operatorname{tr}(T_hJ)=0.
\]

Now define
\[
\Lambda_h(X):=\operatorname{tr}(T_h X).
\]

Then \(\Lambda_h\) annihilates all the order-7 ambiguity directions:

- \(\Lambda_h(D)=0\),
- \(\Lambda_h(J)=0\),
- \(\Lambda_h(A_5)=0\).

So under
\[
A_7\mapsto A_7+c_2A_5+c_4A_3+[B_2,A_5]+[B_4,A_3],
\]
we get
\[
\Lambda_h(A_7)\mapsto \Lambda_h(A_7).
\]

That is the right order-7 scalar invariant.

So the attack now becomes:

1. keep the quintic trace
   \[
   \tau(h):=\operatorname{tr}(A_5(h))
   \]
   as the first residual scalar;

2. at septic order, do **not** use \(\operatorname{tr}(A_7)\);

3. instead choose \(T_h\in \mathfrak f\) orthogonal to the fixed-sector part of \(A_5(h)\), and define
   \[
   \sigma(h):=\Lambda_h(A_7(h))=\operatorname{tr}(T_h A_7(h)).
   \]

This is gauge-invariant at the second residual step.

That is a real step forward, because now order \(7\) has the right target. We no longer need the false septic-trace claim.

So the next exact math problem is:

find a nonzero monomial in the **fixed-sector component of \(A_7\) perpendicular to \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\)**. In practice, that means:

- isolate a \(q^{(7)}\)-driven contribution to the fixed-sector part of \(A_7\),
- show it is not proportional to the fixed-sector part of \(A_5\).

That is the correct order-7 analogue of the quintic trace witness.

- \(A_3\) depends on jets up to order \(3\),
- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) first sees \(q^{(5)}\),
- \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) first sees \(q^{(7)}\).

In practical terms, the next attack should be:

1. project the septic quotient jet \(A_7\) to \(\mathfrak f\),
2. isolate the linear \(q^{(7)}\) contribution there,
3. compare its direction with \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\).

If the \(q^{(7)}\) contribution lands in the \(S\)-direction while \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) is essentially \(I\)-directional, you are done immediately. Even if both have mixed \(I/S\) components, any genuinely new \(7\)-jet coefficient not proportional to the \(5\)-jet profile is enough.

So the real next bottleneck is no longer abstract gauge theory. It is a concrete projection computation:

\[

and then isolating a \(q^{(7)}\)-monomial there.

\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\ \text{mod}\ \mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}.

[A_7^{\mathfrak f}] \in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}.

Use the basis
\[
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\},\qquad
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\},
\]
with
\[
I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},\quad
S=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},\quad
D=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&-1\end{pmatrix},\quad
J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The raw linear \(q^{(7)}\) input first appears in the degree-5 same-point block as a lower-right diagonal perturbation
\[
E_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}
=\frac12 I-\frac12 D.
\]
Since the linearized inverse-square-root map at a diagonal base preserves diagonality, the induced \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of \(X_5\) is also diagonal, hence of the form
\[
X_5^{(q^{(7)})}=u\,I+a\,D
\]
with \(u,a\neq 0\) generically.

The first-order off-diagonal slice is symmetric, so write
\[
X_1^{(\eta_2)}=v\,S.
\]

Now look at the natural order-7 commutator-type channel
\[
X_5 J X_1 - X_1 J X_5.
\]
Using
\[
JS=-D,\qquad SJ=D,\qquad D^2=I,
\]
one gets
\[
(uI+aD)J(vS)- (vS)J(uI+aD)
=
-2v\,(uD+aI).
\]

So the fixed-sector part of this channel is
\[
-2av\,I.
\]

Likewise, the mixed \(X_6/X_0\) channel with an off-diagonal \(X_6\)-slice gives only an \(I\)-component in the fixed sector as well. In other words:

\[
\boxed{
\text{the direct linear }q^{(7)}\text{-driven fixed-sector contribution is }I\text{-directional.}
}
\]

That is the key new fact.

So now the second residual determinant
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7
\]
simplifies: for the direct \(q^{(7)}\) source we have
\[
v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0.
\]
Therefore
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\,v_5.
\]

So the order-7 route works **if and only if** the quintic fixed-sector vector
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}=u_5 I+v_5 S
\]
has a nonzero \(S\)-component.

That is now the exact bottleneck.

So the next attack is no longer broad. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Compute whether }v_5\neq 0.
}
\]

There are two outcomes.

If \(v_5\neq 0\), then the direct \(q^{(7)}\) source already gives
\[
\Delta_7\neq 0,
\]
and the second residual step works.

If \(v_5=0\), then the obvious linear \(q^{(7)}\) source is proportional to \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\), so this particular order-7 route fails, and we would need either

- another order-7 channel that produces an \(S\)-component, or
- to move to the next odd order.

So this is real progress: we have reduced the order-7 question to a single explicit subquestion about the quintic fixed-sector direction.

The next exact computation to do is:
project the known quintic witness \(A_5\) to \(\mathfrak f\) and determine whether it has a nonzero \(S\)-component.

then the order-5 commutator channel is

\[
X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2\alpha v+2\beta u & -\alpha w+\gamma u\\[1mm]
-\alpha w+\gamma u & -2\beta w+2\gamma v
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

This is the full matrix version of the trace formula you already had, because the trace is
\[
2\bigl(v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w)\bigr).
\]

But the important new piece is the **\(S\)-component**. Since the off-diagonal entries are equal, the \(S\)-coefficient is exactly
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]

That is the object we needed.

So the order-7 determinant witness
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7
\]
now simplifies much more concretely:

- the direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven fixed-sector contribution to \(A_7\) is \(I\)-directional, so \(v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0\);
- therefore
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\,(\gamma u-\alpha w).
\]

So the whole order-7 route now reduces to whether
\[
\gamma u-\alpha w
\]
is generically nonzero.

That is a much sharper target than before.

- the \(q^{(5)}\)-witness you already found sits in the diagonal part of \(X_3\), specifically in \(\gamma-\alpha\);
- in the actual one-pair formulas, \(u\) and \(w\) are first-order diagonal entries of \(X_1\), and there is no structural reason for the relation

  to hold identically;

\text{Check whether the diagonal first-order coefficient }u\text{ is generically nonzero.}
}

Because if \(u\not\equiv 0\), then the known \(q^{(5)}\)-driven diagonal asymmetry of \(X_3\) gives
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w \not\equiv 0,
\]
and then the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven \(I\)-component of \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) immediately gives
\[
\Delta_7\not\equiv 0.
\]

So this is real progress:

- its \(S\)-component is explicitly

- the second residual witness at order \(7\) will work as soon as that is generically nonzero.

_Thought for 52s_

Yes. We can push this one more step, and it comes out cleanly.

Let
\[
G_0=\diag(a^2,b^2),\qquad X_0=G_0^{-1/2}=\diag(a^{-1},b^{-1}),
\]
and let the first odd same-point coefficient be
\[
G_1=\begin{pmatrix}h_{11}&h_{12}\\ h_{12}&h_{22}\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Write
\[
X_1=\begin{pmatrix}u&v\\ v&w\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Linearizing \(XGX=I\) gives
\[
X_0G_1X_0+X_1G_0X_0+X_0G_0X_1=0.
\]
Since \(G_0X_0=X_0G_0=\diag(a,b)\), this yields
\[
u=-\frac{h_{11}}{2a^3},\qquad
w=-\frac{h_{22}}{2b^3},\qquad
v=-\frac{h_{12}}{ab(a+b)}.
\]

So the question “is \(u\) generically nonzero?” is exactly the question “is the \((1,1)\)-entry of \(G_1\) generically nonzero?”

From the actual same-point block
\[
G_{m,+}^{\corr}(s)
=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
2q(m+s/2) & \frac12 q'(m+s/2)\\[1mm]
\frac12 q'(m+s/2) & \frac1{12}\bigl(q''(m+s/2)+2q(m+s/2)^3\bigr)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
the coefficient of \(s\) is
\[
G_1=
\frac1\pi
\begin{pmatrix}
q'(m) & \frac14 q''(m)\\[1mm]
\frac14 q''(m) & \frac1{24}\bigl(q'''(m)+6q(m)^2q'(m)\bigr)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Hence
\[
u=-\frac{q'(m)}{2\pi a^3},
\]
\[
w=-\frac{q'''(m)+6q(m)^2q'(m)}{48\pi b^3},
\]
\[
v=-\frac{q''(m)}{4\pi ab(a+b)}.
\]

So \(u\) is generically nonzero unless \(q'(m)\equiv 0\), which would be an absurdly nongeneric degeneracy.

That is already enough for the next step.

Earlier we found the \(S\)-component of the quintic fixed-sector vector:
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]

And we already know the linear \(q^{(5)}\)-source enters the diagonal part of \(X_3\), with the strongest contribution in the lower-right slot. So, to linear order in the \(q^{(5)}\)-slice,
\[
\alpha^{(q^{(5)})}=0,\qquad \gamma^{(q^{(5)})}\neq 0.
\]
Therefore
\[
v_5^{(q^{(5)})}=\gamma^{(q^{(5)})}u.
\]

Since \(u\not\equiv 0\) generically, this gives
\[
v_5\not\equiv 0
\]
generically.

And that is the missing ingredient for the order-7 determinant route, because we already found that the direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven fixed-sector contribution to \(A_7\) is \(I\)-directional:
\[
v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,\qquad u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\neq 0.
\]
So the second residual determinant
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7
\]
has linear \(q^{(7)}\)-part
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\,v_5.
\]

Because \(u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\neq 0\) and \(v_5\not\equiv 0\) generically, this gives
\[
\Delta_7\not\equiv 0
\]
generically.

So this is the real outcome:

- \(u\) is generically nonzero, explicitly \(u=-q'/(2\pi a^3)\);
- therefore the quintic fixed-sector vector has a generically nonzero \(S\)-component;
- therefore the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven \(I\)-component at order \(7\) is not proportional to \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\);
- therefore the second residual order-7 class is generically nonzero.

That is the first genuinely convincing order-7 attack result.

The next thing to do mathematically is to package this as the septic fixed-sector witness:
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7,
\]
with its linear \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient isolated explicitly.

v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\qquad
u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\neq 0.

\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\,v_5.
\]

That means the only thing left is to pin down \(u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\) explicitly and verify it is nonzero with the correct sign/normalization.

The clean way to do that is this.

Let the linear \(q^{(7)}\)-input in the degree-5 same-point block be
\[
G_5^{(q^{(7)})}=d\,E_{22},
\qquad
E_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},
\]
where from the Taylor expansion of \(q''(m+s/2)\),
\[
d=\frac{1}{46080\pi}\,q^{(7)}(h)
\]
up to the sign convention coming from the \(+\) versus \(-\) block. The important point is that \(d\neq 0\) with nonzero coefficient.

Linearizing \(G\mapsto G^{-1/2}\) at
\[
G_0=\diag(a^2,b^2)
\]
gives
\[
X_5^{(q^{(7)})}
=
-\frac{d}{2b^3}E_{22}.
\]
Now decompose
\[
E_{22}=\frac12 I-\frac12 D,
\]
so the fixed-sector part is
\[
\bigl(X_5^{(q^{(7)})}\bigr)^{\mathfrak f}
=
-\frac{d}{4b^3}I.
\]

Then, from the order-7 commutator channel with
\[
X_1^{(\eta_2)}=vS,
\]
the induced fixed-sector contribution is \(I\)-directional with coefficient proportional to
\[
\frac{d\,v}{b^3}.
\]
Using the earlier explicit computation, this coefficient is nonzero; more precisely it is proportional to
\[
-\frac{d\,v}{b^3},
\]
with \(v=-h_{12}/(ab(a+b))\). So as long as the relevant first-order off-diagonal slice is genuinely present, we get
\[
u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\neq 0.
\]

So the order-7 witness reduces to

\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}
\propto
q^{(7)}(h)\,(\gamma u-\alpha w).
\]

And since the \(q^{(5)}\)-driven part of \(v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w\) is already generically nonzero, this gives the desired generic nonvanishing:
\[
\Delta_7\not\equiv 0.
\]

So the real next theorem is now very tight:

> The linear \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of \(\Delta_7\) is nonzero.

At this point I would formulate the proof as a triangular jet argument:

1. \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) first sees the \(5\)-jet and has nonzero \(S\)-component generically.
2. \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) first sees the \(7\)-jet and its direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-source is \(I\)-directional with nonzero coefficient.
3. The septic ambiguity only adds multiples of \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\).
4. Therefore \([A_7^{\mathfrak f}]\in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) is generically nonzero.

That is now a genuine second residual invariant, and it is the right input to go back to the two-pair/minimal-core machinery.
