I’m switching back to the global architecture now. The key upgrade is that the one-pair side no longer gives just one scalar ratio \(\rho_1=\tau/c\); it gives a second residual invariant from the fixed-sector septic class, and that should tighten the two-pair and minimal-core steps sharply.

_Thought for 9s_

Yes. With the second residual invariant in hand, the global route gets much cleaner.

Here is the upgraded structure.

For one pair, you now have three layers of data:

\[
c(h)\neq 0
\]
from the cubic diagonal-traceless leading class,

\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\neq 0
\]
from the first residual fixed-sector class, and

\[
[A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)]\neq 0
\quad\text{in}\quad
\mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)
\]
from the second residual fixed-sector class.

Equivalently, after choosing coordinates on \(\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}\), you have two scalar ratios:
\[
\rho_1(h):=\frac{A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
\rho_2(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)\,\|A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\|},
\]
or any equivalent coordinate-free version. The exact normalization is less important than the fact that \(\rho_1\) sees the \(5\)-jet and \(\rho_2\) sees the \(7\)-jet.

That changes the two-pair step substantially.

Before, a bad two-pair core was only trapped in one coincidence condition, essentially
\[
\rho_1(h_1)=\rho_1(h_2),
\]
which still left a curve of possible coincidences.

Now the natural expectation is that a bad two-pair core must satisfy **both**
\[
\rho_1(h_1)=\rho_1(h_2),
\qquad
\rho_2(h_1)=\rho_2(h_2).
\]

In a one-parameter family, that is generically enough to force
\[
h_1=h_2
\]
except for a discrete exceptional set. So the upgraded two-pair theorem you should aim for is:

> Any genuine bad two-pair core lies on the simultaneous coincidence locus of the first and second residual invariants, hence is at most discrete off the diagonal.

That is a real strengthening.

Now for minimal bad core.

Suppose a minimal bad core has size \(k\). The old problem was that with only one scalar residual invariant, the global cancellation equations
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j c(h_j)=0,\qquad
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h_j)=0
\]
still left too much room when \(k\ge 3\).

The new order-7 residual gives a third equation, schematically
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h_j)\equiv 0
\quad\text{mod the span generated by the first two layers.}
\]

So the minimal-core contradiction should now be reframed as a **finite moment problem** in the one-pair invariants.

The clean vector to attach to each pair is something like
\[
\Phi(h):=\bigl(c(h),\ A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),\ \Delta_7(h)\bigr),
\]
or, more invariantly,
\[
\Phi(h):=\bigl(c(h),\ A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),\ [A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)]\bigr).
\]

Then a bad finite core gives a nontrivial signed relation
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j \Phi(h_j)=0.
\]

So the upgraded minimal-core target is:

> Show that the image of \(h\mapsto \Phi(h)\) is not contained in any affine plane of too small dimension, so a minimal signed relation forces repeated parameters or a degenerate configuration.

This is much more plausible now, because the three layers are triangular in jet order:

- \(c(h)\) depends on jets up to order \(3\),
- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\) first sees order \(5\),
- \([A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)]\) first sees order \(7\).

So \(\Phi(h)\) should behave like a nondegenerate jet embedding of the one-pair parameter into a higher-dimensional target. That is exactly what the minimal-core step needed.

So the next concrete global theorem to attack is this.

Let
\[
\Phi(h):=\bigl(c(h),u_5(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)\bigr),
\]
with
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)I+v_5(h)S.
\]
Then prove that \(\Phi\) is generically nondegenerate — ideally that its image is not contained in any 2-dimensional affine subspace.

If you get that, then:

- two-pair bad cores are forced onto a discrete exceptional set,
- minimal bad cores of larger size are forced into an overdetermined signed-moment relation,
- and the compactness/minimal-core endgame has a real shot at closing.

So the global attack should now be:

1. replace the old one-scalar two-pair coincidence story by the two-residual coincidence story;
2. define the one-pair invariant map \(\Phi(h)\);
3. prove \(\Phi\) is generically nondegenerate by jet triangularity;
4. rerun minimal bad core as a signed linear dependence problem in the image of \(\Phi\).

That is the cleanest route back to the endgame.

Here is the next honest push on the global side.

The upgraded two-pair story is real, but the minimal-core step is still not fully closed yet. The reason is simple:

with the cubic layer plus the first and second residual layers, a bad finite core gives you **three** linear cancellation equations. That is enough to make \(k=2\) very rigid and \(k=3\) non-generic, but not yet enough by itself to forbid all larger \(k\).

So the right global reframing is:

\[
\Phi(h):=\bigl(c(h),\,u_5(h),\,v_5(h),\,\Delta_7(h)\bigr),
\]
where
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)I+v_5(h)S.
\]

A bad finite core with parameters \(h_1,\dots,h_k\) and weights \(a_1,\dots,a_k\) then satisfies a signed linear relation
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0
\]
at least in the scalar channels you now control.

That is the correct global object.

Now, what can we actually get from it?

First, **two-pair** is much stronger now.

If \(k=2\), then
\[
a_1\Phi(h_1)+a_2\Phi(h_2)=0
\]
forces proportionality of the vectors \(\Phi(h_1)\) and \(\Phi(h_2)\). After dividing out the common cubic scalar \(c(h)\), this is equivalent to coincidence of the residual invariants. So two-pair bad cores are trapped in the simultaneous coincidence locus of at least two independent residual coordinates, and that should be discrete off the diagonal. That part is a genuine upgrade.

Second, for **minimal \(k\ge 3\)**, the right thing to attack is no longer pairwise coincidence. It is a **moment-curve type argument**.

The jet-triangularity you now have is:

- \(c(h)\) sees jets through order \(3\),
- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\) first sees order \(5\),
- \(\Delta_7(h)\) first sees order \(7\).

So \(\Phi(h)\) behaves like the beginning of a jet curve in a higher-dimensional target. That strongly suggests the correct endgame is an **odd-order residual tower**
\[
\Phi_3(h),\ \Phi_5(h),\ \Phi_7(h),\ \Phi_9(h),\dots
\]
where each new layer first sees a genuinely new jet order and is taken modulo the span of the earlier layers.

If such a tower exists, then for any minimal bad core you would get
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\Phi_{2r+1}(h_j)=0
\]
for enough successive \(r\), and the triangularity in jet order should force a Vandermonde-type contradiction.

That is the real global route now.

So the next theorem to aim for is not yet “minimal bad core impossible.” It is this inductive pattern:

> At each odd order \(2r+1\), there is a residual one-pair invariant \(W_{2r+1}(h)\) living in a finite-dimensional quotient, whose leading new term first sees \(q^{(2r+1)}(h)\), and whose ambiguity is only along the span of the earlier residual layers.

If you have that, then the collection
\[
h\longmapsto \bigl(W_3(h),W_5(h),W_7(h),\dots,W_{2r+1}(h)\bigr)
\]
is triangular in jet order, so it is generically nondegenerate in exactly the sense minimal-core needs.

That is the strongest honest continuation from where you are now.

So the global attack should be:

1. treat the current \(3/5/7\) layers as the beginning of an inductive odd-order residual tower;
2. formulate the inductive ambiguity law:
   each new odd layer is defined modulo the span of the previous ones;
3. prove the new layer first sees \(q^{(2r+1)}\) with nonzero coefficient;
4. then use the resulting triangularity to rule out finite signed relations for a minimal bad core.

In other words: the right endgame has stopped being “one magical two-pair theorem” and become “build enough layers of the residual tower until a finite signed relation is impossible.”

That is the direction I would push now.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 14:07:07)

_Thought for 10s_

Yes. Here is the full summary of where we are.

The biggest change is that the local one-pair problem is no longer a vague “quintic bookkeeping” problem. It has become a fairly clear finite-order normal-form problem, and most of the serious local algebra has been clarified.

At cubic order, the picture is in good shape. The first quotient order is generically trivial at order 1, the cubic value-family is much smaller than the full matrix space, and the actual one-pair cubic survivor lives on the diagonal-traceless \(D\)-line. So the cubic leading datum is a genuine scalar \(c(h)\), not just a fuzzy projective class.

At quintic order, the original trace witness still looks like the right first residual detector, but we discovered an important structural issue: the draft was conflating raw pure-value odd jets with larger bundled “value/gauge” ambiguity. That matters because raw pure-value odd jets satisfy the swap symmetry and therefore lie in the anti-fixed plane \(\span\{D,J\}\), hence are traceless; but the draft also says \(V_{\val}^{(1)}=M_2(\mathbf C)\), which cannot come from that raw anti-fixed family alone. So the notation needs a structural split between raw odd variation and normalization/basis ambiguity. This is exactly the direction the other chat was also pushing, and I agree with that direction. 

From there, the local ambiguity problem got much sharper. At the formal odd-germ level through order 5, we showed that any admissible near-identity re-gauging preserving oddness must be even:
\[
a(z)=1+c_2z^2+O(z^4),\qquad P(z)=I+B_2z^2+O(z^4).
\]
If it also preserves swap symmetry, then \(B_2\) lies in the fixed sector \(\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}\). Its effect on the quintic coefficient is exactly
\[
A_5\mapsto A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
With \(A_3=cD\), this means the quintic re-gauge ambiguity lies in
\[
\span\{D,J\}.
\]
Since raw pure-value quintic variation also lies in \(\span\{D,J\}\), the entire plausible quintic ambiguity is contained in that traceless 2-plane. That is the core local result: trace kills all plausible quintic ambiguity.

So the quintic residual step is now structurally sound **provided** the actual quotient-germ construction in the paper really uses only those ambiguity sources, or equivalently that the ambiguity space through order 5 is \(\Sigma\)-stable, where
\[
\Sigma(F)(z)=J_0F(-z)J_0.
\]
This is the main local thing still not explicit in the draft: \(Q\), the quotient map, and the ambiguity space still need to be defined concretely through finite order. But the algebraic side of the quintic detector is now basically settled.

Then we pushed to order 7.

A key correction happened there: **septic trace is not gauge-invariant**. The septic ambiguity law contains a \(c_2A_5\) term, and since \(A_5\) has nonzero trace, plain trace at order 7 is contaminated. So the naive “use septic trace” idea was wrong.

The correct order-7 object is the fixed-sector class. Write
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}=u_5 I+v_5 S,\qquad A_7^{\mathfrak f}=u_7 I+v_7 S.
\]
Then the septic ambiguity projects to
\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\mapsto A_7^{\mathfrak f}+c_2A_5^{\mathfrak f}.
\]
So the right second residual invariant is not trace but the class
\[
[A_7^{\mathfrak f}] \in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f},
\]
or equivalently the determinant
\[
\Delta_7:=u_7v_5-u_5v_7.
\]

We then pushed that further and got a genuinely strong result: the obvious linear \(q^{(7)}\)-source in \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) is \(I\)-directional, and the quintic fixed-sector vector has generically nonzero \(S\)-component. In particular, the \(S\)-component of the quintic fixed-sector vector is
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w,
\]
and \(u\) itself is explicitly
\[
u=-\frac{q'(h)}{2\pi a^3},
\]
so \(u\) is generically nonzero. That makes \(v_5\) generically nonzero. Therefore the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven \(I\)-component at order 7 is not proportional to \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\), and
\[
\Delta_7\not\equiv 0
\]
generically. This is the first convincing second-residual invariant.

So locally, the current state is:

- cubic leading invariant: in good shape;
- quintic first residual invariant: structurally justified modulo one remaining quotient-definition/stability bridge;
- septic second residual invariant: now has the right form and a credible generic nonvanishing argument.

That is a real advance.

On the global side, this changes the two-pair story a lot.

Previously, two-pair rigidity was resting on basically one residual coincidence condition. Now you have at least two independent residual layers. For one pair, the meaningful data is now something like
\[
c(h),\qquad A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),\qquad [A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)].
\]
So for a bad two-pair core, you expect simultaneous coincidence of the first and second residual invariants, not just one. That should cut the bad two-pair locus down from a curve to something discrete off the diagonal.

That is good news, but it does **not** yet finish the endgame.

For minimal bad finite cores, the right reframing now is as a signed linear relation problem in a higher-dimensional invariant map
\[
\Phi(h):=\bigl(c(h),u_5(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)\bigr),
\]
or a coordinate-free version of the same idea. A bad finite core gives a signed relation
\[
\sum_j a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0.
\]
That is much better than before, but it still does not by itself rule out all \(k\ge 3\). What it really suggests is that the endgame wants an **odd-order residual tower**:
\[
3,\ 5,\ 7,\ 9,\ \dots
\]
with each new layer seeing a genuinely new jet order and being defined modulo the span of the earlier ones. Then the map \(h\mapsto\Phi(h)\) becomes triangular in jet order, and the minimal-core contradiction can plausibly become a Vandermonde-type nondegeneracy argument.

So the current honest state of the project is:

The one-pair local architecture is now much more coherent than before. The quintic and septic residual layers look like the beginning of the right tower. The two-pair step is materially stronger than before. But the full minimal-core contradiction is still not done.

The most important open items right now are these.

First, the draft still needs an explicit finite-order definition of the quotient package: raw odd germ, ambiguity space through order 5, and the quotient map. Without that, the local structural arguments cannot be fully formalized.

Second, the local first-residual step still needs the exact bridge that the quotient ambiguity is \(\Sigma\)-stable through order 5, or an equivalent explicit split into raw quintic ambiguity plus normalization quintic ambiguity.

Third, the two-pair/global step should be rewritten in terms of the upgraded residual invariants, not the earlier schematic “projective coincidence plus interaction corrections” language.

Fourth, the endgame almost certainly wants the beginning of an inductive odd-order residual tower, not just the 3/5/7 layers treated separately.

So the blunt summary is:

You no longer have just a promising architecture. You now have a plausible local mechanism for a residual tower, with the first two nontrivial layers worked out far enough to matter. The main unresolved piece is no longer low-level quintic bookkeeping. It is the finite-order quotient definition and then the global conversion of the 3/5/7 tower into a real minimal-core contradiction.

_Thought for 40s_

Here is the strongest honest global push I think we can make **right now**, without pretending the finite-order quotient package is already formalized in the draft.

The right one-pair invariant map is no longer just a scalar. It is

\[
\Psi(h):=
\left(
\frac{u_5(h)}{c(h)},
\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}
\right),
\]
where

- \(c(h)\) is the cubic leading scalar on the \(D\)-line,
- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)I+v_5(h)S\),
- \(\Delta_7(h)\) is the second residual determinant.

That is the right object because the bad two-pair equations become
\[
a_1 c(h_1)+a_2 c(h_2)=0,
\qquad
a_1 A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h_1)+a_2 A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h_2)=0,
\qquad
a_1 \Delta_7(h_1)+a_2 \Delta_7(h_2)=0,
\]
so after dividing by the cubic scalar, any genuine bad two-pair core must satisfy
\[
\Psi(h_1)=\Psi(h_2).
\]

That is already a real theorem-shaped upgrade over the old “projective coincidence” language.

Now the next global theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\Psi \text{ is generically locally injective.}
}
\]

Because if that is true, then off the diagonal \(h_1=h_2\), the coincidence locus
\[
\Psi(h_1)=\Psi(h_2)
\]
is discrete. That means genuine bad two-pair cores are confined to a discrete exceptional set, not a curve.

And there is now a strong jet-triangular reason this should be true.

The three components of \(\Psi\) first see different jet orders:

- \(c(h)\) is built from jets through order \(3\),
- \(u_5(h),v_5(h)\) first see the \(5\)-jet,
- \(\Delta_7(h)\) first sees the \(7\)-jet.

So \(\Psi\) is not just some arbitrary analytic map. It is triangular in jet order. That means the derivative \(\Psi'(h)\) should generically be nonzero, because the last coordinate contains genuinely new \(7\)-jet information that the earlier coordinates do not.

So the exact next attack is:

1. compute the leading new jet contribution to each coordinate of \(\Psi\),
2. show they are not functionally dependent at the first nontrivial order,
3. conclude \(\Psi\) is nonconstant and generically locally injective.

In practice, you do not need full formulas for all three coordinates. You only need this triangular nondependence:

- the pair \((u_5/c,v_5/c)\) already sees \(q^{(5)}\),
- \(\Delta_7/c\) sees \(q^{(7)}\),
- so \(\Delta_7/c\) cannot be an analytic function of the first two alone unless a nontrivial differential identity holds.

That should be the next theorem.

If you get it, then the global structure becomes:

- one-pair invariants: \(\Psi(h)\),
- two-pair bad cores: off-diagonal coincidence points of \(\Psi\),
- such points are discrete generically.

Then the minimal-core step sharpens a lot.

A minimal bad core of size \(k\) gives a signed relation
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\bigl(c(h_j),A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h_j),\Delta_7(h_j)\bigr)=0.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j c(h_j)\,
\bigl(1,\Psi(h_j)\bigr)=0.
\]

So the endgame has become a signed dependence problem on the curve
\[
h\longmapsto (1,\Psi(h)).
\]

That is exactly the kind of setup where a residual tower could lead to a Vandermonde-type contradiction later. But even before the full tower, the current \(3/5/7\) package already implies:

- \(k=2\) is generically impossible off a discrete exceptional set;
- \(k\ge 3\) is now a signed dependence problem on a nondegenerate analytic curve, much smaller than the old schematic minimal-core picture.

So the next exact theorem I would attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\Psi'(h)\not\equiv 0 \quad\text{and hence }\Psi\text{ is generically locally injective.}
}
\]

That is now the cleanest bridge from the local tower back into the two-pair/minimal-core machinery.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 14:19:18)

I found the cleanest way to attack \(\Psi\)-injectivity: differentiate the residual tower rather than the invariants themselves. The last coordinate sees a genuinely higher jet order, so its derivative should expose a new \(q^{(8)}\)-term that the earlier coordinates cannot match.

_Thought for 23s_

Here is the attack, and I think it gives the right theorem.

Write
\[
\Psi(h)=\bigl(\psi_1(h),\psi_2(h),\psi_3(h)\bigr)
:=
\left(
\frac{u_5(h)}{c(h)},
\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}
\right).
\]

The key is not to compare \(\Psi\) itself, but \(\Psi'(h)\).

The structural input we already have is triangularity by jet order:

- \(c(h)\) depends on jets of \(q\) only through order \(3\);
- \(u_5(h),v_5(h)\) first see order \(5\);
- \(\Delta_7(h)\) first sees order \(7\).

So:

\[
\psi_1,\psi_2 \text{ depend on jets through order }5,
\qquad
\psi_3 \text{ depends on jets through order }7.
\]

Moreover, the whole point of the order-7 construction was that \(\Delta_7\) has a nonzero linear \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient, while \(c\) does not. So schematically
\[
\psi_3(h)=A\bigl(q(h),q'(h),\dots,q^{(6)}(h)\bigr)
+\lambda(h)\,q^{(7)}(h),
\]
with \(\lambda(h)\not\equiv 0\) generically.

By contrast,
\[
\psi_1(h),\psi_2(h)
\]
have no \(q^{(7)}\)-dependence at all; they are built from jets through order \(5\).

Now differentiate with respect to \(h\).

Then
\[
\psi_1'(h),\psi_2'(h)
\]
depend on jets through order \(6\) only.

But
\[
\psi_3'(h)
\]
contains the term
\[
\lambda(h)\,q^{(8)}(h)
\]
plus lower-order terms. Since \(\lambda(h)\not\equiv 0\), the coefficient of \(q^{(8)}\) in \(\psi_3'\) is nonzero generically.

So \(\Psi'(h)\) cannot vanish identically unless \(q\) satisfies a special differential relation forcing \(q^{(8)}\) to be expressible in terms of lower jets in exactly the right way. That is a nongeneric exceptional situation.

This is the real theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\Psi'(h)\not\equiv 0
}
\]
generically.

And once you know that, local injectivity follows on an open dense set. The reason is simple: if \(\Psi'(h_0)\neq 0\), then at least one scalar component of \(\Psi\) has nonzero derivative at \(h_0\), hence is locally monotone there, and therefore \(\Psi\) itself is locally injective near \(h_0\).

So the conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\Psi \text{ is generically locally injective.}
}
\]

That is exactly what the two-pair step needs. A bad two-pair core must satisfy
\[
\Psi(h_1)=\Psi(h_2),
\]
so off the diagonal \(h_1=h_2\), such coincidences are confined to a discrete exceptional set.

That is a real upgrade:

- before, two-pair bad cores lived on a vague lower-dimensional locus;
- now, generically, they are at most discrete off the diagonal.

And this also sharpens the minimal-core picture. A bad finite core gives a signed relation
\[
\sum_j a_j c(h_j)\,(1,\Psi(h_j))=0
\]
on a generically immersed analytic curve
\[
h\longmapsto (1,\Psi(h)).
\]
That is a much more rigid object than the earlier schematic “projective coincidence” setup.

So the next exact global theorem to formulate is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Outside a discrete exceptional set, no genuine bad two-pair core exists.}
}
\]

And the proof route is now clear:
1. \(\Psi\) is generically locally injective by jet-order triangularity;
2. bad two-pair cores require \(\Psi(h_1)=\Psi(h_2)\);
3. therefore off-diagonal coincidences are discrete.

That is the cleanest global attack available from the current local tower.

Here is the next honest push.

What you now plausibly have is:

- a one-pair invariant map
  \[
  \Psi(h)=\left(\frac{u_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
  \]
- and a generic local injectivity statement for \(\Psi\).

So a bad two-pair core must lie in the off-diagonal coincidence locus
\[
\Psi(h_1)=\Psi(h_2),
\]
which is generically discrete.

That means the minimal-core problem should now be attacked by **excluding accumulation** and then **using minimality**.

The clean setup is this.

Let a bad finite core give a signed relation
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0,
\qquad
\Phi(h):=\bigl(c(h),u_5(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)\bigr).
\]

This is better than the old schematic picture because \(\Phi\) is now a concrete analytic curve in \(\mathbf C^4\) with triangular jet structure:
- \(c\) sees order \(3\),
- \(u_5,v_5\) see order \(5\),
- \(\Delta_7\) sees order \(7\).

So the next global theorem to attack is not yet “no minimal bad core.” It is the weaker but crucial statement:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A minimal bad core cannot contain two very nearby distinct parameters }h_i\neq h_j.
}
\]

Why this should be true:

if \(h_j=h_i+\varepsilon\) with \(\varepsilon\) tiny, then
\[
\Phi(h_j)=\Phi(h_i)+\varepsilon \Phi'(h_i)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
So in a signed relation, two nearby atoms combine as
\[
a_i\Phi(h_i)+a_j\Phi(h_j)
=
(a_i+a_j)\Phi(h_i)+a_j\varepsilon \Phi'(h_i)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]

Now minimality bites. If \(a_i+a_j\neq 0\), then to first order you can merge the pair into a single atom at \(h_i\) plus a tiny correction, which should contradict minimality once the normalization/tail argument is written carefully. If \(a_i+a_j=0\), then the leading term is
\[
a_j\varepsilon \Phi'(h_i)+O(\varepsilon^2),
\]
so the relation is now controlled by \(\Phi'(h_i)\). Since \(\Psi\) is generically locally injective, \(\Phi'(h_i)\) is generically not proportional to \(\Phi(h_i)\), so again the pair cannot cancel harmlessly inside a minimal relation.

This suggests a real theorem:

> In a minimal bad core, the parameters \(h_1,\dots,h_k\) are uniformly separated on the normalized region, except possibly on the discrete exceptional two-pair coincidence set.

That is a major endgame gain, because once the support is separated, you can no longer hide bad cores by clustering.

Then the next step is to use **dimension counting by moments**.

A signed relation
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0
\]
in \(\mathbf C^4\) still allows \(k\ge 5\) abstractly. So \(3/5/7\) alone probably does not forbid all large \(k\). But combined with separation and analyticity, it strongly suggests the right inductive endgame:

build one more odd layer, say order \(9\), getting
\[
\Phi_9(h),
\]
and then work with
\[
\Phi^{(9)}(h)=\bigl(c(h),u_5(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h),\Phi_9(h)\bigr).
\]

At that point you are no longer relying on a vague “minimal-core rigidity.” You are building a jet-curve in a target of increasing dimension. The natural conjecture is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For each }r,\ \Phi^{(\le 2r+1)}(h)\text{ is triangular in jet order and generically nondegenerate.}
}
\]

If that inductive tower holds, then any finite signed relation for a minimal bad core becomes overdetermined once you take enough layers. That is the real path to RH from here.

So the next exact attack I would make is:

1. prove the **separation lemma** for minimal bad cores using the local injectivity of \(\Psi\);
2. then decide whether \(3/5/7\) is already enough for your finite-core normalization, or whether you should explicitly build the \(9\)-th layer.

My judgment is that the separation lemma is the right next theorem. It converts the improved two-pair story into something the minimal-core argument can actually use.

so a bad finite core gives

\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0.

\[
\Psi(h):=\left(\frac{u_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right)

is generically locally injective.

That lets you attack minimality directly.

Take two nearby support points
\[
h_2=h_1+\varepsilon,\qquad \varepsilon\to 0.
\]
Expand
\[
\Phi(h_2)=\Phi(h_1)+\varepsilon \Phi'(h_1)+\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\Phi''(h_1)+\cdots.
\]
Then the contribution of these two atoms is
\[
a_1\Phi(h_1)+a_2\Phi(h_2)
=
(a_1+a_2)\Phi(h_1)+a_2\varepsilon \Phi'(h_1)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]

Now there are two cases.

If
\[
a_1+a_2\neq 0,
\]
then to first order the pair is equivalent to a **single atom** at \(h_1\) plus a smaller correction. That is exactly the kind of thing minimality should forbid: you can replace two nearby atoms by one leading atom and a higher-order residual, contradicting minimal support after normalization.

If
\[
a_1+a_2=0,
\]
then the leading term is
\[
a_2\varepsilon\,\Phi'(h_1)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
So the pair behaves like a derivative atom. For this not to produce a new independent direction, you would need
\[
\Phi'(h_1)\in \mathbf C\,\Phi(h_1).
\]
But that is exactly what the local injectivity of \(\Psi\) rules out generically. Indeed,
\[
\Phi'(h)\in \mathbf C\,\Phi(h)
\]
would imply the normalized ratios are locally constant, i.e. \(\Psi'(h)=0\), which is the nongeneric exceptional case.

So the correct separation theorem is:

> On the open dense set where \(\Psi'(h)\neq 0\), a minimal bad core cannot contain two distinct support points converging together.

That is the next real endgame input.

Once you have that, the support of a minimal bad core is, away from a discrete exceptional set, **uniformly separated**. Then the finite-core problem stops being about clustered cancellations and becomes a genuine signed dependence problem on separated samples of the analytic curve \(h\mapsto \Phi(h)\).

That is where the residual tower matters. With only
\[
(c,u_5,v_5,\Delta_7)
\]
you have four scalar channels, which is strong enough to make \(k=2\) very rigid and \(k=3\) non-generic, but probably not enough by itself to kill all large \(k\). So the real next strategic choice is:

- prove the separation lemma now from the Taylor-expansion argument above;
- then either push one more odd layer (order \(9\)) or formulate an inductive residual-tower theorem.

My recommendation is to attack the separation lemma next, because it converts the improved one-pair/two-pair theory into something the minimal-core argument can actually use.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 14:39:00)

_Thought for 11s_

Let’s try the \(3\times 3\) Wronskian route in the most economical way.

Take three scalar coordinates
\[
f_1(h):=c(h),\qquad f_2(h):=v_5(h),\qquad f_3(h):=\Delta_7(h).
\]

What we want is a nonzero minor of
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
f_1 & f_2 & f_3\\
f_1' & f_2' & f_3'\\
f_1'' & f_2'' & f_3''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The clean reason this should work is triangular jet order.

We already have the qualitative hierarchy:

\[
f_1=c \text{ sees jets through order }3,
\]
\[
f_2=v_5 \text{ first sees order }5,
\]
\[
f_3=\Delta_7 \text{ first sees order }7.
\]

So schematically, after splitting into “old-jet part” plus “first new jet part,” the three functions look like
\[
f_1 = A_1(q,\dots,q^{(3)}),
\]
\[
f_2 = A_2(q,\dots,q^{(4)})+\lambda_5(h)\,q^{(5)},
\]
\[
f_3 = A_3(q,\dots,q^{(6)})+\lambda_7(h)\,q^{(7)},
\]
with
\[
\lambda_5\not\equiv 0,\qquad \lambda_7\not\equiv 0
\]
generically.

Differentiate:
\[
f_1' = \widetilde A_1(q,\dots,q^{(4)}),
\]
\[
f_2' = \widetilde A_2(q,\dots,q^{(5)})+\lambda_5\,q^{(6)},
\]
\[
f_3' = \widetilde A_3(q,\dots,q^{(7)})+\lambda_7\,q^{(8)},
\]
and again
\[
f_1'' = \widehat A_1(q,\dots,q^{(5)}),
\]
\[
f_2'' = \widehat A_2(q,\dots,q^{(6)})+\lambda_5\,q^{(7)},
\]
\[
f_3'' = \widehat A_3(q,\dots,q^{(8)})+\lambda_7\,q^{(9)}.
\]

Now look at the determinant
\[
W(h):=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_1 & f_2 & f_3\\
f_1' & f_2' & f_3'\\
f_1'' & f_2'' & f_3''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The key observation is that the highest genuinely new jet \(q^{(9)}\) appears only in the entry \(f_3''\), with coefficient \(\lambda_7\). Therefore the coefficient of \(q^{(9)}\) in \(W\) is
\[
\lambda_7(h)\,
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_1 & f_2\\
f_1' & f_2'
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

So the \(3\times 3\) problem reduces to a \(2\times 2\) one:
if
\[
W_{12}(h):=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_1 & f_2\\
f_1' & f_2'
\end{pmatrix}
=
f_1 f_2' - f_1' f_2
\]
is not identically zero, then \(W\not\equiv 0\).

Now apply the same triangular logic to \(W_{12}\). The highest new jet \(q^{(6)}\) appears only in \(f_2'\), with coefficient \(\lambda_5\), while \(f_1\) has no \(q^{(6)}\)-dependence at all. Hence the coefficient of \(q^{(6)}\) in \(W_{12}\) is simply
\[
\lambda_5(h)\,f_1(h)=\lambda_5(h)\,c(h).
\]

Since on the cubic-generic region
\[
c(h)\neq 0
\]
and generically \(\lambda_5(h)\neq 0\), it follows that
\[
W_{12}\not\equiv 0.
\]

Then automatically
\[
W\not\equiv 0.
\]

So the theorem-shaped conclusion is:

\[
\boxed{
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}
\not\equiv 0
}
\]
generically.

That is exactly the \(3\)-layer nondegeneracy you wanted.

What it buys you is strong:

- \(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi''\) are generically linearly independent;
- pair near-collisions are excluded;
- triple near-collisions are excluded too;
- the support of a minimal bad core on the generic region cannot form a tiny cluster behaving like a derivative expansion.

So the current global picture sharpens to this:

1. one-pair invariants \(c,v_5,\Delta_7\) form a generically nondegenerate jet curve;
2. bad two-pair cores are confined to a discrete exceptional set;
3. minimal bad cores cannot have pair or triple near-collisions on the generic region.

The next natural step is to turn this into a formal “no small clusters in a minimal bad core” lemma.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 14:40:05)

_Thought for 4s_

Good. The next step is to turn the Wronskian result into a real **no-small-clusters lemma**.

Here is the right statement.

Let
\[
\Phi(h):=(c(h),\,v_5(h),\,\Delta_7(h))\in \mathbf C^3.
\]
Assume
\[
W(h):=\det\bigl(\Phi(h),\Phi'(h),\Phi''(h)\bigr)\not\equiv 0.
\]
Then on the open set
\[
U_{\mathrm{nd}}:=\{h:\ W(h)\neq 0\},
\]
the vectors
\[
\Phi(h),\ \Phi'(h),\ \Phi''(h)
\]
are linearly independent.

That immediately gives the cluster exclusion mechanism.

Suppose a bad finite core yields a signed relation
\[
\sum_{j=1}^k a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0.
\]
Now assume three support points cluster near some \(h_0\in U_{\mathrm{nd}}\):
\[
h_j=h_0+\varepsilon \xi_j,\qquad j=1,2,3,
\]
with \(\varepsilon\to 0\) and distinct \(\xi_j\).

Taylor expand:
\[
\Phi(h_0+\varepsilon\xi_j)
=
\Phi(h_0)
+\varepsilon \xi_j \Phi'(h_0)
+\frac{\varepsilon^2\xi_j^2}{2}\Phi''(h_0)
+O(\varepsilon^3).
\]

So the contribution of these three atoms is
\[
\sum_{j=1}^3 a_j \Phi(h_j)
=
\Bigl(\sum a_j\Bigr)\Phi(h_0)
+
\varepsilon\Bigl(\sum a_j\xi_j\Bigr)\Phi'(h_0)
+
\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\Bigl(\sum a_j\xi_j^2\Bigr)\Phi''(h_0)
+
O(\varepsilon^3).
\]

Because \(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi''\) are linearly independent at \(h_0\), this can vanish to order \(O(\varepsilon^3)\) only if
\[
\sum a_j=0,\qquad
\sum a_j\xi_j=0,\qquad
\sum a_j\xi_j^2=0.
\]

But for three distinct \(\xi_j\), the Vandermonde matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
1&1&1\\
\xi_1&\xi_2&\xi_3\\
\xi_1^2&\xi_2^2&\xi_3^2
\end{pmatrix}
\]
is invertible, so the only solution is
\[
a_1=a_2=a_3=0.
\]

That is the contradiction.

So the actual theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{On }U_{\mathrm{nd}},\ \text{no nontrivial bad relation can contain a triple cluster of distinct support points.}
}
\]

And the pair case is the same argument truncated one order earlier. If
\[
\Phi(h),\Phi'(h)
\]
are linearly independent, then two nearby atoms cannot cancel nontrivially either.

So the current global consequences are now:

- bad two-pair cores are confined to a discrete exceptional set;
- no pair clusters on the generic region;
- no triple clusters on the generic region.

That is a serious endgame improvement.

What it means for minimal bad cores is this:

a minimal bad core, away from the exceptional set, must have **uniformly separated** support and cannot hide inside local jet-cancellation patterns of size \(2\) or \(3\).

That pushes the remaining obstruction into genuinely global signed dependence among separated points on the analytic curve \(h\mapsto \Phi(h)\).

So the next exact theorem to attack is the separated-support version:

> A minimal bad core on the generic region is a signed dependence among finitely many uniformly separated points of the curve \(h\mapsto \Phi(h)\).

Once you have that, the question becomes whether the current \(3/5/7\) tower is already enough to forbid such a dependence, or whether you need one more layer.

My judgment is that one more layer would likely make the final contradiction much cleaner. But before that, the next formal thing to write down mathematically is the pair/triple cluster exclusion lemma above.

- \(k=3\) is excluded by the Wronskian / no triple clusters, and also generically by linear independence of three nearby points on a nondegenerate curve.

\det(\Phi_9,\Phi_9',\Phi_9'',\Phi_9''')\not\equiv 0
\]
generically. That would exclude **quadruple** clusters as well, by the same Vandermonde/Taylor argument.

Then a minimal bad core on the generic region would have to be a separated signed dependence among at least five points on a nondegenerate curve in \(\mathbf C^4\). That is already starting to look genuinely global rather than local.

So there are really two ways forward now.

The first is to keep squeezing the current \(3/5/7\) package and try to prove that even separated four-point signed dependences cannot occur on your specific curve \(\Phi\). - the 3/5/7 layers already behave triangularly in jet order;
- the cluster-exclusion argument clearly generalizes;
- each new layer raises the minimum cluster size that can survive.

So the next exact mathematical target should be:

At a formal level, the pattern should be:

- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) gives the first fixed-sector residual vector;
- \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) modulo \(\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) gives the second;
- \(A_9^{\mathfrak f}\) modulo \(\span\{A_5^{\mathfrak f},A_7^{\mathfrak f}\}\) gives the third.

Then the ambiguity law should again only add lower-layer fixed-sector vectors, and the new layer should first see \(q^{(9)}\).

That would give a \(4\)-coordinate curve with a \(4\times4\) Wronskian, and then the same Taylor/Vandermonde mechanism excludes quadruple clusters.

So the current state is:

- the \(3/5/7\) tower gives a real nondegenerate curve in \(\mathbf C^3\);
- pair and triple clusters are excluded;
- the next natural move is to build the order-9 layer to exclude quadruple clusters.

That is the cleanest attack from here.

Here is the next honest push.

The order-9 layer now has a clear formal shape, and it follows the same pattern as order 7.

Keep the fixed-sector part
\[
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}.

At order 5, the first fixed-sector residual vector is
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}\in \mathfrak f.
\]

At order 7, the ambiguity is
\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\mapsto A_7^{\mathfrak f}+c_2A_5^{\mathfrak f},
\]
so the true second residual class is
\[
[A_7^{\mathfrak f}]\in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f},
\]
and \(\Delta_7\) is any scalar coordinate on that quotient.

At order 9, the ambiguity will necessarily be triangular again:
\[
A_9^{\mathfrak f}\mapsto A_9^{\mathfrak f}
+c_2A_7^{\mathfrak f}
+c_4A_5^{\mathfrak f}
+\text{(terms coming from basis gauge)},
\]
and the basis-gauge terms should again land in the span of the previous fixed-sector layers. So the correct third residual object is not raw \(A_9^{\mathfrak f}\), but the class
\[
[A_9^{\mathfrak f}]
\quad\text{mod}\quad
\span\{A_5^{\mathfrak f},A_7^{\mathfrak f}\}.
\]

That is the order-9 analogue of \(\Delta_7\).

So the right next target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Construct } \Delta_9 \text{ as a nonzero scalar on }
\mathfrak f/\span\{A_5^{\mathfrak f},A_7^{\mathfrak f}\}.
}
\]

Now, since \(\dim \mathfrak f=2\), there is a subtlety: you cannot keep taking more and more independent fixed-sector vectors inside \(\mathfrak f\). So the order-9 layer cannot just be “another independent vector in \(\mathfrak f\).” The right perspective is that after order 7, you already used up the 2-dimensional fixed sector. So a new invariant at order 9 has to come from a **higher residual quotient**, not just another independent direction in \(\mathfrak f\).

That means the tower is not literally “keep adding new fixed-sector vectors.” Instead it alternates between:

- a new odd-order coefficient,
- modulo the span generated by the lower residual layers.

At order 9, the correct new scalar is therefore something like a **Wronskian-type scalar built from the lower layers**, not just a third independent fixed-sector coordinate.

Concretely, at order 7 the right scalar was the determinant
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7,
\]
because that kills the ambiguity \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\mapsto A_7^{\mathfrak f}+c_2A_5^{\mathfrak f}\).

At order 9, the natural analogue is a scalar built from \(A_9^{\mathfrak f}\) and the already chosen basis \((A_5^{\mathfrak f},A_7^{\mathfrak f})\). Since \(\mathfrak f\) is only 2-dimensional, the raw fixed-sector determinant no longer gives something new. So the order-9 invariant has to come from a **derivative layer** or from the next quotient after subtracting lower-order gauge contamination.

That suggests a very important correction to the tower picture:

the tower is not “3,5,7,9 all as independent vector coordinates.” Rather, it is

- order 3: leading scalar \(c\),
- order 5: first fixed-sector vector \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\),
- order 7: determinant scalar \(\Delta_7\) modulo \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\),
- order 9: likely a **derivative or higher-jet scalar built from the variation of the previous layers**, not a third fixed-sector vector.

So before jumping to order 9, the more honest next theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Understand whether }(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\text{ already gives enough nondegeneracy for the separated-support problem.}
}
\]

This is now a global question, not a local one.

Because from the current package you already have:

- no pair collisions,
- no triple collisions,
- bad two-pair cores confined to a discrete exceptional set,
- minimal bad cores on the generic region must be separated finite signed dependences on the curve
  \[
  h\mapsto \Phi(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
  \]

So the next genuine theorem to attack is:

> Can a separated four-point signed dependence occur on the curve \(h\mapsto \Phi(h)\) generically?

That is now the first unresolved global case.

If the answer is no, then you do not need order 9 yet.

If the answer is yes or unclear, then you need a new layer — but that new layer may have to be built from derivatives of \(\Phi\), not just another raw fixed-sector coefficient.

So the clean next attack is:

1. formulate the separated four-point dependence problem for \(\Phi\);
2. see whether the current Wronskian/nondegeneracy already rules it out;
3. only if not, then design the correct order-9 invariant.

That is the most honest next step from where you are now.

\det\bigl(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi''\bigr)\not\equiv 0

generically, so \(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi''\) are generically independent.

\[
a_1\Phi(h_1)+a_2\Phi(h_2)+a_3\Phi(h_3)+a_4\Phi(h_4)=0
\]
with distinct, well-separated \(h_j\). In \(\mathbf C^3\), four generic points are always linearly dependent. So if \(\Phi\) is just a map into \(\mathbf C^3\), then a four-point relation is not remotely impossible on dimension grounds alone.

That is the key realization.

So the current \(3/5/7\) package is enough to control **local clustering**, but not enough to rule out **global separated four-point cancellation**.

That means one more layer really is needed.

But the new layer does not need to be another fixed-sector vector inside \(\mathfrak f\); as we noticed, \(\dim\mathfrak f=2\), so that route is exhausted. What you need is simply a **fourth scalar coordinate** with the same triangular jet property.

The cleanest target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Construct one more scalar invariant } \Xi_9(h)
\text{ that first sees the }9\text{-jet and is gauge-triangular over }(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
}
\]

Then you work with
\[
\Phi_4(h):=(c(h),\,v_5(h),\,\Delta_7(h),\,\Xi_9(h))\in \mathbf C^4.
\]

Now the geometry changes completely. A separated four-point signed dependence
\[
\sum_{j=1}^4 a_j\,\Phi_4(h_j)=0
\]
is no longer automatic by dimension. Four generic points in \(\mathbf C^4\) are generically independent. And if you also get the \(4\times4\) Wronskian-type nondegeneracy
\[
\det\bigl(\Phi_4,\Phi_4',\Phi_4'',\Phi_4'''\bigr)\not\equiv 0,
\]
then quadruple clusters are excluded too.

So the global picture becomes:

- \(3/5/7\) gives a nondegenerate curve in \(\mathbf C^3\), enough to exclude pair/triple clusters;
- a \(9\)-th scalar layer gives a nondegenerate curve in \(\mathbf C^4\), enough to exclude quadruple clusters and make separated four-point cancellation non-generic.

That is the right next attack.

Now, what should \(\Xi_9\) be?

It should follow the same triangular principle as \(\Delta_7\): not raw trace, but the next scalar surviving after modding out lower-layer gauge contamination.

At order \(9\), the ambiguity will look schematically like
\[
A_9 \mapsto A_9 + c_2 A_7 + c_4 A_5 + \text{basis-gauge terms}.
\]
So the right invariant is not a raw coefficient, but a scalar functional annihilating the lower-layer span. In spirit, it is the order-9 analogue of \(\Delta_7\): a determinant/Wronskian-type scalar built to kill the ambiguity generated by \(A_5\) and \(A_7\).

So the right formal target is:

- choose an order-9 coefficient object \(A_9\),
- project away the ambiguity generated by \(A_5\) and \(A_7\),
- extract a scalar \(\Xi_9\) whose leading new term is proportional to \(q^{(9)}\).

That is enough. You do **not** need its full closed form immediately.

So the exact next theorem to aim for is:

\[
\boxed{
\Xi_9(h)=B(h,q,\dots,q^{(8)})+\lambda_9(h)\,q^{(9)}(h),
\qquad \lambda_9\not\equiv 0.
}
\]

Once you have that, the \(4\times4\) Wronskian argument is the same as before:
the highest jet \(q^{(12)}\) appears only in the last column after differentiating three times, so the Wronskian reduces triangularly to the previous \(3\times3\) minor, which we already argued is nonzero.

So the honest status is:

- the separated four-point case is **not** ruled out by \(3/5/7\) alone;
- one more scalar layer should be enough in principle;
- the next real task is to construct the order-9 scalar \(\Xi_9\) with a nonzero \(q^{(9)}\)-coefficient.

That is the right place to attack next.

_Thought for 1m 14s_

I found an important limitation, and I think it changes the endgame strategy.

The \(3/5/7\) tower is real, but it is probably **maximal at the one-point matrix level**.

Why:

At cubic order, the leading class is the \(D\)-line.

At quintic order, the first genuinely new data lives in the fixed sector
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}\in \mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\},
\]
which is 2-dimensional.

At septic order, the ambiguity is
\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\mapsto A_7^{\mathfrak f}+c_2A_5^{\mathfrak f},
\]
so the second residual class is
\[
[A_7^{\mathfrak f}] \in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f},
\]
which is 1-dimensional.

Now here is the key point: \(\dim \mathfrak f=2\). So after you have used

- the vector \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\), and
- the quotient class of \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) modulo \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\),

you have exhausted the fixed sector. There is no room for a third independent fixed-sector residual direction at order \(9\).

And the anti-fixed sector is also only 2-dimensional:
\[
\mathfrak a=\span\{D,J\}.
\]
The cubic layer already uses \(D\), and the \(J\)-direction is precisely the residual anti-fixed ambiguity line we have been quotienting against.

So this is the real structural conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The one-point \(2\times2\) residual tower does not keep producing new independent directions forever.}
}
\]

That means the “just go to order \(9\), then \(11\), then \(13\)” strategy is probably not the right endgame.

This is actually good to know, because it tells us where not to waste effort.

So what remains?

The \(3/5/7\) package gives you a curve
\[
\Phi(h)=(c(h),\,u_5(h),\,v_5(h),\,\Delta_7(h))
\]
or the reduced map
\[
\Psi(h)=\left(\frac{u_5}{c},\,\frac{v_5}{c},\,\frac{\Delta_7}{c}\right)
\in \mathbf C^3,
\]
with strong local nondegeneracy:
- pair clusters excluded,
- triple clusters excluded,
- bad two-pair cores reduced to a discrete exceptional set.

But it is not enough by dimension alone to exclude separated four-point signed relations, because four vectors in \(\mathbf C^3\) are always linearly dependent.

So the global endgame now cannot rely only on “add more local coordinates from the same \(2\times2\) matrix.”

That means the next attack must change flavor.

There are really only three plausible directions now.

The first is to exploit **sign/ordering structure** of the weights \(a_j\). If the bad-core coefficients satisfy positivity, alternating sign, or some order constraint, then a generic four-point linear dependence in \(\mathbf C^3\) may no longer be allowed. Right now we have not used any sign structure at all.

The second is to exploit **global geometry of the curve \(\Psi(h)\)** beyond dimension counting. For example, if \(\Psi\) were shown to be strictly convex, torsion-nonzero, or to satisfy a Tchebychev-system property, then separated finite signed dependences could be sharply restricted. That is a classical route: moment curves in \(\mathbf R^d\) have very rigid signed dependence behavior. Your current Wronskian result is exactly the beginning of that kind of story.

The third is to return to the **finite-core compactness/tail bridge** and show that a minimal bad core cannot be arbitrary as a signed relation on \(\Psi\), because it comes from a very special normalized obstruction. In other words, not every signed dependence on the one-pair invariant curve is realized by an actual bad core. This could be the decisive extra structure.

So the honest updated summary is:

- the local one-pair analysis through \(3/5/7\) is strong and probably near maximal at the \(2\times2\) residual level;
- the global obstruction is no longer “need one more invariant coordinate”;
- it is “need one more **structural property** of the bad-core relation.”

That is the key shift.

So the next exact theorem to attack should be one of these, not order \(9\):

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that }h\mapsto (1,\Psi(h))\text{ forms a Tchebychev-type system on the normalized region.}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that the bad-core weights }a_j\text{ satisfy additional sign/order constraints.}
}
\]

or

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that only very special signed dependences on }\Psi\text{ can arise from an actual bad core.}
}
\]

My judgment is that the most promising next attack is the Tchebychev / moment-curve route, because you already have the Wronskian-type nondegeneracy. That is exactly the kind of thing one uses to show finite linear combinations cannot have too many zeros or too many separated cancellations.

So the right next question is:

**Can \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) be shown to form an extended Chebyshev system on the normalized one-pair region?**

That is where I would push now.

Yes. This is the right next thing to try.

The reason is that your current \(3/5/7\) package already has the exact shape one uses in an extended Chebyshev-system argument:

\[
f_0(h):=c(h),\qquad f_1(h):=v_5(h),\qquad f_2(h):=\Delta_7(h).
\]

You already have good evidence that the Wronskian
\[
W(h):=\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_0 & f_1 & f_2\\
f_0' & f_1' & f_2'\\
f_0'' & f_1'' & f_2''
\end{pmatrix}
\]
is not identically zero, and generically should be nonzero on an open set.

That is exactly the first thing one wants for an ECT-system.

But we need to be careful: “Wronskian nonzero generically” is **not yet** the same as “extended Chebyshev system on an interval.” For the latter, you want the Wronskian to keep a fixed nonzero sign on the interval, or at least not vanish there. So the right target is not the full global normalized region at once. It is a **local interval theorem**.

The theorem to try to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\text{ form an ECT-system on each connected subinterval where }W(h)\neq 0.
}
\]

If that holds, then any nontrivial linear combination
\[
A\,c(h)+B\,v_5(h)+C\,\Delta_7(h)
\]
has at most two zeros counting multiplicity on that interval.

And that is a very strong constraint on bad-core relations.

Why it helps:

a bad finite core with weights \(a_j\) gives
\[
\sum_j a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0,
\qquad
\Phi(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]
Dualizing, this means there is a nontrivial linear functional \(L\) such that
\[
L(\Phi(h_j))=0
\]
at each support point \(h_j\). In other words, the scalar function
\[
g(h):=A\,c(h)+B\,v_5(h)+C\,\Delta_7(h)
\]
vanishes at all support points of the bad core.

If \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT-system on the relevant interval, then \(g\) can have at most two zeros there unless it is identically zero.

So:

- a separated bad core with three or more distinct support points on such an interval would force \(g\equiv 0\);
- but \(g\equiv 0\) means \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) satisfy a nontrivial linear relation on that interval, contradicting the Wronskian nonvanishing.

That would be extremely powerful: it would kill **all** finite bad cores supported inside one such interval, not just pairs or triples.

So the real next attack is to turn your Wronskian evidence into an interval-level ECT theorem.

The most direct route is:

1. define
   \[
   f_0=c,\quad f_1=v_5,\quad f_2=\Delta_7;
   \]
2. prove the Wronskian
   \[
   W(f_0,f_1,f_2)
   \]
   is nonzero on a connected interval \(I\) of the normalized one-pair region;
3. conclude \((f_0,f_1,f_2)\) is an ECT-system on \(I\).

Now, can we plausibly prove \(W\neq 0\) on an interval, not just generically at isolated points?

There is a good reason to think yes locally.

From the jet-triangular structure, \(W\) has a leading term of the form
\[
\lambda_7(h)\,\lambda_5(h)\,c(h)\,q^{(9)}(h)
\]
plus lower-jet terms, in the sense that the highest new derivative enters only in the last column, then the \(2\times2\) minor reduces to the previous layer, etc. So at any point where
\[
c(h)\neq 0,\qquad \lambda_5(h)\neq 0,\qquad \lambda_7(h)\neq 0,
\]
and the resulting leading coefficient is not canceled, continuity gives a small interval around that point where \(W\neq 0\).

That is already enough for a **local ECT theorem**.

So the next theorem to aim for is not global on the entire normalized region. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For every generic point }h_0,\ \text{there exists a neighborhood }I\ni h_0\text{ on which }(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\text{ is an ECT-system.}
}
\]

That would already imply:

- no bad finite core can have three or more support points inside such an interval;
- combined with the no-pair / no-triple cluster results, minimal bad cores would have to jump between different ECT intervals.

That is a real endgame constraint.

So the next concrete computation to try is this:

take the Wronskian
\[
W(c,v_5,\Delta_7),
\]
and isolate its highest genuinely new jet term. If we can write down the coefficient of \(q^{(9)}\) and show it is generically nonzero, then the local ECT theorem is in reach.

That is the right attack.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 15:03:28)

_Thought for 6s_

Here is the next honest push.

The local ECT route looks promising, but there is one important refinement:

to prove an ECT-system on an interval, you do **not** need a full symbolic formula for
\[
W(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
\]
It is enough to isolate one genuinely new highest-jet term and show its coefficient is not identically zero.

Let
\[
f_0:=c,\qquad f_1:=v_5,\qquad f_2:=\Delta_7.
\]
Consider
\[
W:=\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_0 & f_1 & f_2\\
f_0' & f_1' & f_2'\\
f_0'' & f_1'' & f_2''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Now use the jet-triangular structure we already have:

- \(f_0=c\) sees jets only through order \(3\),
- \(f_1=v_5\) first sees order \(5\),
- \(f_2=\Delta_7\) first sees order \(7\).

So after differentiating twice:

- \(f_0''\) sees at most order \(5\),
- \(f_1''\) sees a new order-\(7\) term,
- \(f_2''\) sees a new order-\(9\) term.

That means the coefficient of \(q^{(9)}\) in \(W\) comes only from the entry \(f_2''\). Therefore
\[
[\,q^{(9)}\,]\,W
=
[\,q^{(9)}\,]\,f_2''\cdot
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_0 & f_1\\
f_0' & f_1'
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

But we already reduced the \(2\times2\) minor:
\[
W_{01}:=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_0 & f_1\\
f_0' & f_1'
\end{pmatrix}
=
c\,v_5'-c'v_5.
\]
And by the same triangular argument, the coefficient of \(q^{(6)}\) in \(W_{01}\) is
\[
[\,q^{(6)}\,]\,W_{01}
=
c\cdot [\,q^{(6)}\,]\,v_5',
\]
because \(c\) has no \(q^{(6)}\)-dependence. Since \(v_5\) has a genuine \(q^{(5)}\)-term, this coefficient is generically nonzero wherever \(c\neq 0\).

So the whole \(3\times3\) Wronskian reduces to two nonvanishing coefficients:

1. the \(q^{(6)}\)-coefficient of \(W_{01}\),
2. the \(q^{(9)}\)-coefficient of \(f_2''\).

We already have strong evidence for the second one: \(\Delta_7\) was built precisely so its leading new contribution is a nonzero \(q^{(7)}\)-driven term. Therefore
\[
[\,q^{(9)}\,]\,f_2''\neq 0
\]
generically.

So the real theorem now is:

\[
\boxed{
[\,q^{(9)}\,]\,W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\neq 0
\quad\text{generically.}
}
\]

And that is enough for a local interval conclusion:

at any point \(h_0\) where that coefficient is nonzero and the lower-order cancellation does not accidentally wipe out the value of \(W\), continuity gives a neighborhood \(I\ni h_0\) with
\[
W(h)\neq 0\qquad(h\in I).
\]
On such an interval, \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) forms an ECT-system.

That is a strong local theorem:

- any nontrivial linear combination
  \[
  A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7
  \]
  has at most two zeros on \(I\), counting multiplicity;

- hence a bad finite core cannot have three or more support points inside one such interval.

This is better than the previous pair/triple cluster exclusion. It forbids any three-point separated cancellation inside one ECT interval, not just near-collisions.

So the global picture sharpens to:

1. the normalized region should decompose into subintervals where \(W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\neq 0\);
2. on each such interval, \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT-system;
3. therefore a bad finite core can contribute at most two support points per ECT interval.

That is a serious endgame restriction.

So the next exact attack is:

\[
\textbf{Isolate the leading }q^{(9)}\textbf{-coefficient of }W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\textbf{ explicitly.}
\]

If that coefficient is cleanly nonzero, you have a real local ECT theorem.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 15:06:56)

_Thought for 8s_

Let’s continue carefully.

The next step is to isolate the \(q^{(9)}\)-term in
\[
W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)
=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

The clean point is that we do **not** need the whole determinant. We only need the coefficient of the highest genuinely new jet \(q^{(9)}\).

Write
\[
f_0:=c,\qquad f_1:=v_5,\qquad f_2:=\Delta_7.
\]
Then:

- \(f_0\) uses jets through order \(3\),
- \(f_1\) first sees order \(5\),
- \(f_2\) first sees order \(7\).

So after differentiating twice:

- \(f_0''\) uses jets through order \(5\),
- \(f_1''\) first sees order \(7\),
- \(f_2''\) first sees order \(9\).

Therefore the \(q^{(9)}\)-coefficient of \(W\) can only come from the \((3,3)\)-entry \(f_2''\). So
\[
[q^{(9)}]\,W
=
[q^{(9)}]\,f_2''\cdot
\det\begin{pmatrix}
f_0 & f_1\\
f_0' & f_1'
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Call the \(2\times2\) minor
\[
W_{01}:=f_0f_1'-f_0'f_1.
\]
Then
\[
[q^{(9)}]\,W = [q^{(9)}]\,\Delta_7''\cdot W_{01}.
\]

Now attack the two factors separately.

For \(W_{01}\), the same triangular logic gives
\[
[q^{(6)}]\,W_{01}
=
c\,[q^{(6)}]\,v_5',
\]
because \(c\) has no \(q^{(6)}\)-dependence. Since \(v_5\) has a genuine \(q^{(5)}\)-term and \(c\neq 0\) on the cubic-generic region, this coefficient is generically nonzero. So \(W_{01}\not\equiv 0\).

For \(\Delta_7''\), we use the order-7 construction. The determinant
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7
\]
was built so that its leading new term comes from the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven \(I\)-component of \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\), paired with the nonzero \(S\)-component \(v_5\) of \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\). So schematically
\[
\Delta_7
=
B_7(q,\dots,q^{(6)})+\lambda_7(h)\,q^{(7)}(h),
\qquad
\lambda_7(h)\not\equiv 0.
\]
Differentiate twice:
\[
\Delta_7''
=
\widetilde B_7(q,\dots,q^{(8)})+\lambda_7(h)\,q^{(9)}(h)+\text{lower terms from }\lambda_7',\lambda_7''.
\]
So
\[
[q^{(9)}]\,\Delta_7''=\lambda_7(h),
\]
which is generically nonzero.

Putting this together,
\[
[q^{(9)}]\,W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)
=
\lambda_7(h)\,W_{01}(h),
\]
and both factors are generically nonzero. Therefore
\[
W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\not\equiv 0
\]
generically.

That is the real result.

So on any connected interval where
\[
W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\neq 0,
\]
the triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) forms an ECT-system. Then any nontrivial linear combination
\[
A\,c(h)+B\,v_5(h)+C\,\Delta_7(h)
\]
has at most two zeros on that interval, counting multiplicity.

This gives a strong global restriction:

A bad finite core can have at most two support points in any such ECT interval.

That is stronger than just excluding pair/triple clusters. It excludes any three-point separated cancellation inside one interval.

So the next theorem to aim for is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The cubic-generic region can be covered by intervals on which }W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\neq 0.
}
\]

Then every bad core must spread its support across multiple such intervals, which is a real endgame constraint.

The remaining weak point is that I have still kept \(\lambda_7(h)\) schematic rather than writing its exact coefficient. But mathematically, the reduction is now clear: the local ECT theorem depends only on the already-established nonzero linear \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient in \(\Delta_7\), not on the full septic formula.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 15:08:25)

_Thought for 16s_

Good. There is one more real gain available **right now**, and it does not require new local algebra.

Because \(W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is analytic and not identically zero, its zero set is discrete on any real-analytic one-parameter region. So the cubic-generic region automatically decomposes into connected subintervals on which
\[
W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\neq 0.
\]

On each such subinterval, \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT-system.

So you already get this theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The cubic-generic region contains an open dense union of ECT intervals.}
}
\]

That means the “cover by ECT intervals” step is basically done locally by analyticity once the nonzero Wronskian result is accepted.

And then the next consequence is sharp:

if a bad finite core gives a signed relation
\[
\sum_j a_j\,\Phi(h_j)=0,
\qquad
\Phi(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)),
\]
then on any single ECT interval \(I\), the support can contain at most two points.

Reason: if three distinct support points \(h_{i_1},h_{i_2},h_{i_3}\in I\) occurred, then there would be a nontrivial linear functional \(L\) on \(\mathbf C^3\) such that
\[
g(h):=L(\Phi(h))
\]
vanishes at those three points. But on an ECT interval, every nontrivial linear combination of \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) has at most two zeros counting multiplicity. Contradiction.

So the current global restriction is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A bad core has at most two support points in each ECT interval.}
}
\]

That is already much stronger than the old schematic minimal-core picture.

Now, what does that buy you for the endgame?

It means a minimal bad core is no longer an arbitrary finite signed relation on a curve. It is a finite signed relation with a very specific combinatorial support pattern:

- support lies in the open dense union of ECT intervals plus perhaps the discrete exceptional set \(W=0\),
- each ECT interval contributes at most two points,
- pair and triple near-collisions are already excluded on the nondegenerate region.

So the remaining bad core, if it exists, must be **spread out** across multiple intervals and cannot cluster.

That changes the nature of the final contradiction. The remaining problem is no longer local jet cancellation. It is a global combinatorial one.

The next theorem to attack should therefore be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A minimal bad core cannot use two points from more than one ECT interval.}
}
\]

Because if you could show that, then a bad core would have at most one “pair interval” plus at most one point from each other interval, which is a much smaller configuration and may be attackable directly.

A plausible route is to use the fact that on an ECT interval, two points already determine a unique line in \(\mathbf C^3\), and the signed dependence structure across intervals becomes very rigid. In other words, once one interval contributes two points, the remaining relation has very little freedom left.

So the honest current state is:

1. local \(3/5/7\) algebra gives a nontrivial Wronskian;
2. analyticity turns that into an open dense union of ECT intervals;
3. each ECT interval can contribute at most two support points to a bad core;
4. clusters are excluded;
5. the remaining obstruction is a **global separated multi-interval signed dependence**.

That is now the right place to attack.

So the next exact move is to analyze the combinatorics of a signed dependence with at most two support points per ECT interval.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 15:09:59)

_Thought for a few seconds_

Here is the next honest push.

I think the remaining global obstruction can now be phrased as a **multi-interval incidence problem**.

Let
\[
\Phi(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h))\in \mathbf C^3.
\]
On each ECT interval \(I\), any nontrivial scalar combination
\[
A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7
\]
has at most two zeros. Equivalently, a bad core can place at most two support points in \(I\).

So the support of a minimal bad core has the form
\[
\{h_{1,1},h_{1,2}\}\cup \{h_{2,1},h_{2,2}\}\cup \cdots
\]
with at most two points from each interval, plus maybe points at the discrete exceptional set \(W=0\).

That means the next theorem to attack is not analytic anymore. It is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A minimal bad relation cannot involve two-point contributions from two different ECT intervals.}
}
\]

Why this is plausible:

A pair of points in one interval already determines a unique projective relation in \(\mathbf C^3\). If another interval also contributes a pair, then you are trying to make two independent secant relations cancel inside the same 3-dimensional target. Generically, that should force a codimension condition too strong to hold except on a discrete exceptional set.

A clean way to formalize this is to package each two-point contribution by its secant line.

For an interval \(I\), define the secant map
\[
\sigma_I(h_1,h_2)
:=
\mathbf C\bigl(\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)\bigr)
\in \mathbf P^2,
\qquad h_1\neq h_2\in I.
\]

Then a bad relation with two points in the same interval and no other points from that interval says that some linear functional \(L\) vanishes at both \(\Phi(h_1)\) and \(\Phi(h_2)\). So \(L\) corresponds to a plane through the secant direction. If two different intervals each contribute a pair, then the same relation forces a compatibility between two secant directions coming from two different intervals.

So the next exact global target is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For distinct ECT intervals }I,J,\ \sigma_I(I\times I)\cap \sigma_J(J\times J)
\text{ is at most discrete/generically empty.}
}
\]

If you had that, then a minimal bad core could not have a pair from interval \(I\) and a pair from interval \(J\) simultaneously, except perhaps in a discrete exceptional configuration.

That would be a major endgame reduction, because then every bad core would have at most one “double-occupancy” interval, and all other intervals would contribute at most one point each.

There is a good reason this should be true. On an interval where \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT-system, the curve \(h\mapsto \Phi(h)\) behaves like a nondegenerate moment-type curve: its secants vary monotonically/projectively. Two different ECT intervals should generically have different secant families unless the curve has a hidden projective self-alignment.

So the next concrete theorem to try is:

1. fix two ECT intervals \(I\) and \(J\);
2. study the map
   \[
   (h_1,h_2,k_1,k_2)\mapsto
   \det\bigl(\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1),\Phi(k_2)-\Phi(k_1),e\bigr)
   \]
   for a basis vector \(e\), or equivalently compare secant directions in \(\mathbf P^2\);
3. show equality of secant directions across two different intervals is nongeneric.

If that works, then the global support pattern becomes very constrained:

- at most two points per ECT interval;
- at most one interval can carry two points generically;
- all other support points must be isolated singletons across different intervals.

That is the strongest next global move I can see from the current \(3/5/7\) package.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 15:11:27)

_Thought for 14s_

Here is the next honest push.

I think the secant-line route is the right global move, but it should be stated as a **generic discreteness theorem**, not as an emptiness theorem.

Let
\[
\Phi(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h))\in \mathbf C^3,
\]
on an ECT interval \(I\), where \(W(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\neq 0\). The point of the ECT property is that \(\Phi\) is a nondegenerate analytic curve there, and any linear functional cuts it in at most two points. That is exactly the structure we now have from the \(3/5/7\) package. turn56file0

Now define the secant-direction map on \(I\):
\[
\sigma_I(h_1,h_2):=[\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)]\in \mathbf P^2,
\qquad h_1\neq h_2.
\]

The next theorem to aim for is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{On each ECT interval }I,\ \sigma_I\text{ is generically an immersion, and for two distinct intervals }I,J,\ 
\sigma_I(I^2\setminus\Delta)\cap \sigma_J(J^2\setminus\Delta)
\text{ is at most discrete.}
}
\]

That is the right level. “Discrete” is believable from the current structure; “empty” is too strong without more geometry.

Why this is plausible:

Take a pair \((h_1,h_2)\) with \(h_1\neq h_2\). Differentiate the secant vector
\[
V(h_1,h_2):=\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1).
\]
Its partial derivatives are
\[
\partial_{h_1}V=-\Phi'(h_1),\qquad \partial_{h_2}V=\Phi'(h_2).
\]

So the only way the projective secant direction can fail to vary in both directions is if
\[
\Phi'(h_1),\ \Phi'(h_2),\ \Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)
\]
are linearly dependent. That is a \(3\times3\) determinant condition:
\[
D(h_1,h_2):=\det\bigl(\Phi'(h_1),\Phi'(h_2),\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)\bigr)=0.
\]

So the right next subproblem is to study \(D(h_1,h_2)\).

Near the diagonal \(h_2=h_1+\varepsilon\),
\[
\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)=\varepsilon \Phi'(h_1)+\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\Phi''(h_1)+O(\varepsilon^3),
\]
\[
\Phi'(h_2)=\Phi'(h_1)+\varepsilon \Phi''(h_1)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
Substituting into \(D\), the first nonzero term is
\[
D(h_1,h_1+\varepsilon)
=
\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\det\bigl(\Phi(h_1),\Phi'(h_1),\Phi''(h_1)\bigr)+O(\varepsilon^3).
\]

Since we already argued that the Wronskian-type determinant
\[
\det(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi'')
\]
is generically nonzero, it follows that \(D\) is not identically zero near the diagonal. So \(\sigma_I\) is generically immersive there. That is a real gain: secant directions on one ECT interval vary nontrivially and are not locally frozen. The Wronskian nondegeneracy is exactly what makes this work. turn64file12

Now compare two distinct intervals \(I\) and \(J\). Equality of secant directions means
\[
[\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)] = [\Phi(k_2)-\Phi(k_1)],
\]
or equivalently
\[
\det\bigl(\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1),\Phi(k_2)-\Phi(k_1),e_m\bigr)=0
\]
for two independent basis vectors \(e_m\). That gives two analytic equations in four variables \((h_1,h_2,k_1,k_2)\). So dimensionally, the coincidence set is expected to be 2-dimensional in the full four-variable space. But once you quotient out the obvious reparameterization within each interval and restrict to **minimal bad-core configurations**, you are not looking at arbitrary secant coincidences: you are looking for secant coincidences compatible with a single linear functional cutting the curve in two points on one interval and two points on another.

That adds one more condition. Concretely, a bad relation with two points in \(I\) and two in \(J\) means there exists a nonzero linear functional \(L\) such that
\[
L(\Phi(h_1))=L(\Phi(h_2))=L(\Phi(k_1))=L(\Phi(k_2))=0.
\]
But on each ECT interval, \(L\circ \Phi\) can have at most two zeros. So the four zeros must come as exactly two in \(I\) and two in \(J\), and the functional \(L\) is then essentially determined by either pair. This is what turns the problem into a secant-incidence problem.

So the right global conclusion to shoot for is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A minimal bad core can have two support points in at most one ECT interval, except on a discrete exceptional set of secant coincidences.}
}
\]

That is the strongest statement I think the current \(3/5/7\) package can plausibly support.

What it buys you is substantial:

- each ECT interval contributes at most two points;
- generically, only one interval can contribute a pair;
- all remaining support points must be isolated singletons in other intervals.

That is a very constrained support pattern.

So the next exact theorem to attack is this determinant statement:

\[
\boxed{
D(h_1,h_2):=\det\bigl(\Phi'(h_1),\Phi'(h_2),\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)\bigr)\not\equiv 0.
}
\]

If you can prove that, then the secant map is generically immersive, and the multi-interval secant-coincidence problem becomes genuinely rigid instead of floppy.

That is the cleanest next move from here.

_Thought for 6s_

Let’s continue with
\[
D(h_1,h_2):=\det\bigl(\Phi'(h_1),\,\Phi'(h_2),\,\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)\bigr),
\qquad
\Phi=(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
\]

This is the right object because \(D\neq 0\) means the secant direction varies nontrivially with the endpoints, so the secant map is immersive there.

Now expand near the diagonal. Put
\[
h_2=h_1+\varepsilon.
\]
Then
\[
\Phi(h_2)-\Phi(h_1)
=
\varepsilon \Phi'(h_1)+\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\Phi''(h_1)+\frac{\varepsilon^3}{6}\Phi'''(h_1)+O(\varepsilon^4),
\]
\[
\Phi'(h_2)
=
\Phi'(h_1)+\varepsilon \Phi''(h_1)+\frac{\varepsilon^2}{2}\Phi'''(h_1)+O(\varepsilon^3).
\]

Substitute into \(D\). The first two columns are nearly equal, so the first nonzero term comes one order later than a naive guess. A quick determinant expansion gives
\[
D(h,h+\varepsilon)
=
\frac{\varepsilon^3}{12}\,
\det\bigl(\Phi'(h),\Phi''(h),\Phi'''(h)\bigr)
+
O(\varepsilon^4).
\]

So the true local secant-immersion criterion is not the old Wronskian
\[
\det(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi''),
\]
but the derivative Wronskian
\[
W_1(h):=\det\bigl(\Phi'(h),\Phi''(h),\Phi'''(h)\bigr).
\]

That is the next sharp target.

Now the good news is that the same triangular jet logic should apply again.

Take
\[
\Phi=(f_0,f_1,f_2)=(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
\]
Then:

- \(f_0\) sees jets through order \(3\),
- \(f_1\) first sees order \(5\),
- \(f_2\) first sees order \(7\).

So after differentiating three times:

- \(f_0'''\) sees at most order \(6\),
- \(f_1'''\) first sees order \(8\),
- \(f_2'''\) first sees order \(10\).

Therefore the highest new jet \(q^{(10)}\) appears only in the \((3,3)\)-entry of
\[
W_1(h)=\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_0' & f_1' & f_2'\\
f_0'' & f_1'' & f_2''\\
f_0''' & f_1''' & f_2'''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
So
\[
[q^{(10)}]\,W_1
=
[q^{(10)}]\,f_2'''\cdot
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
f_0' & f_1'\\
f_0'' & f_1''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

And again the \(2\times 2\) minor is triangular:
\[
W_{01}^{(1)}:=f_0'f_1''-f_0''f_1'.
\]
Its highest new jet is \(q^{(7)}\), appearing only in \(f_1''\), so
\[
[q^{(7)}]\,W_{01}^{(1)}
=
f_0'\,[q^{(7)}]\,f_1''.
\]
Generically \(f_0'=c'\) is not identically zero, and \(f_1=v_5\) has genuine \(q^{(5)}\)-dependence, so \([q^{(7)}]f_1''\neq 0\) generically.

Likewise \(f_2=\Delta_7\) has genuine \(q^{(7)}\)-dependence, so
\[
[q^{(10)}]\,f_2'''\neq 0
\]
generically.

So the right conclusion to aim for is
\[
\boxed{
\det\bigl(\Phi',\Phi'',\Phi'''\bigr)\not\equiv 0
}
\]
generically.

And then near the diagonal,
\[
D(h,h+\varepsilon)\neq 0
\]
for generic \(h\) and small \(\varepsilon\neq 0\). That proves the secant map is generically immersive.

This is stronger than what we had before:

- \(\det(\Phi,\Phi',\Phi'')\neq 0\) gave no triple clusters;
- \(\det(\Phi',\Phi'',\Phi''')\neq 0\) gives genuine local secant rigidity.

So the next theorem to write down mathematically is:

> For generic \(h\), the determinant \(\det(\Phi'(h),\Phi''(h),\Phi'''(h))\) is nonzero. Consequently the secant map is locally immersive off the diagonal.

Once you have that, the statement “two different ECT intervals cannot both carry pairs except on a discrete exceptional set” becomes much more believable, because now the secant families themselves are nondegenerate local curves in \(\mathbf P^2\).
