_Thought for 1m 23s_

Here is the strongest place the attack has reached.

The current draft still treats the two-pair and minimal-bad-core stages as a rigidity program rather than a finished contradiction, which matches what we have found: the one-pair \(3/5/7\) geometry is now much stronger, but the remaining obstacle is global, not local. turn67file4

The key gain is this.

Set
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
On the open dense set where
\[
c(h)\neq 0,\qquad x'(h)\neq 0,\qquad K(h):=x'(h)y''(h)-y'(h)x''(h)\neq 0,
\]
the image of \(h\mapsto (x(h),y(h))\) breaks into strictly convex graph components. On each such component, a line meets the graph in at most two points, so a bad core contributes at most two support points there.

We can now push adjacent-component analysis further.

If two adjacent convex components are separated by a \(K=0\) boundary with \(x'\neq 0\), then their tangent-slope intervals touch at most at one endpoint, so no line can be 2-secant to both. If they are separated by a generic \(x'=0\) boundary with \(x''\neq 0\), then in local coordinates
\[
x(t)=x_0+\alpha t^2+O(t^3),\qquad
y(t)=y_0+\beta t+\gamma t^2+O(t^3),\qquad \beta\neq 0,
\]
and for any line \(y=sx+b\),
\[
F(t):=y(t)-sx(t)-b
\]
has nonzero linear term \(F'(0)=\beta\), so near the boundary it is monotone and cannot have two zeros on each side. Thus generic adjacent components do not support a common local 4-secant either.

So the remaining obstruction has been reduced to this:

\[
\boxed{\text{only non-adjacent convex components can still potentially support a pair-pair configuration.}}
\]

For two ordered convex components
\[
C_1:\ y=Y_1(x),\ x\in[a_1,b_1],\qquad
C_2:\ y=Y_2(x),\ x\in[a_2,b_2],\qquad
b_1<a_2,
\]
define
\[
m_i(s):=\min_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx),\qquad
M_i(s):=\max_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx).
\]
Then a line \(y=sx+b\) is 2-secant to \(C_i\) iff
\[
m_i(s)<b<M_i(s).
\]
So a common 4-secant exists iff
\[
g_1(s):=m_1(s)-M_2(s)<0,\qquad
g_2(s):=m_2(s)-M_1(s)<0
\]
for some slope \(s\).

And for ordered components, these two functions are monotone in opposite directions:
\[
g_1'(s)>0,\qquad g_2'(s)<0.
\]

This is the real reduction. It means:

- common 4-secant slopes, if they exist, form at most one connected interval;
- to rule them out, it is enough to check endpoint inequalities at the ends of the common tangent-slope range.

So the next exact scalar to attack is the endpoint tangent-separation function. If \(h_*\) is the right endpoint of a convex component \(C_1\), with tangent slope
\[
m(h_*):=\frac{dy}{dx}(h_*)=\frac{c(h_*)\Delta_7'(h_*)-c'(h_*)\Delta_7(h_*)}{c(h_*)v_5'(h_*)-c'(h_*)v_5(h_*)},
\]
then the tangent line at \(h_*\) lies below a neighboring component \(C_2\) exactly when
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h):=
\Delta_7(h)c(h_*)-\Delta_7(h_*)c(h)
-
m(h_*)\bigl(v_5(h)c(h_*)-v_5(h_*)c(h)\bigr)
\]
has the right sign on \(C_2\).

So the attack has now become completely concrete:

1. Identify the convex components of
\[
h\mapsto \left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

2. Order them by \(x\)-range.

3. For each adjacent pair, evaluate the endpoint tangent-separation scalar \(\Theta_{h_*}(h)\) on the neighboring branch.

If that sign is one-sided, then that adjacent pair cannot share a common 4-secant. By the reductions above, that would leave only non-adjacent pair-pair configurations, which are much more global and much sparser.

So the present state is:

- one-pair \(3/5/7\) local geometry is strong;
- adjacent pair-pair obstructions are essentially gone generically;
- the remaining gap is endpoint separation for non-adjacent convex branches.

That is now the exact battlefield.

Here is the next honest push.

I think the non-adjacent case can be reduced to the adjacent case by monotonicity, and that is the right attack now.

Suppose the convex graph components are ordered by \(x\)-range:
\[
C_1,\ C_2,\ C_3,\dots
\]
with
\[
[a_i,b_i]\cap[a_j,b_j]=\varnothing,\qquad b_i<a_{i+1}.
\]

For two ordered components \(C_i<C_j\), define as before
\[
m_i(s):=\min_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx),\qquad
M_i(s):=\max_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx),
\]
and
\[
g_{ij}^{(1)}(s):=m_i(s)-M_j(s),\qquad
g_{ij}^{(2)}(s):=m_j(s)-M_i(s).
\]

A common 4-secant line for \(C_i\) and \(C_j\) exists iff there is a slope \(s\) with
\[
g_{ij}^{(1)}(s)<0,\qquad g_{ij}^{(2)}(s)<0.
\]

Now the key comparison is this:

if \(i<j<k\), then because the \(x\)-ranges are ordered,
\[
M_k(s)\le M_j(s)
\]
for every \(s\), and
\[
m_k(s)\le m_j(s)
\]
need not hold, but what matters is the pair of inequalities against a fixed left component \(C_i\).

For the first obstruction function,
\[
g_{ik}^{(1)}(s)=m_i(s)-M_k(s).
\]
Since \(C_k\) lies farther to the right than \(C_j\), the upper envelope \(M_k\) is shifted more strongly by slope than \(M_j\). In fact the derivative comparison is stricter:
\[
\frac{d}{ds}\bigl(M_j(s)-M_k(s)\bigr)>0
\]
piecewise, because all endpoint \(x\)-coordinates of \(C_k\) exceed those of \(C_j\). So once \(g_{ij}^{(1)}\) is nonnegative at one end of the common slope range, \(g_{ik}^{(1)}\) is even less favorable to overlap.

Likewise for the second obstruction function,
\[
g_{ik}^{(2)}(s)=m_k(s)-M_i(s),
\]
the farther-right component \(C_k\) has minimizer \(x_k(s)\) strictly larger than that of \(C_j\), so
\[
m_k'(s)=-x_k(s)<-x_j(s)=m_j'(s).
\]
Thus \(g_{ik}^{(2)}\) decreases faster than \(g_{ij}^{(2)}\).

What this means geometrically is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If adjacent pairs are endpoint-separated, then non-adjacent pairs are even more separated.}
}
\]

So the real next theorem to aim for is:

> It is enough to rule out common 4-secant lines for adjacent convex components.

Because once that is done, monotonicity in the \(x\)-ordering propagates the exclusion to all farther-separated pairs.

That is a genuine simplification.

So the entire remaining global problem has now collapsed to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For each adjacent pair of convex components, prove endpoint tangent separation.}
}
\]

And once that is proved, the consequences are strong:

- no component can share a 4-secant line with any other component;
- each convex component contributes at most two support points;
- and at most one component can contribute a pair in a bad core.

At that point a minimal bad core on the generic region would have support pattern:
- either all singletons, or
- one pair plus singletons.

That is much smaller than where we started.

Now, can we push the adjacent endpoint-separation a bit further abstractly?

Yes. There are only two generic kinds of adjacent boundaries:

1. \(K=0\) with \(x'\neq 0\), which we already handled: tangent-slope ranges only touch at an endpoint, so no common 4-secant.

2. \(x'=0\) with \(x''\neq 0\), where the local normal form
   \[
   x(t)=x_0+\alpha t^2+O(t^3),\qquad
   y(t)=y_0+\beta t+\gamma t^2+O(t^3),\qquad \beta\neq 0
   \]
   already shows no local common 4-secant near the boundary.

So the only way an adjacent pair could still share a common 4-secant is via a genuinely **nonlocal** line cutting both branches far from the boundary. That is exactly where the endpoint tangent-separation scalar
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h)
=
\Delta_7(h)c(h_*)-\Delta_7(h_*)c(h)
-
m(h_*)\bigl(v_5(h)c(h_*)-v_5(h_*)c(h)\bigr)
\]
comes in.

So the next precise attack is:

1. take an adjacent boundary point \(h_*\),
2. compute the tangent slope
   \[
   m(h_*)=\frac{c(h_*)\Delta_7'(h_*)-c'(h_*)\Delta_7(h_*)}{c(h_*)v_5'(h_*)-c'(h_*)v_5(h_*)},
   \]
3. study the sign of \(\Theta_{h_*}(h)\) on the neighboring component.

If \(\Theta_{h_*}(h)\) keeps one sign there, then the endpoint tangent line separates the two components, and adjacent common 4-secants are impossible.

So the current attack has reached a very sharp endpoint:

the remaining proof obligation is a sign theorem for \(\Theta_{h_*}(h)\) on adjacent branches. If that goes through, the whole generic pair-pair obstruction disappears.

on an entire neighboring component from the current information alone.

But we **can** push it one important step further:

we can show what the sign theorem would reduce to locally, and exactly what extra input is missing.

Write the affine graph coordinates as
\[
x(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad
y(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]
Then
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h)=c(h)c(h_*)\bigl(y(h)-\ell_{h_*}(x(h))\bigr),
\]
where \(\ell_{h_*}\) is the tangent line at the boundary point \(h_*\):
\[
\ell_{h_*}(x)=y(h_*)+m(h_*)(x-x(h_*)).
\]

So the sign of \(\Theta_{h_*}\) is exactly the sign of “neighboring branch minus the tangent line at \(h_*\),” up to the sign of \(c(h)c(h_*)\).

That means the problem is pure planar geometry.

Now take the generic \(x'=0\) boundary case. After shifting \(h_*=0\) and the point to the origin, the local normal form is
\[
x(t)=\alpha t^2+\alpha_3 t^3+O(t^4),\qquad \alpha\neq 0,
\]
\[
y(t)=\beta t+\gamma t^2+\delta t^3+O(t^4),\qquad \beta\neq 0.
\]

This is the right place to compute.

First, since \(x'(0)=0\) and \(y'(0)=\beta\neq 0\), the tangent is vertical in the \((x,y)\)-chart. So the “endpoint tangent line” is not a finite-slope affine line at all: it is the vertical line
\[
x=0.
\]

That is very important. It means the earlier formula using the slope
\[
m(h_*)=\frac{y'}{x'}
\]
does not make sense at a generic \(x'=0\) boundary. So the endpoint-tangent-separation theorem splits again:

- finite-slope endpoint tangents for ordinary component endpoints;
- vertical tangents at \(x'=0\) turning points.

For the \(x'=0\) case, the correct separation scalar is not \(\Theta_{h_*}\) built from \(m(h_*)\). It is simply
\[
x(h)-x(h_*).
\]

And now the local geometry is actually easy:

from
\[
x(t)=\alpha t^2+O(t^3),
\]
both adjacent branches lie on the same side of the vertical tangent \(x=0\) if \(\alpha>0\), or the opposite side if \(\alpha<0\). So the vertical tangent line itself does **not** separate the two branches. That means vertical-tangent separation is the wrong picture.

But recall what we already proved locally: because
\[
y(t)=\beta t+O(t^2),\qquad \beta\neq 0,
\]
any ordinary affine line intersects the local union of the two branches in at most one point near \(t=0\). So local 4-secant obstruction is already gone. The remaining issue is only nonlocal secants hitting the two branches far from the turning point.

So for \(x'=0\) boundaries, the correct global question is not tangent separation by the tangent line at the boundary. It is whether some nonlocal secant line can cut both branches twice. That needs more than the local normal form.

Now look at the other boundary type: \(K=0\), \(x'\neq 0\). There the slope
\[
m(h)=\frac{y'}{x'}
\]
is finite and smooth, and the earlier endpoint-tangent-separation scalar \(\Theta_{h_*}\) makes sense. But in that case we already showed something stronger: the tangent-slope intervals of the two adjacent convex components only meet at an endpoint, so no common 4-secant exists anyway. So \(\Theta_{h_*}\) is not needed there either.

This is the real conclusion:

\[
\boxed{
\Theta_{h_*}\text{ is not the universal remaining bottleneck.}
}
\]

At \(K=0\) boundaries, adjacency is already harmless.
At \(x'=0\) boundaries, the tangent is vertical and \(\Theta_{h_*}\) is not the right object.

So the adjacent-component problem is actually basically done generically:

- \(K=0\) boundaries: no common 4-secant by slope-range separation.
- \(x'=0\) boundaries: no **local** common 4-secant by the normal form.

What remains is genuinely **nonlocal**: could one line cut two non-adjacent convex components twice, or cut two branches adjacent across an \(x'=0\) turning point twice but far from the turning point?

That is no longer an endpoint tangent problem. It is a global line-incidence problem for the whole planar curve
\[
h\mapsto \left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

So the next honest attack is:

1. stop trying to prove \(\Theta_{h_*}\) sign on every adjacent branch;
2. treat \(K=0\) adjacencies as solved;
3. treat \(x'=0\) adjacencies as locally solved;
4. focus on the truly remaining question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can one affine line meet two separated convex graph components twice each?}
}
\]

And that is where the support-function overlap formulation
\[
m_i(s)<b<M_i(s)
\]
for each component is still the right global tool.

So the attack has clarified the battlefield again:

the adjacent-boundary tangent-sign theorem is **not** the right universal endpoint. The real remaining obstruction is purely global 4-secant incidence across separated convex components.

_Thought for 24s_

Here is the next honest push.

The remaining obstruction is now genuinely this:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can one affine line be 2-secant to two separated convex graph components?}
}
\]

And the right language is still the support-function one.

Take two separated convex graph components
\[
C_i:\ y=Y_i(x),\qquad x\in[a_i,b_i],\qquad i=1,2,
\]
with
\[
b_1<a_2.
\]

For slope \(s\), define
\[
m_i(s):=\min_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx),
\qquad
M_i(s):=\max_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx).
\]

Then a line \(y=sx+b\) is 2-secant to \(C_i\) iff
\[
m_i(s)<b<M_i(s).
\]

So a common 4-secant line exists iff there is an \(s\) such that
\[
m_1(s)<M_2(s)
\quad\text{and}\quad
m_2(s)<M_1(s).
\]

Now the crucial observation is:

for separated components, the second inequality
\[
m_2(s)<M_1(s)
\]
is the hard one, not the first.

Why? Because \(C_2\) is to the right of \(C_1\), so \(m_1\) is the lower support of the left branch and \(M_2\) is the upper secant ceiling of the right branch. There is usually plenty of room for
\[
m_1(s)<M_2(s).
\]
But
\[
m_2(s)<M_1(s)
\]
means the tangent support of the right branch drops below the top secant envelope of the left branch. That is the real crossing condition.

So define the key scalar
\[
G_{12}(s):=m_2(s)-M_1(s).
\]

Then a common 4-secant requires
\[
G_{12}(s)<0.
\]

Now, because \(C_2\) is to the right of \(C_1\),

- \(m_2'(s)=-x_2(s)\), where \(x_2(s)\in[a_2,b_2]\),
- \(M_1'(s)\in\{-a_1,-b_1\}\).

Hence
\[
G_{12}'(s)= -x_2(s)+e_1(s),\qquad e_1(s)\in\{a_1,b_1\}.
\]
Since
\[
e_1(s)\le b_1<a_2\le x_2(s),
\]
we get
\[
G_{12}'(s)<0.
\]

So this function is strictly decreasing.

That is the real one-variable monotonicity theorem:

\[
\boxed{
G_{12}(s)=m_2(s)-M_1(s)\text{ is strictly decreasing on any common slope range.}
}
\]

This has a strong consequence:

the set of slopes \(s\) for which a common 4-secant can exist is either empty or a single interval determined by one threshold crossing of \(G_{12}\).

So the problem is reduced to checking the sign of \(G_{12}\) at one reference slope.

And now there is a natural reference slope:
the slope where the support point on \(C_2\) is at its **leftmost endpoint** \(a_2\), namely
\[
s=Y_2'(a_2),
\]
assuming increasing slope on the branch.

At that slope,
\[
m_2(s)=Y_2(a_2)-s a_2.
\]
And
\[
M_1(s)=\max\{Y_1(a_1)-s a_1,\;Y_1(b_1)-s b_1\}.
\]

So the existence of any common 4-secant line between \(C_1\) and \(C_2\) is controlled by a single endpoint comparison:
\[
Y_2(a_2)-s a_2
\quad\stackrel{?}{<}\quad
\max\{Y_1(a_1)-s a_1,\;Y_1(b_1)-s b_1\},
\qquad s=Y_2'(a_2).
\]

Geometrically, this asks whether the left-end tangent support value of the right component lies below the secant ceiling of the left component.

That is the cleanest reduction we have.

So the next exact attack should be:

1. identify adjacent or separated convex components in the actual curve
   \[
   h\mapsto \left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right);
   \]
2. at the left endpoint of each right component \(C_2\), compute the tangent slope
   \[
   s=Y_2'(a_2);
   \]
3. compare
   \[
   Y_2(a_2)-s a_2
   \]
   against the endpoint secant ceiling of the left component.

If this comparison goes the favorable way, then because \(G_{12}\) is strictly decreasing, there is no common 4-secant at any slope.

So the battlefield is now a single endpoint inequality per ordered pair of convex components. That is about as far as the abstract reduction can go.

b_1<a_2,

the entire 4-secant question is controlled by the single function
\[
G_{12}(s):=m_2(s)-M_1(s),
\]
where
\[
m_2(s)=\min_{x\in[a_2,b_2]}(Y_2(x)-sx),
\qquad
M_1(s)=\max_{x\in[a_1,b_1]}(Y_1(x)-sx).
\]

A common 4-secant line requires \(G_{12}(s)<0\) for some slope \(s\). And we already proved the decisive monotonicity:
\[
G_{12}'(s)<0
\]
on any common slope range. So \(G_{12}\) is strictly decreasing.

That means there is exactly one real remaining question:

\[
\boxed{
\text{What is the sign of }G_{12}(s_0)\text{ at one endpoint slope }s_0?
}
\]

If it is nonnegative there, then by monotonicity the whole common-4-secant problem is dead for that ordered pair.

The cleanest reference slope is the left-end tangent slope of the right component:
\[
s_0:=Y_2'(a_2).
\]
At this slope,
\[
m_2(s_0)=Y_2(a_2)-s_0a_2.
\]
So
\[
G_{12}(s_0)
=
Y_2(a_2)-s_0a_2
-
\max\{Y_1(a_1)-s_0a_1,\;Y_1(b_1)-s_0b_1\}.
\]

Geometrically, this is just:

**take the tangent line to \(C_2\) at its left endpoint; compare its intercept with the highest endpoint-supported line of \(C_1\) of the same slope.**

So the whole remaining obstruction is equivalent to this endpoint tangent/endpoint secant comparison.

Now here is the strongest further simplification.

Because \(C_1\) is convex, if the tangent line to \(C_2\) at \(a_2\) lies above **both endpoints** of \(C_1\), then it lies above the entire graph of \(C_1\). So it is enough to check the two endpoint inequalities
\[
Y_2(a_2)-s_0a_2 \ge Y_1(a_1)-s_0a_1,
\]
\[
Y_2(a_2)-s_0a_2 \ge Y_1(b_1)-s_0b_1.
\]

Equivalently,
\[
Y_2(a_2)-Y_1(a_1)\ge s_0(a_2-a_1),
\]
\[
Y_2(a_2)-Y_1(b_1)\ge s_0(a_2-b_1).
\]

If these hold, then
\[
G_{12}(s_0)\ge 0,
\]
and since \(G_{12}\) is strictly decreasing, the only way to get a common 4-secant later would be for \(G_{12}\) to cross below zero at larger slope. But this can happen at most once, and if the common slope range starts at \(s_0\), then you are done.

So the theorem you want now is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For each ordered adjacent pair of convex components, the tangent line at the left endpoint of the right component lies above the left component.}
}
\]

That is now the exact global bottleneck.

And this bottleneck is not another abstract local-algebra issue. It is a statement about the actual endpoint geometry of the curve
\[
h\mapsto \left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

So the next concrete attack is:

1. Identify what the endpoints \(a_i,b_i\) of convex components correspond to in \(h\): either \(x'=0\) or \(K=0\).

2. At such an endpoint \(h_*\), compute
   \[
   x(h_*)=\frac{v_5(h_*)}{c(h_*)},
   \qquad
   y(h_*)=\frac{\Delta_7(h_*)}{c(h_*)},
   \qquad
   s_0=\frac{dy}{dx}(h_*).
   \]

3. Evaluate the endpoint tangent-separation scalar
   \[
   \Lambda_{h_*}(h)
   :=
   y(h)-y(h_*)-s_0\bigl(x(h)-x(h_*)\bigr).
   \]

4. Show that \(\Lambda_{h_*}(h)\le 0\) on the neighboring left component, or at least at its endpoints.

That is now the sharpest attack left.

So the honest current status is:

- all the local 3/5/7 algebra has been reduced to a planar convex-curve problem;
- all the combinatorics of bad cores has been reduced to common 4-secant lines between ordered convex components;
- and that has now been reduced to a single endpoint tangent-vs-endpoint secant inequality.

That is as far as the abstract attack goes. The next step has to use actual structure of the specific curve, not just general convexity.

Now rewrite those in terms of the original invariants \(c,v_5,\Delta_7\).

So the \(x'=0\) boundaries are exactly the zeros of \(M\) on the cubic-generic region.

\[

\[

\[
x''(h)=\frac{M'(h)c(h)-2M(h)c'(h)}{c(h)^3},
\qquad
y''(h)=\frac{N'(h)c(h)-2N(h)c'(h)}{c(h)^3}.
\]

\[
K(h)=x'(h)y''(h)-y'(h)x''(h)
=
\frac{M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)}{c(h)^4}.
\]

\[

K(h)=\frac{M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)}{c(h)^4},
\qquad
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5,\quad N=c\,\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7.

\]

\[
\{\,c\neq 0,\ M\neq 0,\ M N'-N M'\neq 0\,\}.
\]

1. \(c=0\), which is outside the cubic-generic region anyway;

\frac{c(h)^4 K(h)}{M(h)^2}.

### LaTeX to insert

```latex
\subsection{Fixed-sector refinement at quintic and septic order}

The trace witness at quintic order suggests a more structured refinement of the
one-pair residual germ than the trace alone records. For bookkeeping purposes,
it is convenient to decompose the quintic and septic coefficients into fixed and
anti-fixed parts with respect to the involution
\[
\iota(A):=J_0AJ_0,
\qquad
J_0=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\
1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Write
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A):=\frac12(A+J_0AJ_0),
\qquad
\pi_{\mathfrak a}(A):=\frac12(A-J_0AJ_0).
\]
Then
\[
\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\},
\qquad
\mathfrak a=\operatorname{span}\{D,J\},
\]
where
\[
I=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&1
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
S=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\1&0
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
D=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&-1
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
J=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&-1\\1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

\begin{definition}[Fixed-sector quintic and septic coefficients]
\label{def:fixed-sector-quintic-septic}
Let
\[
F_h(z)=A_3(h)z^3+A_5(h)z^5+A_7(h)z^7+\cdots
\]
denote the normalized one-pair quotient odd germ on the cubic-generic region.
Its fixed-sector quintic and septic coefficients are defined by
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h):=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_5(h)),
\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h):=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_7(h)).
\]
Write
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)\,I+v_5(h)\,S,
\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_7(h)\,I+v_7(h)\,S.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Second residual determinant]
\label{def:second-residual-determinant}
On the cubic-generic region, define
\[
\Delta_7(h):=u_7(h)v_5(h)-u_5(h)v_7(h).
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Interpretation]
\label{rem:fixed-sector-residual-package}
The quintic fixed-sector vector \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)\) refines the first
residual data beyond the trace detector alone, and the determinant
\(\Delta_7(h)\) records the next residual quotient at septic order. In the
working notes, these form the first two nontrivial layers of a one-pair
residual package:
\[
c(h),\qquad A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h),\qquad \Delta_7(h).
\]
The current draft has not yet fully translated these objects into the existing
coefficient notation \((u,v,w,\alpha,\beta,\gamma,r,\dots)\), but they are the
natural variables for the geometric reformulation of the multi-pair problem.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: fixed-sector geometry]
\label{rem:wip-fixed-sector-geometry}
The later local analysis suggests the normalized affine chart
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}
\]
on the cubic-generic region. The intended geometric program is to study the
planar curve
\[
h\longmapsto (x(h),y(h))
\]
rather than only the scalar quintic trace. In this formulation, the remaining
generic obstruction is reduced to common four-secant lines of this planar
curve.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Derived structural identities]
\label{rem:derived-fixed-sector-identities}
The working analysis gives the following identities, which should be proved in
a later draft in the paper's native coefficient language.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If
\[
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha&\beta\\\beta&\gamma
\end{pmatrix},
\]
then the quintic fixed-sector \(S\)-component coming from the commutator-type
channel is
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]

\item The direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven contribution to the septic
fixed-sector coefficient is \(I\)-directional. In particular, on that slice,
\[
v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\qquad
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]
Thus the determinant \(\Delta_7\) is the natural second residual scalar once
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) has been fixed.
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}
```

```latex
\begin{lemma}[First-order value-family generator]
\label{lem:first-order-value-family-generator}
Let $G_0$ and $N_0$ denote the background same-point and mixed blocks,
and write
\[
X_0:=G_0^{-1/2}.
\]
Let $L$ be the Fr\'echet derivative of the inverse-square-root map
$G\mapsto G^{-1/2}$ at $G_0$, so that
\[
(G_0+\varepsilon H)^{-1/2}
=
X_0+\varepsilon L(H)+O(\varepsilon^2).
\]
If $\dot G_1$ and $\dot N_1$ are the first odd same-point and mixed
variations generated by the pure value family, then the induced first-order
odd coefficient in the corrected local block is
\[
B_1
=
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1).
\]
Equivalently, the first-order value-family $V_{\val}^{(1)}$ is the image of
the linear map
\[
(\dot G_1,\dot N_1)\longmapsto
L(\dot G_1)\,N_0\,X_0
+
X_0\,\dot N_1\,X_0
+
X_0\,N_0\,L(\dot G_1).
\]
\end{lemma}
```

to

```latex
Lemma~\ref{lem:first-order-value-family-generator}
```

which the conversation identified as the real gauge-structure theorem needed before any residual-trace argument can be made honest. turn87file10

### Insert this exact block

```latex
\subsection{Normalization gauge at the first residual step}

The phrase ``value/gauge family'' bundles together two logically distinct
sources of ambiguity:

\begin{enumerate}
\item the \emph{pure value family}, recorded coefficientwise by the spaces
\(
V_{\val}^{(2j+1)}\subset \mathcal J_{2j+1},
\)
and

\item the \emph{normalization gauge}, consisting of even scalar
renormalizations and even jet-basis changes.
\end{enumerate}

For the first residual step it is useful to separate these two.

\begin{definition}[Normalization gauge]
\label{def:normalization-gauge}
Let \(F(z)\) be a matrix-valued odd germ. The normalization gauge acts by
\[
F(z)\longmapsto a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1},
\]
where
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+c_4 z^4+\cdots
\]
is an even scalar germ with \(a(0)=1\), and
\[
P(z)=I+B_2 z^2+B_4 z^4+\cdots
\]
is an even \(2\times 2\) matrix germ with \(P(0)=I\).
\end{definition}

\begin{proposition}[First-residual transformation law]
\label{prop:first-residual-gauge-law}
Let
\[
F(z)=A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5+O(z^7)
\]
be an odd matrix germ, and let
\[
F^{\new}(z):=a(z)\,P(z)\,F(z)\,P(z)^{-1}
\]
with \(a,P\) as in Definition~\ref{def:normalization-gauge}. Then
\[
A_3^{\new}=A_3,
\qquad
A_5^{\new}=A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3].
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Expand
\[
P(z)^{-1}=I-B_2 z^2+O(z^4).
\]
Then
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
(I+B_2 z^2)\,(A_3 z^3+A_5 z^5)\,(I-B_2 z^2)+O(z^7),
\]
so
\[
P(z)F(z)P(z)^{-1}
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7).
\]
Multiplying by
\[
a(z)=1+c_2 z^2+O(z^4)
\]
gives
\[
F^{\new}(z)
=
A_3 z^3+\bigl(A_5+c_2A_3+[B_2,A_3]\bigr)z^5+O(z^7),
\]
as claimed.
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Residual effect of normalization gauge]
\label{cor:first-residual-gauge-law}
Let \(\ell:=\mathbf C A_3\). Under the normalization gauge,
the quintic coefficient changes by
\[
A_5 \longmapsto A_5 + c_2 A_3 + [B_2,A_3].
\]
Hence its class in the residual quotient modulo the leading line satisfies
\[
[A_5] \longmapsto [A_5+[B_2,A_3}]
\qquad\text{in}\qquad
\mathcal J_5/\ell.
\]
In particular, scalar renormalization is invisible in the first residual
quotient.
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
This is immediate from Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-gauge-law}, since
\(c_2A_3\in \ell\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Trace invariance under normalization gauge]
\label{cor:trace-invariant-under-normalization-gauge}
If \(A_3\) is diagonal-traceless, then every commutator \([B_2,A_3]\) is
traceless. Consequently
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_5^{\new})=\operatorname{tr}(A_5)
\]
for every normalization-gauge transform \(F^{\new}\) of \(F\).
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
The scalar renormalization term \(c_2A_3\) is traceless because \(A_3\) is.
The commutator term is traceless because
\[
\operatorname{tr}[B_2,A_3]=0.
\]
Hence Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-gauge-law} gives
\[
\operatorname{tr}(A_5^{\new})=\operatorname{tr}(A_5).
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[What remains at quintic order]
\label{rem:quintic-pure-value-bottleneck}
The normalization gauge is therefore harmless for the first residual class.
The only remaining obstruction to using trace as a residual detector is the
pure value quintic slice \(V_{\val}^{(5)}\). To make the residual-trace
argument fully rigorous, it suffices to prove
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0
\qquad (h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}),
\]
or more weakly that trace annihilates the image of \(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\) in
the residual quotient.
\end{remark}
```

## 3. Replace the current overstrong first-residual proposition

### Find this exact block
It starts at
```latex
\begin{proposition}[Nontrivial first residual class]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-nontrivial}
```

and ends at its `\end{proof}`.

### Replace the entire block with this

```latex
\begin{proposition}[Generic first residual nonvanishing under trace-free quintic value slice]
\label{prop:first-residual-class-generic}
Assume that for every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\),
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0.
\]
Then there exists an open dense subset
\[
\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\subset \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}
\]
such that
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\qquad\text{in }Q/\ell_h
\]
for every \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).

Equivalently, on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) the quintic quotient coefficient
is not collinear with the cubic leading line:
\[
v_1(h)\notin \ell_h.
\]
Hence the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant on
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, for \(h\in\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\)
the cubic quotient coefficient
\[
v_0(h)=[J_3^{\pair}(h)]
\]
is nonzero and defines the leading line \(\ell_h\), and this line is
diagonal-traceless.

By Corollary~\ref{cor:trace-invariant-under-normalization-gauge}, trace is
invariant under the normalization gauge at the first residual step. By the
present hypothesis, trace also annihilates the pure value quintic slice
\(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\). Therefore trace descends to a well-defined linear
functional
\[
\tau_h:Q/\ell_h\to \mathbf C.
\]

By Lemma~\ref{lem:no-cancellation-quintic-trace},
\[
\tau_h(\overline v_1(h))
=
\operatorname{tr}J_5^{\pair}(h)
\not\equiv 0
\]
as a function of \(h\) on \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Since the family is
real-analytic in \(h\), the zero set of
\[
h\longmapsto \tau_h(\overline v_1(h))
\]
is a proper analytic subset of \(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}\). Its complement
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) is therefore open and dense.

For \(h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\) we have
\[
\tau_h(\overline v_1(h))\neq 0,
\]
hence
\[
\overline v_1(h)\neq 0
\qquad\text{in }Q/\ell_h.
\]
Thus the one-pair quotient odd germ is not projectively constant on
\(\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: trace-free pure value quintic slice]
\label{rem:wip-trace-free-pure-value-quintic}
The remaining local task is to prove
\[
\operatorname{tr}\bigl(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\bigr)=0
\qquad (h\in \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}),
\]
or more weakly that trace annihilates the image of \(V_{\val}^{(5)}(h)\) in
the residual quotient. Once this is established,
Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-class-generic}
gives a fully rigorous open-dense nonvanishing theorem for the first residual
class.
\end{remark}
```

This replacement is necessary because the current proposition still claims pointwise nonvanishing from a proof that only establishes non-identical trace nonvanishing. The conversation explicitly identified that as an overreach. turn87file11

### Insert this exact block

```latex
\subsection{Fixed-sector refinement at quintic and septic order}

The trace witness at quintic order suggests a more structured refinement of the
one-pair residual germ than the trace alone records. For bookkeeping purposes,
it is convenient to decompose the quintic and septic coefficients into fixed and
anti-fixed parts with respect to the involution
\[
\iota(A):=J_0AJ_0,
\qquad
J_0=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\
1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Write
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A):=\frac12(A+J_0AJ_0),
\qquad
\pi_{\mathfrak a}(A):=\frac12(A-J_0AJ_0).
\]
Then
\[
\mathfrak f=\operatorname{span}\{I,S\},
\qquad
\mathfrak a=\operatorname{span}\{D,J\},
\]
where
\[
I=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&1
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
S=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&1\\1&0
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
D=
\begin{pmatrix}
1&0\\0&-1
\end{pmatrix},
\quad
J=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&-1\\1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

\begin{definition}[Fixed-sector quintic and septic coefficients]
\label{def:fixed-sector-quintic-septic}
Let
\[
F_h(z)=A_3(h)z^3+A_5(h)z^5+A_7(h)z^7+\cdots
\]
denote the normalized one-pair quotient odd germ on the cubic-generic region.
Its fixed-sector quintic and septic coefficients are defined by
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h):=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_5(h)),
\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h):=\pi_{\mathfrak f}(A_7(h)).
\]
Write
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)\,I+v_5(h)\,S,
\qquad
A_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_7(h)\,I+v_7(h)\,S.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{definition}[Second residual determinant]
\label{def:second-residual-determinant}
On the cubic-generic region, define
\[
\Delta_7(h):=u_7(h)v_5(h)-u_5(h)v_7(h).
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{remark}[Derived fixed-sector identities]
\label{rem:derived-fixed-sector-identities}
The working analysis gives the following identities, which should be proved in
a later draft in the paper's native coefficient language.

\begin{enumerate}
\item If
\[
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha&\beta\\\beta&\gamma
\end{pmatrix},
\]
then the quintic fixed-sector \(S\)-component coming from the commutator-type
channel is
\[
v_5=\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]

\item The direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven contribution to the septic
fixed-sector coefficient is \(I\)-directional. In particular, on that slice,
\[
v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\qquad
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]
Thus the determinant \(\Delta_7\) is the natural second residual scalar once
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) has been fixed.
\end{enumerate}
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Work in progress: normalized affine one-pair invariant curve]
\label{rem:wip-normalized-affine-one-pair-curve}
The later working analysis suggests the normalized affine chart
\[
x(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}
\]
on the cubic-generic region. The intended geometric program is to study the
planar curve
\[
h\longmapsto (x(h),y(h))
\]
rather than only the scalar quintic trace. In this formulation, the remaining
generic obstruction is reduced to common four-secant lines of this planar
curve.
\end{remark}
```

This block ensures that \(u_5,v_5,u_7,v_7,\Delta_7\) are actually in the draft and not just in the conversation. turn87file7

```latex
\overline v_1(h)\in Q/\ell_h
```

### Insert this exact block

```latex
\begin{remark}[Work in progress: planar convexity reformulation]
\label{rem:wip-planar-convexity-reformulation}
The later working analysis suggests that the remaining generic obstruction
should be reformulated as a planar geometry problem for the curve
\[
h\longmapsto \left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]
On the open set where
\[
c(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
\frac{d}{dh}\!\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)}\right)\neq 0,
\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
x'(h) & y'(h)\\
x''(h) & y''(h)
\end{pmatrix}\neq 0,
\]
with
\[
x(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
y(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)},
\]
the image breaks into strictly convex graph components. On each such component,
a line meets the curve in at most two points. The remaining generic global
obstruction is therefore reduced to the possibility of a common four-secant
line meeting two separated convex components twice each.

In this formulation, the unresolved endgame is no longer local quintic
bookkeeping but endpoint/support-function geometry for the planar curve
\((v_5/c,\Delta_7/c)\).
\end{remark}
```

with

I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},\quad

J=\begin{pmatrix}0&-1\\1&0\end{pmatrix}.

The septic ambiguity law is

Projecting to \(\mathfrak f\), and using
\[
A_3\in \mathfrak a,\qquad [\mathfrak f,\mathfrak a]\subset \mathfrak a,\qquad [\mathfrak f,\mathfrak f]=0,
\]
gives
\[

\]

\[
[A_7^{\mathfrak f}]\in \mathfrak f/\mathbf C A_5^{\mathfrak f},
\]
exactly as we wanted. turn105file9

Now isolate the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice.

The raw linear \(q^{(7)}\) input first appears in the degree-5 same-point block as the lower-right diagonal perturbation
\[
E_{22}=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix}
=\frac12 I-\frac12 D.
\]
Since the linearized inverse-square-root map at a diagonal base preserves diagonality, the induced \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of \(X_5\) is also diagonal, so it has the form
\[
X_5^{(q^{(7)})}=u\,I+a\,D
\]
for some generically nonzero scalars \(u,a\). Meanwhile the first-order \(\eta_2\)-slice is symmetric off-diagonal:
\[
X_1^{(\eta_2)}=v\,S.
\]

Now compute the natural septic commutator channel:
\[
X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5.
\]
Using
\[
JS=-D,\qquad SJ=D,\qquad D^2=I,
\]
one gets
\[
(uI+aD)J(vS)-(vS)J(uI+aD)
=
-2v(uD+aI).
\]
Projecting to \(\mathfrak f\), the fixed-sector part is therefore
\[
-2av\,I.
\]
So the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven fixed-sector contribution from the \(X_5/X_1\) channel is **purely \(I\)-directional**. \]
\[
A_7^{\mathfrak f}\mapsto A_7^{\mathfrak f}+c_2A_5^{\mathfrak f},

and on the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice,

\qquad

Yes. We can now derive the **explicit septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector pair** in coefficient language.

That is the right thing to do next, because the conversation already fixed the septic transport pattern
\[
J_{7,K_1}^{\pair}(h)
\sim
X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0
+
X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5
+
X_2JX_4-X_4JX_2,
\]
and it already identified the direct \(q^{(7)}\) witness as living in the \(X_5/X_1\) channel, with the \(X_6\) channel as the only remaining competitor. turn107file1

Here is the clean algebra.

Write the symmetric coefficient matrices as
\[
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u & v\\
v & w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_2=
\begin{pmatrix}
a & b\\
b & c
\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[
X_4=
\begin{pmatrix}
p & r\\
r & s
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_5=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha_5 & \beta_5\\
\beta_5 & \gamma_5
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_6=
\begin{pmatrix}
\mu_6 & \nu_6\\
\nu_6 & \lambda_6
\end{pmatrix},
\]
and
\[
X_0=\diag(x_1,x_2),
\qquad
J=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&-1\\
1&0
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Now use the fixed-sector basis
\[
I=\begin{pmatrix}1&0\\0&1\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
S=\begin{pmatrix}0&1\\1&0\end{pmatrix},
\]
so for any symmetric matrix
\[
M=\begin{pmatrix}A&B\\B&C\end{pmatrix}
\]
its fixed-sector part is
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(M)=\frac{A+C}{2}\,I+B\,S.
\]

For two symmetric matrices
\[
X=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\b&c\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
Y=\begin{pmatrix}p&q\\q&r\end{pmatrix},
\]
a direct calculation gives
\[
XJY-YJX
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2aq+2bp & -ar+cp\\
-ar+cp & -2br+2cq
\end{pmatrix},
\]
hence
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(XJY-YJX)
=
\bigl(b(p-r)+q(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-ar)S.
\]

Apply this to the three septic \(K_1\)-transport channels.

First,
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5)
=
\bigl(\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w)S.
\]

Second,
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_2JX_4-X_4JX_2)
=
\bigl(b(p-s)+r(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-as)S.
\]

Third, a direct computation with \(X_0=\diag(x_1,x_2)\) gives
\[
X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2x_1\nu_6 & -x_1\lambda_6+x_2\mu_6\\
-x_1\lambda_6+x_2\mu_6 & 2x_2\nu_6
\end{pmatrix},
\]
so
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0)
=
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6\,I
+
(x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6)S.
\]

Therefore, if we define the septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector coefficient by
\[
A_{7,K_1}^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_{7,K_1}(h)\,I+v_{7,K_1}(h)\,S,
\]
then
\[
u_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6
+
\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)
+
b(p-s)+r(c-a)
\Bigr),
\]
\[
v_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6
+
\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w
+
cp-as
\Bigr).
\]

That is the full explicit septic \(K_1\)-transport pair in coefficient language.

Now specialize to the **direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice** discussed in the conversation.

On that slice:

- the \(X_5\) contribution comes from the linear \(G_5\)-input and is diagonal, so
  \[
  \beta_5^{(q^{(7)})}=0;
  \]
- the \(X_1\) contribution is the off-diagonal \(\eta_2\)-slice;
- the mixed \(X_6\)-slice is off-diagonal on that grading, so
  \[
  \mu_6^{(q^{(7)})}=\lambda_6^{(q^{(7)})}=0;
  \]
- the \(X_2/X_4\) channel is not on the pure \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(7)})\) slice.

So on the direct septic witness slice,
\[
v_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\]
and
\[
u_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6^{(q^{(7)})}
+
v\,(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)^{(q^{(7)})}
\Bigr).
\]

This recovers the earlier structural conclusion that the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven fixed-sector contribution is \(I\)-directional, and therefore
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}
=
u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]

So the honest state is now:

- we **can** write the septic \(K_1\)-transport pair \((u_{7,K_1},v_{7,K_1})\) explicitly;
- this is enough to make the second residual determinant \(\Delta_7\) concrete at the level of the local transport algebra;
- what we still do **not** have is a proof that **no other order-7 sector** contributes additional fixed-sector terms that must be added to these formulas.

So this is not yet the full total \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) of the normalized quotient odd germ, but it is the full explicit \(K_1\)-transport septic contribution, and that is the part the conversation had isolated as the actual second residual witness. turn107file1

### Replace the entire block with this

```latex
\begin{proposition}[Explicit fixed-sector formulas at quintic order]
\label{prop:explicit-quintic-fixed-sector}
Write
\[
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\
v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha&\beta\\
\beta&\gamma
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Then the fixed-sector part of the basic quintic commutator channel
\[
X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3
\]
is
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3)
=
\bigl(v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma u-\alpha w)S.
\]
Consequently, for the quintic fixed-sector coefficient
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)\,I+v_5(h)\,S,
\]
the commutator-type contribution is explicitly
\[
u_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}
=
v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w),
\qquad
v_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}
=
\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
A direct multiplication gives
\[
X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2\alpha\beta+2\beta u & \gamma u-\alpha w\\[.5ex]
\gamma u-\alpha w & -2\beta w+2\gamma\beta
\end{pmatrix},
\]
and hence its fixed-sector projection is obtained by taking the average of the
diagonal entries for the \(I\)-component and the common off-diagonal entry for
the \(S\)-component:
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3)
=
\frac{(-2\alpha v+2\beta u)+(-2\beta w+2\gamma v)}{2}\,I
+
(\gamma u-\alpha w)S.
\]
This simplifies to
\[
\bigl(v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma u-\alpha w)S,
\]
as claimed.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Explicit septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector formulas]
\label{prop:explicit-septic-k1-fixed-sector}
Write
\[
X_0=\diag(x_1,x_2),
\qquad
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\
v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_2=
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\\
b&c
\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[
X_4=
\begin{pmatrix}
p&r\\
r&s
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_5=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha_5&\beta_5\\
\beta_5&\gamma_5
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_6=
\begin{pmatrix}
\mu_6&\nu_6\\
\nu_6&\lambda_6
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Then the fixed-sector parts of the three septic \(K_1\)-transport channels are
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5)
=
\bigl(\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w)S,
\]
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_2JX_4-X_4JX_2)
=
\bigl(b(p-s)+r(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-as)S,
\]
and
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0)
=
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6\,I
+
(x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6)S.
\]

Consequently, if the septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector coefficient is
written
\[
A_{7,K_1}^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_{7,K_1}(h)\,I+v_{7,K_1}(h)\,S,
\]
then
\[
u_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6
+
\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)
+
b(p-s)+r(c-a)
\Bigr),
\]
\[
v_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6
+
\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w
+
cp-as
\Bigr).
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
For two symmetric matrices
\[
X=
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\\
b&c
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
Y=
\begin{pmatrix}
p&q\\
q&r
\end{pmatrix},
\]
a direct calculation gives
\[
XJY-YJX
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2aq+2bp & -ar+cp\\
-ar+cp & -2br+2cq
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Taking fixed-sector projection yields
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(XJY-YJX)
=
\bigl(b(p-r)+q(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-ar)S.
\]
Applying this formula to \((X,Y)=(X_5,X_1)\) and \((X,Y)=(X_2,X_4)\) gives the
first two displayed identities.

For the \(X_0/X_6\) channel, a direct multiplication gives
\[
X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2x_1\nu_6 & -x_1\lambda_6+x_2\mu_6\\
-x_1\lambda_6+x_2\mu_6 & 2x_2\nu_6
\end{pmatrix},
\]
whose fixed-sector part is
\[
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6\,I
+
(x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6)S.
\]
Summing the three channels gives the stated formulas for
\(u_{7,K_1}\) and \(v_{7,K_1}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven septic slice]
\label{cor:direct-q7-septic-slice}
On the direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice, the septic \(K_1\)-transport
fixed-sector contribution is \(I\)-directional. More precisely,
\[
v_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\]
and
\[
u_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6^{(q^{(7)})}
+
v\,(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)^{(q^{(7)})}
\Bigr),
\]
so in particular
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
On the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice, the linear \(X_5\)-input is diagonal,
hence
\[
\beta_5^{(q^{(7)})}=0.
\]
The mixed \(X_6\)-slice is off-diagonal on this grading, so
\[
\mu_6^{(q^{(7)})}=\lambda_6^{(q^{(7)})}=0.
\]
Moreover the \(X_2/X_4\)-channel does not lie on the pure
\((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(7)})\)-slice. Therefore the \(S\)-component vanishes and the
displayed formula for \(u_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}\) follows from
Proposition~\ref{prop:explicit-septic-k1-fixed-sector}. Since
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7,
\]
the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice satisfies
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Status of explicit fixed-sector formulas]
\label{rem:status-explicit-fixed-sector-formulas}
Proposition~\ref{prop:explicit-quintic-fixed-sector} gives an explicit
coefficient formula for the quintic commutator contribution to
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\), and
Proposition~\ref{prop:explicit-septic-k1-fixed-sector} gives an explicit
formula for the septic \(K_1\)-transport contribution to
\(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\). What is still not known in full explicit form is the
total septic fixed-sector coefficient after all order-7 sectors are included.
Thus the formulas above should be read as the explicit transport-level
contribution underlying the second residual determinant \(\Delta_7\), not yet
as a closed formula for the full total pair \((u_7,v_7)\).
\end{remark}
```

\qquad

b&c
\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[

r&s
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad

\beta_5&\gamma_5

\end{pmatrix}.

\[

=
-\,X_6K_1X_0+X_5K_1X_1-X_4K_1X_2+X_3K_1X_3-X_2K_1X_4+X_1K_1X_5-X_0K_1X_6.

Since \(K_1=\kappa J\), this becomes the commutator-style combination
\[

=

X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0
+
X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5
+
X_2JX_4-X_4JX_2

for symmetric \(X_3\). In fact, more strongly,

so its fixed-sector projection is zero. Thus the fixed-sector part comes only from the first three channels. turn110file0

Now for symmetric
\[
X=\begin{pmatrix}a&b\\ b&c\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
Y=\begin{pmatrix}p&q\\ q&r\end{pmatrix},
\]
one computes
\[
XJY-YJX
=
\begin{pmatrix}

-ar+cp & -2br+2cq
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Projecting to
\[
\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}
\]
gives
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(XJY-YJX)
=
\bigl(b(p-r)+q(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-ar)S.
\]

Applying this to the septic \(K_1\) channels yields
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5)

+
(cp-as)S,

=
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6\,I
+
(x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6)S.

+
cp-as
\Bigr).

```latex
\begin{proposition}[Explicit fixed-sector formulas at quintic order]
\label{prop:explicit-quintic-fixed-sector}
Write
\[
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\
v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_3=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha&\beta\\
\beta&\gamma
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Then the fixed-sector part of the basic quintic commutator channel
\[
X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3
\]
is
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3)
=
\bigl(v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma u-\alpha w)S.
\]
Consequently, for the quintic fixed-sector coefficient
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)\,I+v_5(h)\,S,
\]
the commutator-type contribution is explicitly
\[
u_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}
=
v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w),
\qquad
v_5^{(\mathrm{comm})}
=
\gamma u-\alpha w.
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
A direct computation gives
\[
X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2\alpha v+2\beta u & -\alpha w+\gamma u\\[.5ex]
-\alpha w+\gamma u & -2\beta w+2\gamma v
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
The fixed-sector projection of a symmetric matrix
\[
\begin{pmatrix}
A&B\\
B&C
\end{pmatrix}
\]
is
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}
\begin{pmatrix}
A&B\\
B&C
\end{pmatrix}
=
\frac{A+C}{2}\,I+B\,S.
\]
Applying this to the displayed matrix gives
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_3JX_1-X_1JX_3)
=
\frac{(-2\alpha v+2\beta u)+(-2\beta w+2\gamma v)}{2}\,I
+
(\gamma u-\alpha w)S,
\]
which simplifies to
\[
\bigl(v(\gamma-\alpha)+\beta(u-w)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma u-\alpha w)S.
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[Explicit septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector formulas]
\label{prop:explicit-septic-k1-fixed-sector}
Write
\[
X_0=\diag(x_1,x_2),
\qquad
X_1=
\begin{pmatrix}
u&v\\
v&w
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_2=
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\\
b&c
\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[
X_4=
\begin{pmatrix}
p&r\\
r&s
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_5=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha_5&\beta_5\\
\beta_5&\gamma_5
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
X_6=
\begin{pmatrix}
\mu_6&\nu_6\\
\nu_6&\lambda_6
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Then the fixed-sector parts of the three septic \(K_1\)-transport channels are
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_5JX_1-X_1JX_5)
=
\bigl(\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)\bigr)I
+
(\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w)S,
\]
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_2JX_4-X_4JX_2)
=
\bigl(b(p-s)+r(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-as)S,
\]
and
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0)
=
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6\,I
+
(x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6)S.
\]

Consequently, if the septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector coefficient is
written
\[
A_{7,K_1}^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_{7,K_1}(h)\,I+v_{7,K_1}(h)\,S,
\]
then
\[
u_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6
+
\beta_5(u-w)+v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)
+
b(p-s)+r(c-a)
\Bigr),
\]
\[
v_{7,K_1}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6
+
\gamma_5u-\alpha_5w
+
cp-as
\Bigr).
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
For symmetric matrices
\[
X=
\begin{pmatrix}
a&b\\
b&c
\end{pmatrix},
\qquad
Y=
\begin{pmatrix}
p&q\\
q&r
\end{pmatrix},
\]
a direct computation gives
\[
XJY-YJX
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2aq+2bp & -ar+cp\\
-ar+cp & -2br+2cq
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
Projecting to the fixed sector yields
\[
\pi_{\mathfrak f}(XJY-YJX)
=
\bigl(b(p-r)+q(c-a)\bigr)I
+
(cp-ar)S.
\]
Applying this formula to \((X,Y)=(X_5,X_1)\) and \((X,Y)=(X_2,X_4)\) gives the
first two displayed identities.

For the \(X_0/X_6\) channel, one computes directly
\[
X_0JX_6-X_6JX_0
=
\begin{pmatrix}
-2x_1\nu_6 & -x_1\lambda_6+x_2\mu_6\\
-x_1\lambda_6+x_2\mu_6 & 2x_2\nu_6
\end{pmatrix},
\]
whose fixed-sector projection is
\[
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6\,I
+
(x_2\mu_6-x_1\lambda_6)S.
\]
Summing the three fixed-sector contributions gives the stated formulas for
\(u_{7,K_1}\) and \(v_{7,K_1}\).
\end{proof}

\begin{corollary}[Direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven septic slice]
\label{cor:direct-q7-septic-slice}
On the direct linear \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice, the septic \(K_1\)-transport
fixed-sector contribution is \(I\)-directional. More precisely,
\[
v_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\]
and
\[
u_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}
=
\kappa\Bigl(
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6^{(q^{(7)})}
+
v\,(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)^{(q^{(7)})}
\Bigr).
\]
In particular,
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]
\end{corollary}

\begin{proof}
On the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice, the linear \(X_5\)-input is diagonal,
so
\[
\beta_5^{(q^{(7)})}=0.
\]
The mixed \(X_6\)-slice is off-diagonal on this grading, so
\[
\mu_6^{(q^{(7)})}=\lambda_6^{(q^{(7)})}=0.
\]
Moreover the \(X_2/X_4\)-channel does not lie on the pure
\((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(7)})\)-slice. Therefore the \(S\)-component vanishes and the
displayed formula for \(u_{7,K_1}^{(q^{(7)})}\) follows from
Proposition~\ref{prop:explicit-septic-k1-fixed-sector}. Since
\[
\Delta_7=u_7v_5-u_5v_7,
\]
the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice satisfies
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Status of explicit fixed-sector formulas]
\label{rem:status-explicit-fixed-sector-formulas}
Proposition~\ref{prop:explicit-quintic-fixed-sector} gives an explicit
coefficient formula for the quintic commutator contribution to
\(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\), and
Proposition~\ref{prop:explicit-septic-k1-fixed-sector} gives an explicit
formula for the septic \(K_1\)-transport contribution to
\(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\). What is still not known in full explicit form is the
total septic fixed-sector coefficient after all order-\(7\) sectors are
included. Thus the formulas above should be read as the explicit
transport-level contribution underlying the second residual determinant
\(\Delta_7\), not yet as a closed formula for the full total pair
\((u_7,v_7)\).
\end{remark}
```

So the attack can resume from the strengthened \(3/5/7\) geometry. turn114file5

The one important correction from the later conversation is this:

**the right order-7 scalar is not raw septic trace.**  
Septic trace is not gauge-invariant. The correct second residual scalar is the fixed-sector quotient scalar, which in coordinates is exactly \(\Delta_7\). Equivalently, if
\[
A_5^{\mathfrak f}=u_5I+v_5S,
\]
then the unique fixed-sector linear functional annihilating \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\) is represented by
\[
T_h:=v_5(h)\,I-u_5(h)\,S,
\]
since under the trace pairing on \(\mathfrak f=\span\{I,S\}\),
\[
\operatorname{tr}(T_hA_5^{\mathfrak f})=0.
\]
Then
\[
\sigma(h):=\operatorname{tr}\bigl(T_hA_7^{\mathfrak f}(h)\bigr)
=2\bigl(v_5u_7-u_5v_7\bigr)
=2\Delta_7(h).
\]
So \(\Delta_7\) is the canonical second residual scalar. turn114file4

That means the remaining local gap is no longer “what is the second invariant?” We now know the right one. The remaining local question is narrower:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove that }\Delta_7(h)\not\equiv 0\text{ generically on the cubic/quintic generic region.}
}
\]

And we can push that quite far already.

From the septic derivation we now have:
- the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice of \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\) is \(I\)-directional,
- hence
  \[
  v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
  \]
- and therefore
  \[
  \Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
  \]

So if the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient in the \(I\)-direction is generically nonzero, and \(v_5\not\equiv 0\), then \(\Delta_7\not\equiv 0\). That is the exact septic analogue of the quintic witness logic. turn114file1

So the remaining gap can now be stated cleanly:

### Remaining local gap
We still need a no-cancellation theorem for the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven \(I\)-slice of \(A_7^{\mathfrak f}\), or equivalently for \(\Delta_7\). Concretely:
- isolate the monomial
  \[
  \kappa(h)\,\eta_2(h)\,q^{(7)}(h)
  \]
  in the septic \(K_1\)-transport fixed-sector \(I\)-component;
- show the \(X_6\)-channel does not cancel that witness on the fixed-sector quotient.

That is the local bottleneck.

### Remaining global gap
Assuming \(\Delta_7\) is generically nonzero, the global obstruction is already clear:
study the normalized planar curve
\[
h\longmapsto \left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
and rule out the remaining bad-core configurations via its convex/secant geometry. turn113file6

So yes, we can continue the attack, and the next exact move is:

---

## Continue the attack

Define the normalized scalars
\[
\rho_1(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
\rho_2(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}.
\]

The point of these is:

- \(\rho_1\) is the first residual direction normalized by the cubic line,
- \(\rho_2\) is the second residual direction normalized by the cubic line,
- and \(\rho_2\) is canonically defined because \(\Delta_7\) is.

Now the key structural claim we want is:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_2 \text{ is genuinely new, i.e. it is not an affine function of }\rho_1.
}
\]

Why this should hold:

- \(c\) depends only on jets up to order \(3\),
- \(v_5\) is quintic and first sees the \(q^{(5)}\)-slice,
- \(\Delta_7\) first sees the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice.

So \(\rho_1\) only sees up to the \(5\)-jet, while \(\rho_2\) sees the \(7\)-jet. Therefore an affine relation
\[
\rho_2=A\rho_1+B
\]
would force the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of \(\rho_2\) to vanish, contradicting the septic witness once established. This is exactly the triangularity argument the conversation was already using for the two-pair coincidence map. turn113file10

That gives the next exact theorem target:

\[
\boxed{
\text{On the generic region, } \rho_2 \not\equiv A\rho_1+B.
}
\]

And then define the coincidence map
\[
G(h_1,h_2):=
\bigl(\rho_1(h_1)-\rho_1(h_2),\ \rho_2(h_1)-\rho_2(h_2)\bigr).
\]
If \(\rho_1,\rho_2\) are not affinely dependent, then the Jacobian determinant
\[
\Delta(h_1,h_2):=
\rho_1'(h_1)\rho_2'(h_2)-\rho_2'(h_1)\rho_1'(h_2)
\]
is not identically zero, so off-diagonal coincidence points are generically isolated. That is the precise two-pair rigidity upgrade we were aiming for. turn113file10

So the attack is now properly back on track.

The exact next mathematical subproblem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove the direct septic witness } \Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5 \text{ is generically nonzero.}
}
\]

That is now the local theorem that unlocks the second scalar \(\rho_2\), and after that the two-pair map \(G\) becomes the right global object.

The clean way to proceed is to isolate the \(I\)-component of the septic \(K_1\)-transport block on the \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(7)})\)-graded slice and show the \(X_6\)-channel cannot cancel it.

Nothing happened to it — that is still the **global endgame**.

We temporarily zoomed back into the septic layer because without a genuinely new second scalar, the secant/tangent geometry has no teeth. The planar curve story needs two independent normalized coordinates:
\[

\]

The secant/tangent attack was built on the curve
\[
h\longmapsto (\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h))
=
\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

So the logic is:

1. **Local layer:** prove \(\Delta_7\) is real and generically nonzero, and not affinely dependent on \(v_5\).  
   This is why we came back to order 7.

2. **Then global layer:** use that curve’s geometry:
   - tangent map,
   - secant map,
   - convex components,
   - common 4-secant obstruction,
   - endpoint/support-function geometry.

So secant/tangent was never abandoned. We just stepped back one level because the second coordinate of the curve still needed to be secured.

The clean hierarchy now is:

- **Order 3:** cubic line \(c(h)\).
- **Order 5:** first normalized scalar \(\rho_1=v_5/c\).
- **Order 7:** second normalized scalar \(\rho_2=\Delta_7/c\).
- **After that:** resume the secant/tangent/convexity attack on
  \[
  h\mapsto (\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h)).
  \]

and finish the secant/tangent program:

just one order higher.

Here is that computation on the minimal graded slice.

Take the diagonal baseline
\[
G_0=\diag(a^2,b^2),\qquad X_0=\diag(1/a,1/b),
\]
an off-diagonal first-order perturbation
\[
A=\begin{pmatrix}0&u\\ u&0\end{pmatrix}
\]
for the \(\eta_2\)-slice, and a diagonal fifth-order perturbation
\[
E=\begin{pmatrix}0&0\\ 0&e\end{pmatrix}
\]
for the \(q^{(7)}\)-slice. Expand
\[
X(\varepsilon,\delta)
=
(G_0+\varepsilon A+\delta E)^{-1/2}
=
X_0+\varepsilon U+\delta Y+\varepsilon\delta Z+\cdots.
\]

Solving
\[
X(\varepsilon,\delta)\,(G_0+\varepsilon A+\delta E)\,X(\varepsilon,\delta)=I
\]
to the required orders gives
\[
U=
\begin{pmatrix}
0 & -\dfrac{u}{ab(a+b)}\\[1.2ex]
-\dfrac{u}{ab(a+b)} & 0
\end{pmatrix},
\]
\[
Y=
\begin{pmatrix}
0&0\\[.5ex]
0&-\dfrac{e}{2b^3}
\end{pmatrix},
\]
and
\[
Z_{12}
=
\frac{eu(a+2b)}{2ab^3(a+b)^2}.
\]

Interpretation:

- \(U_{12}\) is the direct \(\eta_2\)-slice of \(X_1\),
- \(Y_{22}-Y_{11}\) is the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of the diagonal-traceless part of \(X_5\),
- \(Z_{12}\) is the mixed \((\eta_2,q^{(7)})\)-slice of \(X_6\).

So on the pure \((K_1,\eta_2,q^{(7)})\)-graded slice, the two competing terms in the septic \(K_1\)-transport \(I\)-component are exactly
\[
(x_2-x_1)\,\nu_6^{(q^{(7)})}
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
v\,(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)^{(q^{(7)})},
\]
with
\[
x_1=\frac1a,\qquad x_2=\frac1b,\qquad
\nu_6^{(q^{(7)})}=Z_{12},\qquad
v=U_{12},\qquad
(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)^{(q^{(7)})}=Y_{22}-Y_{11}.
\]

Substituting the computed coefficients gives
\[
(x_2-x_1)\nu_6^{(q^{(7)})}
+
v(\gamma_5-\alpha_5)^{(q^{(7)})}
=
\frac{eu\,(a^2+ab-b^2)}{a^2b^4(a+b)^2}.
\]

That is not identically zero. It can vanish on a special codimension-one locus
\[
a^2+ab-b^2=0,
\]
but generically it is nonzero. So the direct septic witness survives. In particular,
\[
u_7^{(q^{(7)})}\not\equiv 0
\]
generically on the quintic-generic region.

Since the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice is \(I\)-directional, we still have
\[
v_7^{(q^{(7)})}=0,
\qquad
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5,

so on the region where \(v_5\neq 0\),

generically as well. This is exactly the second residual scalar we needed. turn115file10

That is the triangularity we need. turn115file10

So the next theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\rho_2 \not\equiv A\rho_1+B
\quad\text{for any constants }A,B.
}
\]

Why this is now the correct statement:

- \(c\) is controlled by the cubic layer;
- \(v_5\) first sees the quintic layer;
- \(\Delta_7\) first sees the septic second residual layer;
- and the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of \(\Delta_7\) is generically nonzero, while \(v_5\) has no direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice.

Therefore any affine relation
\[
\Delta_7 = A v_5 + B c
\]
would force the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-coefficient of the left-hand side to vanish, contradiction. Dividing by \(c\) gives the displayed non-affinity of \(\rho_2\).

That already gives a real global gain: the curve
\[
\Gamma(h):=(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h))
\]
is not contained in an affine line.

Now define the secant-coincidence map
\[
\Sigma(h_1,h_2):=\Gamma(h_2)-\Gamma(h_1)
=
\bigl(\rho_1(h_2)-\rho_1(h_1),\ \rho_2(h_2)-\rho_2(h_1)\bigr).
\]

A two-pair coincidence for the strengthened invariant data is exactly a projective equality of such secants:
\[
[\Sigma(h_1,h_2)] = [\Sigma(k_1,k_2)].
\]

So the real local-to-global derivative object is
\[
D(h_1,h_2)
:=
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
\rho_1'(h_1) & \rho_1'(h_2) & \rho_1(h_2)-\rho_1(h_1)\\
\rho_2'(h_1) & \rho_2'(h_2) & \rho_2(h_2)-\rho_2(h_1)
\end{pmatrix},
\]
which in planar language is the condition for the secant-direction map to be immersive. More transparently, near the diagonal \(h_2=h_1+\varepsilon\), immersion is controlled by the curvature numerator
\[
K_\Gamma(h)
:=
\rho_1'(h)\rho_2''(h)-\rho_2'(h)\rho_1''(h).
\]

So the next exact target is:

\[
\boxed{
K_\Gamma \not\equiv 0.
}
\]

If that holds, then on an open dense set the curve \(\Gamma\) breaks into strictly convex graph components. On each such component:

- tangent slope is strictly monotone;
- a line meets the curve in at most two points;
- and the remaining bad-core obstruction reduces to a common 4-secant line meeting two separated convex components twice each.

That is exactly the secant/tangent program we had before — but now it is finally attached to the correct second scalar.

So the attack now splits cleanly into two subproblems.

### 1. Prove nonzero curvature for \(\Gamma\)

This is the next local/global bridge:
\[
K_\Gamma(h)=\rho_1'(h)\rho_2''(h)-\rho_2'(h)\rho_1''(h).
\]

The right heuristic is the same jet-triangularity as before:

- \(\rho_1\) is governed first by the quintic slice,
- \(\rho_2\) is governed first by the septic slice,
- so \(K_\Gamma\) should carry a direct \(q^{(7)}\)-term coming from differentiating \(\rho_2\) that cannot be canceled by the lower-order structure of \(\rho_1\).

If that works, then \(\Gamma\) is generically not just non-affine but genuinely curved.

### 2. Then return to convex components and 4-secants

Once \(K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0\), define the convex-component region by
\[
\rho_1'(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
K_\Gamma(h)\neq 0.
\]

On each connected component of that set, \(\Gamma\) is a strictly convex graph. Then:

- adjacent \(K_\Gamma=0\) boundaries are harmless by tangent-slope separation;
- generic \(\rho_1'=0\) turning points are locally harmless by the same normal-form argument as before;
- and the remaining obstruction is a line meeting two separated convex components twice each.

So the remaining gap is now exactly what it should be:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove }K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0,\text{ then finish the common 4-secant exclusion.}
}
\]

That is the right continuation of the attack. The septic layer is no longer the main issue; it has now supplied the second coordinate that the secant/tangent geometry needed.

The next thing I would do is attack \(K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0\) by the same triangularity logic we just used for \(\rho_2\not\equiv A\rho_1+B\): isolate the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice in \(\rho_2\), differentiate, and show it cannot be absorbed by the lower-order jet structure of \(\rho_1\).

I’m attacking \(K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0\) directly now. The key is to use the same jet-triangularity that made \(\rho_2\) genuinely new, and show that after differentiating, the direct septic slice in \(\rho_2\) still cannot be absorbed by the lower-order structure coming from \(\rho_1\).

_Thought for 14s_

Here is the next real push.

Set
\[
\Gamma(h)=\bigl(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h)\bigr)
=
\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
and define the curvature numerator
\[
K_\Gamma(h):=\rho_1'(h)\rho_2''(h)-\rho_2'(h)\rho_1''(h).
\]

We want to show
\[
\boxed{K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0.}
\]

The clean way to attack it is to write everything over the common denominator \(c^4\).

Let
\[
M:=cv_5'-c'v_5,
\qquad
N:=c\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7.
\]
Then
\[
\rho_1'=\frac{M}{c^2},
\qquad
\rho_2'=\frac{N}{c^2}.
\]
Differentiating once,
\[
\rho_1''=\frac{Mc'-?}{}
\]
but the standard cancellation gives
\[
\rho_1''=\frac{M'c-2Mc'}{c^3},
\qquad
\rho_2''=\frac{N'c-2Nc'}{c^3}.
\]
So
\[
K_\Gamma
=
\frac{M}{c^2}\frac{N'c-2Nc'}{c^3}
-
\frac{N}{c^2}\frac{M'c-2Mc'}{c^3}
=
\frac{MN'-NM'}{c^4}.
\]
Thus the real target is
\[
\boxed{L:=MN'-NM'\not\equiv 0.}
\]

That is a better formulation, because it makes the structure transparent: \(K_\Gamma\) vanishes identically exactly when the logarithmic derivatives of \(M\) and \(N\) agree, i.e. when \(N/M\) is constant on each connected component where \(M\neq 0\). But
\[
\frac{N}{M}=\frac{\rho_2'}{\rho_1'}=\frac{d\rho_2}{d\rho_1}.
\]
So \(L\equiv 0\) would mean that \(\rho_2\) is locally affine in \(\rho_1\):
\[
\rho_2=A\rho_1+B.
\]
And that is exactly what we already argued should fail by jet-triangularity, because \(\rho_2\) has a direct septic witness while \(\rho_1\) does not.

So the attack can be closed in one of two equivalent ways.

First route: direct contradiction from \(L\equiv 0\).

Assume \(L\equiv 0\). Then on any open set where \(M\neq 0\),
\[
\left(\frac{N}{M}\right)'=\frac{MN'-NM'}{M^2}=0,
\]
so
\[
\frac{N}{M}=A
\]
for some constant \(A\). Hence
\[
\rho_2'=A\rho_1',
\]
so
\[
\rho_2=A\rho_1+B.
\]
Equivalently,
\[
\Delta_7=Av_5+Bc.
\]
But the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of \(\Delta_7\) is generically nonzero, while \(v_5\) and \(c\) do not contain that direct septic slice. Contradiction.

So provided we accept that triangularity statement, this proves
\[
\boxed{L\not\equiv 0.}
\]

That is already a real theorem.

Second route: isolate the direct septic slice in \(L\).

This is slightly more concrete. The first place \(\Delta_7\) sees the genuine second residual witness is the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice:
\[
\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}=u_7^{(q^{(7)})}v_5.
\]
So
\[
N=c\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7
\]
inherits a direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice
\[
N^{(q^{(7)})}=c(\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})})'-c'(\Delta_7^{(q^{(7)})}),
\]
while
\[
M=cv_5'-c'v_5
\]
has no direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice of that type. Therefore in
\[
L=MN'-NM'
\]
the term
\[
M\,(N^{(q^{(7)})})'
-
N^{(q^{(7)})}M'
\]
is a genuine septic-leading contribution that cannot be canceled by lower-order pieces coming only from \(c\) and \(v_5\). This is the differential version of the same triangularity argument.

I think the first route is cleaner and stronger: it shows that the entire curvature problem reduces to the already-motivated non-affinity
\[
\rho_2\not\equiv A\rho_1+B.
\]

So the next theorem is now basically:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If the direct septic witness in }\Delta_7\text{ is generically nonzero, then }K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0.
}
\]

And once that is in hand, the secant/tangent program restarts exactly where it had paused:

- on the open set where
  \[
  \rho_1'\neq 0,\qquad K_\Gamma\neq 0,
  \]
  the curve \(\Gamma\) is a strictly convex graph;
- each connected component contributes at most two support points to a bad core;
- adjacent \(K_\Gamma=0\) boundaries are harmless by tangent-slope separation;
- generic \(\rho_1'=0\) turning points are locally harmless by the same normal-form argument as before;
- so the remaining obstruction is a common 4-secant line meeting two separated convex components twice each.

That is now the exact battlefield again.

So the attack result is:

\[
\boxed{
K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0
\text{ reduces to }
\rho_2\not\equiv A\rho_1+B,
\text{ and that in turn reduces to the direct septic witness in }\Delta_7.
}
\]

The next thing to do is to write this as a formal proposition in the paper, because it is the bridge from the order-7 local work back to the global secant/tangent geometry.

```latex
\subsection{From the one-pair residual germ to two-pair and minimal-core rigidity}
```

turn116file6turn116file10

So, **insert the following block immediately after**
```latex
\begin{remark}[Status of explicit fixed-sector formulas]
\label{rem:status-explicit-fixed-sector-formulas}
...
\end{remark}
```

and before the next subsection.

```latex
\subsection{Normalized planar curve and curvature bridge}

On the region where \(c(h)\neq 0\), define the normalized scalars
\[
\rho_1(h):=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},
\qquad
\rho_2(h):=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)},
\]
and the associated planar invariant curve
\[
\Gamma(h):=\bigl(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h)\bigr).
\]

\begin{proposition}[Non-affinity of the second normalized residual scalar]
\label{prop:rho2-not-affine-in-rho1}
Assume that the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice of \(\Delta_7(h)\) is
generically nonzero on the quintic-generic region. Then there do not exist
constants \(A,B\in\mathbf C\) such that
\[
\rho_2(h)\equiv A\,\rho_1(h)+B
\]
on any connected component of \(\{h:c(h)\neq 0\}\).

Equivalently,
\[
\Delta_7(h)\not\equiv A\,v_5(h)+B\,c(h).
\]
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Suppose
\[
\Delta_7(h)\equiv A\,v_5(h)+B\,c(h)
\]
on a connected component of \(\{c\neq 0\}\). Then the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven
slice of the right-hand side vanishes, because \(v_5\) and \(c\) are governed
by the cubic/quintic layers and carry no direct septic \(q^{(7)}\)-slice.
But the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice of \(\Delta_7\) is generically nonzero
by hypothesis. This is a contradiction. Dividing by \(c(h)\) yields
\[
\rho_2(h)\not\equiv A\,\rho_1(h)+B.
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Normalized derivative numerators]
\label{def:MN-normalized-curve}
Define
\[
M(h):=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h),
\qquad
N(h):=c(h)\Delta_7'(h)-c'(h)\Delta_7(h).
\]
Then
\[
\rho_1'(h)=\frac{M(h)}{c(h)^2},
\qquad
\rho_2'(h)=\frac{N(h)}{c(h)^2}.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{proposition}[Curvature bridge for the normalized planar curve]
\label{prop:curvature-bridge-normalized-curve}
Let
\[
K_\Gamma(h):=\rho_1'(h)\rho_2''(h)-\rho_2'(h)\rho_1''(h)
\]
denote the affine curvature numerator of the planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\).
Then
\[
K_\Gamma(h)=\frac{M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)}{c(h)^4}.
\]
In particular, if \(K_\Gamma\equiv 0\) on a connected open set on which
\(M\neq 0\), then \(\rho_2\) is affine in \(\rho_1\) on that set.
Consequently, under the hypothesis of
Proposition~\ref{prop:rho2-not-affine-in-rho1}, one has
\[
K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0
\]
on the generic region.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
By Definition~\ref{def:MN-normalized-curve},
\[
\rho_1'=\frac{M}{c^2},
\qquad
\rho_2'=\frac{N}{c^2}.
\]
Differentiating gives
\[
\rho_1''=\frac{M'c-2Mc'}{c^3},
\qquad
\rho_2''=\frac{N'c-2Nc'}{c^3}.
\]
Therefore
\[
K_\Gamma
=
\frac{M}{c^2}\frac{N'c-2Nc'}{c^3}
-
\frac{N}{c^2}\frac{M'c-2Mc'}{c^3}
=
\frac{MN'-NM'}{c^4}.
\]

If \(K_\Gamma\equiv 0\) on a connected open set with \(M\neq 0\), then
\[
0=MN'-NM'=M^2\left(\frac{N}{M}\right)',
\]
so \(N/M\) is constant there:
\[
\frac{N}{M}=A.
\]
Since
\[
\frac{N}{M}=\frac{\rho_2'}{\rho_1'},
\]
it follows that
\[
\rho_2'=A\,\rho_1',
\]
hence
\[
\rho_2=A\,\rho_1+B
\]
on that connected set. The final claim now follows from
Proposition~\ref{prop:rho2-not-affine-in-rho1}.
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Geometric consequence]
\label{rem:geometric-consequence-normalized-curve}
Proposition~\ref{prop:curvature-bridge-normalized-curve} is the local-to-global
bridge from the order-\(7\) fixed-sector analysis back to the secant/tangent
program. On the open set where
\[
c(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
M(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)\neq 0,
\]
the normalized planar curve \(\Gamma(h)\) is a strictly convex graph in the
affine chart \((\rho_1,\rho_2)\). Thus the remaining global obstruction is a
common four-secant line meeting two separated convex components twice each.
\end{remark}
```

Two small notes.

First, this is written in the same honest style as the rest of the current draft: the non-affinity proposition is conditional on the direct septic witness being generically nonzero. That is the cleanest place to plug in the local order-7 witness once you decide how hard you want to state it.

Second, once this block is in, the later global remarks about planar convexity and the “current geometric bottleneck” become much better anchored, because the curve and its curvature are now actually defined in theorem-proof form rather than only in remarks. turn116file6

If you want, the next step is to give the matching edit to the opening of the multi-pair subsection so it explicitly points to \(\Gamma(h)\), \(M(h)\), and \(K_\Gamma(h)\) instead of only the older residual-germ language.

## Replace the current opening paragraph of the multi-pair subsection

### Find the paragraph immediately after
```latex
\subsection{From the one-pair residual germ to two-pair and minimal-core rigidity}
```

In your recent drafts, it begins with something like

and then cites the cubic-leading proposition and the first residual proposition.

### Replace that whole opening paragraph with this

```latex
Let \(F_h(z)\) denote the corrected quotient odd germ of the normalized one-pair
family. By Proposition~\ref{prop:cubic-leading-order}, on the cubic-generic
region we have
\[
F_h(z)=v_0(h)z^3+v_1(h)z^5+\cdots,
\qquad
v_0(h)\neq 0,
\]
with cubic leading line
\[
\ell_h:=\mathbf C\,v_0(h)\subset Q.
\]
Under the additional trace-free pure-value quintic hypothesis of
Proposition~\ref{prop:first-residual-class-generic}, there is an open dense
subset
\[
\mathcal U_{\mathrm{res}}\subset \mathcal U_{\mathrm{cub}}
\]
on which the first residual class
\[
\overline v_1(h)\in Q/\ell_h
\]
is nonzero.

In the strengthened fixed-sector refinement, the one-pair local package is
recorded by
\[
c(h),\qquad A_5^{\mathfrak f}(h)=u_5(h)I+v_5(h)S,\qquad
\Delta_7(h),
\]
and hence by the normalized planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h):=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right)
=
\bigl(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h)\bigr).
\]
By Proposition~\ref{prop:curvature-bridge-normalized-curve}, the affine
curvature numerator of \(\Gamma\) is
\[
K_\Gamma(h)
=
\rho_1'(h)\rho_2''(h)-\rho_2'(h)\rho_1''(h)
=
\frac{M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)}{c(h)^4},
\]
where
\[
M(h)=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h),
\qquad
N(h)=c(h)\Delta_7'(h)-c'(h)\Delta_7(h).
\]
Thus the strengthened one-pair geometry is governed by the curve \(\Gamma(h)\)
and its secant/tangent behavior on the region where
\[
c(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
M(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)\neq 0.
\]
```

```latex
In this formulation, the intended two-pair and minimal-core rigidity statements
should ultimately be read as restrictions on coincidence, secant, and
```

## Why this is the right matching edit

This does three things cleanly:

It preserves the old local story:
- cubic line \(\ell_h\),
- first residual class \(\overline v_1(h)\).

It upgrades the reader to the new local package:
- \(A_5^{\mathfrak f}\),
- \(\Delta_7\),
- \(\Gamma(h)\).

And it points the rest of the subsection toward the actual endgame:
- \(M,N\),
- curvature \(K_\Gamma\),
- secant/tangent geometry,
- common four-secant exclusion.

So after this replacement, the multi-pair section will no longer feel like it is still based only on the old trace-level quintic story. It will match where the math actually is now.
