## Assistant (2026-04-18 18:56:39)

_Thought for 18s_

Good. Now that the curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h)\right)
=
\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right)
\]
and its curvature bridge are in place, the attack goes back to the global geometry.

The next object is the convex-region set
\[
\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cvx}}
:=
\{\,h:\ c(h)\neq 0,\ M(h)\neq 0,\ L(h)\neq 0\,\},
\]
where
\[
M(h)=c(h)v_5'(h)-c'(h)v_5(h),
\qquad
L(h)=M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h),
\]
and
\[
N(h)=c(h)\Delta_7'(h)-c'(h)\Delta_7(h).
\]

On this set,
\[
\rho_1'(h)=\frac{M(h)}{c(h)^2}\neq 0,
\qquad
K_\Gamma(h)=\frac{L(h)}{c(h)^4}\neq 0.
\]
So on each connected component \(C\subset\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cvx}}\), the first coordinate \(\rho_1\) is monotone and the curve can be written as a graph
\[
\rho_2=Y_C(\rho_1),
\]
with
\[
Y_C''\neq 0.
\]

That is the first real geometric theorem:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Each connected component of }\mathcal U_{\mathrm{cvx}}
\text{ maps to a strictly convex graph branch of }\Gamma.
}
\]

And that immediately gives:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A line meets each such branch in at most two points.}
}
\]

So any bad core can contribute at most two support points on each convex branch.

Now the attack splits by branch adjacency.

## 1. Boundaries where \(L=0\)

Here \(\rho_1'\neq 0\), so \(\rho_1\) remains a valid parameter, but the curvature changes sign. The tangent slope on a convex branch is
\[
m(h):=\frac{d\rho_2}{d\rho_1}=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}.
\]
Differentiate:
\[
m'(h)=\frac{MN'-NM'}{M^2}=\frac{L(h)}{M(h)^2}.
\]
So on each convex component, \(m\) is strictly monotone, and when two adjacent branches are separated by an \(L=0\) boundary, their tangent-slope intervals can meet at most at one endpoint value.

That means no line can be 2-secant to both adjacent branches across an \(L=0\) boundary.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Adjacent convex branches separated by }L=0\text{ are harmless.}
}
\]

## 2. Boundaries where \(M=0\)

Here \(\rho_1'=0\), so \(\rho_1\) has a turning point. Generically, if \(M'(h_*)\neq 0\), then
\[
\rho_1''(h_*)\neq 0,
\]
so after reparametrizing near \(h_*=0\), the local normal form is
\[
x(t)=x_0+\alpha t^2+O(t^3),
\qquad
y(t)=y_0+\beta t+\gamma t^2+O(t^3),
\qquad \beta\neq 0.
\]
Then for any affine line \(y=sx+b\),
\[
F(t):=y(t)-sx(t)-b
\]
has nonzero linear term \(F'(0)=\beta\), so \(F\) is locally monotone. Hence a single line cannot have two intersections on each side of the turning point.

So:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Adjacent branches across a generic }M=0\text{ turning point are locally harmless too.}
}
\]

That is important. It means the remaining obstruction is not local adjacency.

## 3. What remains

The only serious remaining possibility is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{a line meeting two \emph{separated} convex branches twice each.}
}
\]

So now go to support functions.

Take two ordered convex graph branches
\[
C_1:\ y=Y_1(x),\ x\in[a_1,b_1],
\qquad
C_2:\ y=Y_2(x),\ x\in[a_2,b_2],
\qquad
b_1<a_2.
\]

For a slope \(s\), define
\[
m_i(s):=\min_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx),
\qquad
M_i(s):=\max_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx).
\]
A line \(y=sx+b\) is 2-secant to \(C_i\) iff
\[
m_i(s)<b<M_i(s).
\]

So common 4-secants for \(C_1,C_2\) are controlled by
\[
G_{12}(s):=m_2(s)-M_1(s).
\]
If \(G_{12}(s)\ge 0\), then no line of slope \(s\) can be 2-secant to both branches. Earlier we already found the key monotonicity:
\[
G_{12}'(s)<0
\]
on any common slope range. So for each ordered pair of separated branches, common 4-secants are controlled by a single decreasing scalar function.

That means the real global target is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove }G_{12}(s)\ge 0\text{ on the relevant slope range for every separated pair }C_1<C_2.
}
\]

Equivalently: prove endpoint/support separation between ordered convex branches of \(\Gamma\).

So the attack has now landed in its clean final form:

1. local \(3/5/7\) structure gives the planar curve \(\Gamma\),
2. \(M\neq 0\), \(L\neq 0\) gives strictly convex graph branches,
3. adjacent branch interactions are harmless,
4. the only remaining obstruction is a common 4-secant between separated branches,
5. that obstruction is one-dimensional, encoded by \(G_{12}(s)\).

That is where the proof effort should now concentrate.

The next exact theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For separated convex branches of }\Gamma,\text{ the support-function overlap is empty.}
}
\]

And the cleanest first version is the adjacent-separated case: prove the tangent line at the left endpoint of the right branch lies above the whole left branch, or the symmetric right-end statement. That would force \(G_{12}\ge 0\) at one end, and monotonicity would do the rest.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 18:58:16)

_Thought for 16s_

Here is the next push.

We now know the remaining obstruction is a common 4-secant to two separated convex branches of
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

So take two ordered convex branches
\[
C_1:\ y=Y_1(x),\ x\in[a_1,b_1],\qquad
C_2:\ y=Y_2(x),\ x\in[a_2,b_2],\qquad
b_1<a_2.
\]

For slope \(s\), define
\[
m_i(s):=\min_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx),\qquad
M_i(s):=\max_{x\in[a_i,b_i]}(Y_i(x)-sx).
\]
A line \(y=sx+b\) is 2-secant to \(C_i\) iff
\[
m_i(s)<b<M_i(s).
\]

So a common 4-secant exists iff
\[
m_1(s)<M_2(s)\quad\text{and}\quad m_2(s)<M_1(s)
\]
for some \(s\). As before, the hard inequality is
\[
m_2(s)<M_1(s).
\]
Define
\[
G_{12}(s):=m_2(s)-M_1(s).
\]
Then common 4-secants require \(G_{12}(s)<0\).

The key fact is still
\[
G_{12}'(s)<0
\]
on any common slope range, because
\[
m_2'(s)=-x_2(s),\qquad M_1'(s)\in\{-a_1,-b_1\},
\]
and every \(x_2(s)\in[a_2,b_2]\) satisfies \(x_2(s)>b_1\).

So the whole problem is: can \(G_{12}\) ever start negative?

That reduces things to an endpoint check. Let
\[
s_-:=Y_2'(a_2),
\]
the left-end tangent slope of the right branch. Then
\[
m_2(s_-)=Y_2(a_2)-s_-a_2,
\]
so
\[
G_{12}(s_-)
=
Y_2(a_2)-s_-a_2
-
\max\{Y_1(a_1)-s_-a_1,\ Y_1(b_1)-s_-b_1\}.
\]

Geometrically: \(G_{12}(s_-)\ge 0\) exactly means the tangent line to \(C_2\) at its left endpoint lies above the whole branch \(C_1\). Since \(G_{12}\) is decreasing, if this holds and \(s_-\) is the left end of the common slope range, then there is no common 4-secant at all.

So the next real theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For two separated convex branches, the left-end tangent of the right branch lies above the left branch.}
}
\]

Now, why is this plausible for our curve \(\Gamma\)?

Because the branches are not arbitrary convex graphs. They come from a single analytic parameter curve \(h\mapsto(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h))\), and the branch decomposition is caused only by zeros of
\[
M=c v_5'-c'v_5
\quad\text{or}\quad
L=MN'-NM'.
\]

We already handled local adjacency:

- across \(L=0\), tangent slope monotonicity changes sign but no 4-secant survives;
- across generic \(M=0\), the turning-point normal form kills local double-double intersections.

So if a common 4-secant survives at all, it must be genuinely nonlocal, spanning branches separated by at least one full break in the \(x\)-ordering.

That makes the tangent-separation statement much more likely, because the right branch is not just infinitesimally to the right — it is separated by a whole interval in \(x\), and the slope-support function for the right branch keeps drifting down while the left branch’s upper support keeps drifting down more slowly.

There is a more algebraic way to phrase this.

Define the tangent-support function of branch \(C_2\):
\[
\tau_2(x):=Y_2(a_2)+Y_2'(a_2)(x-a_2).
\]
Then we want
\[
Y_1(x)\le \tau_2(x)\qquad (x\in[a_1,b_1]).
\]
Since \(Y_1\) is convex, it is enough to check the endpoints:
\[
Y_1(a_1)\le \tau_2(a_1),\qquad
Y_1(b_1)\le \tau_2(b_1).
\]

So the whole remaining 4-secant problem has collapsed to two endpoint inequalities per ordered branch pair.

That is the cleanest form yet.

Now, in terms of the original invariant curve \(\Gamma(h)\), if \(h_*\) is the left endpoint of the right branch, then
\[
x_*=\rho_1(h_*),\qquad y_*=\rho_2(h_*),\qquad s_*=\frac{d\rho_2}{d\rho_1}(h_*)=\frac{N(h_*)}{M(h_*)}.
\]
The tangent line is
\[
\ell_{h_*}(x)=y_*+s_*(x-x_*).
\]
So the branch-separation condition becomes
\[
\rho_2(h)\le \ell_{h_*}(\rho_1(h))
\]
for all \(h\) on the left branch.

Clearing denominators, this is equivalent to the sign of
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h)
:=
\Delta_7(h)c(h_*)-\Delta_7(h_*)c(h)
-
s_*\bigl(v_5(h)c(h_*)-v_5(h_*)c(h)\bigr).
\]

So the next exact scalar to study is back to
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h),
\]
but now in the right way: not as a universal local tangent object, but as the endpoint-support separator between two ordered convex branches.

That is the sharpest battlefield.

So the attack has become:

1. choose a separated ordered pair of convex branches;
2. at the left endpoint \(h_*\) of the right branch, form the tangent-support separator \(\Theta_{h_*}(h)\);
3. show \(\Theta_{h_*}(h)\le 0\) on the left branch, or at least at its endpoints;
4. conclude \(G_{12}(s)\ge 0\), hence no common 4-secant.

At this point, the proof is no longer blocked by missing invariants. It is blocked by this endpoint-support sign theorem for the planar curve \(\Gamma\).

That is the real remaining gap.

_Thought for 8s_

Here is the next honest push.

We are down to an endpoint-support sign problem for the planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h)\right)
=
\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

Take two ordered convex branches
\[
C_1:\ y=Y_1(x),\ x\in[a_1,b_1],\qquad
C_2:\ y=Y_2(x),\ x\in[a_2,b_2],\qquad
b_1<a_2.
\]

We already reduced common 4-secants to the decreasing support-gap function
\[
G_{12}(s):=m_2(s)-M_1(s),
\]
where
\[
m_2(s)=\min_{x\in[a_2,b_2]}(Y_2(x)-sx),\qquad
M_1(s)=\max_{x\in[a_1,b_1]}(Y_1(x)-sx).
\]
A common 4-secant requires \(G_{12}(s)<0\) for some \(s\).

So the key is to show
\[
G_{12}(s_-)\ge 0,
\qquad s_-:=Y_2'(a_2).
\]
That is equivalent to saying: the tangent line to \(C_2\) at its left endpoint lies above all of \(C_1\).

Now the right way to push this is to differentiate the separator along the left branch.

Fix the left-end tangent of the right branch:
\[
\ell_*(x):=Y_2(a_2)+s_*(x-a_2),
\qquad
s_*:=Y_2'(a_2).
\]
Define on the left branch
\[
H(x):=\ell_*(x)-Y_1(x).
\]
We want \(H\ge 0\) on \([a_1,b_1]\).

Because \(Y_1\) is strictly convex,
\[
H''(x)=-Y_1''(x)<0.
\]
So \(H\) is strictly concave.

That is the key structural fact.

For a concave function on an interval, it is enough to check the endpoints:
\[
H(a_1)\ge 0,\qquad H(b_1)\ge 0
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
H(x)\ge 0\ \text{for all }x\in[a_1,b_1].
\]

So the whole separated-branch obstruction reduces to two scalar inequalities:
\[
Y_2(a_2)-Y_1(a_1)\ge s_*(a_2-a_1),
\]
\[
Y_2(a_2)-Y_1(b_1)\ge s_*(a_2-b_1).
\]

This is better than the earlier support-function formulation: it is now literally two endpoint inequalities per ordered pair.

Now rewrite these back in the original parameter \(h\). Let \(h_*\) be the left endpoint of the right branch. Then
\[
x_*=\rho_1(h_*),\qquad y_*=\rho_2(h_*),\qquad s_*=\frac{N(h_*)}{M(h_*)}.
\]
For a point \(h\) on the left branch, define
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h)
:=
\rho_2(h_*)-\rho_2(h)-s_*\bigl(\rho_1(h_*)-\rho_1(h)\bigr).
\]
Then the two endpoint inequalities are exactly
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h_{1,\mathrm{left}})\ge 0,
\qquad
\Theta_{h_*}(h_{1,\mathrm{right}})\ge 0.
\]

Clearing denominators gives the invariant numerator
\[
\widetilde\Theta_{h_*}(h)
:=
\Delta_7(h_*)c(h)-\Delta_7(h)c(h_*)
-
s_*\bigl(v_5(h_*)c(h)-v_5(h)c(h_*)\bigr),
\]
with
\[
s_*=\frac{N(h_*)}{M(h_*)}.
\]

So the remaining global theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For each ordered separated branch pair, }
\widetilde\Theta_{h_*}(h)\ge 0
\text{ at the two endpoints of the left branch.}
}
\]

If that is true, concavity gives branch separation, hence no common 4-secant.

Now here is the next refinement.

You do not actually need to check every separated pair independently. Because the branches are \(x\)-ordered, if the endpoint tangent of each branch separates it from the immediately preceding branch, then by transitivity of “lies above” plus branch ordering, the same tangent separates it from all farther-left branches. So it is enough to prove the endpoint inequalities only for adjacent ordered branches.

That reduces the whole remaining 4-secant problem to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For each adjacent ordered convex branch pair, the left-end tangent of the right branch lies above the left branch endpoints.}
}
\]

That is the cleanest remaining target I can see.

So the battlefield is now extremely sharp:

- local \(3/5/7\) work gave the curve \(\Gamma\),
- curvature gave convex branches,
- local adjacency across \(M=0\) and \(L=0\) is harmless,
- separated-branch common 4-secants reduce to endpoint tangent separation,
- and by concavity it is enough to verify two endpoint inequalities for adjacent branch pairs.

That is the next exact theorem to attack.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 19:02:57)

_Thought for 1m 44s_

Yes — and I think the attack just sharpened in a better way than the endpoint-support route.

The secant/tangent problem is really a **projective zero-counting** problem for the triple
\[
(c,\ v_5,\ \Delta_7).
\]

Here is the key observation.

A line in the affine chart
\[
y=sx+b
\qquad\text{with}\qquad
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\ y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}
\]
meets the curve exactly where
\[
\Delta_7(h)-s\,v_5(h)-b\,c(h)=0.
\]

So every secant, tangent, 4-secant, common tangent, endpoint-support line — all of it — is encoded by zeros of the scalar family
\[
\Lambda_{s,b}(h):=\Delta_7(h)-s\,v_5(h)-b\,c(h).
\]

That means the global geometric obstruction can be rephrased as:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can a nontrivial linear combination of }c,\ v_5,\ \Delta_7\text{ have four zeros?}
}
\]

That is much cleaner.

And now the normalized curvature computation fits perfectly. Let
\[
W(h):=
\det
\begin{pmatrix}
c(h) & v_5(h) & \Delta_7(h)\\
c'(h) & v_5'(h) & \Delta_7'(h)\\
c''(h) & v_5''(h) & \Delta_7''(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Then with
\[
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5,\qquad
N=c\,\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7,
\]
a direct calculation gives
\[
MN'-NM' = c\,W.
\]
So the curvature numerator of the normalized planar curve is
\[
K_\Gamma
=
\frac{MN'-NM'}{c^4}
=
\frac{W}{c^3}.
\]

This is the right bridge:

- \(W\not\equiv 0\) means the triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is not projectively degenerate;
- \(K_\Gamma\not\equiv 0\) is exactly the affine version of the same fact;
- and the secant/tangent problem is really about zero-counting for \(\Lambda_{s,b}\).

So I think the next theorem to attack is not merely “endpoint tangent separation.” It is the stronger and cleaner statement:

\[
\boxed{
(c,\ v_5,\ \Delta_7)\ \text{should form an ECT/Tchebychev-type triple on generic intervals.}
}
\]

If that were proved, then every nontrivial linear combination
\[
A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7
\]
would have at most two zeros counting multiplicity on such an interval. But a tangent is a double zero, and a 4-secant gives four zeros, so that would kill the whole secant/tangent obstruction in one stroke.

This is better than the earlier support-function route because it speaks directly in the native variables of the proof.

So the attack now becomes:

1. prove or at least attack the nonvanishing of the projective Wronskian
   \[
   W(h)=\det
   \begin{pmatrix}
   c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
   c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
   c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
   \end{pmatrix};
   \]

2. if possible, strengthen this to an ECT statement for the triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\);

3. then interpret every affine secant/tangent line as a zero set of
   \[
   \Lambda_{s,b}(h)=\Delta_7-sv_5-bc.
   \]

So the remaining gap, restated cleanly, is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that }(c,\ v_5,\ \Delta_7)\text{ is generically a nondegenerate Chebyshev-type triple.}
}
\]

That is now the sharpest continuation I can see, and it feels more intrinsic than the previous branch-by-branch tangent-support route.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 19:03:42)

I’ve found a cleaner bridge: the secant/tangent problem is really zero-counting for linear combinations of \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\). I’m checking how far the Wronskian route genuinely gets us before it overclaims.

_Thought for 56s_

Here is the cleanest continuation I can honestly defend.

Let
\[
f_0:=c,\qquad f_1:=v_5,\qquad f_2:=\Delta_7,
\]
and define the projective Wronskian
\[
W(h):=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Also recall
\[
M:=cv_5'-c'v_5,\qquad
N:=c\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7.
\]

A direct expansion gives
\[
MN'-NM' = c\,W.
\]
So, on \(\{c\neq 0\}\),
\[
K_\Gamma
=
\frac{MN'-NM'}{c^4}
=
\frac{W}{c^3},
\]
where
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

That identity is the right bridge.

Now fix a connected interval \(I\) on which
\[
c\neq 0,\qquad M\neq 0,\qquad W\neq 0.
\]
Then:

- \(c\neq 0\) means the affine chart is valid;
- \(M\neq 0\) means \(\rho_1=v_5/c\) is monotone;
- \(W\neq 0\) means the normalized curve has nonzero curvature.

So \(\Gamma(I)\) is a strictly convex graph branch.

But there is a stronger way to phrase the same fact.

## Local ECT theorem

On such an interval \(I\), the triple
\[
(c,v_5,\Delta_7)
\]
is an **ECT-system**. The reason is the classical criterion: for three \(C^2\) functions, if the successive initial Wronskians
\[
c,\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}c&v_5\\ c'&v_5'\end{pmatrix}=M,\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}=W
\]
do not vanish on \(I\), then \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an extended complete Chebyshev system on \(I\).

That gives the zero-counting statement we wanted:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Every nontrivial linear combination }A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7
\text{ has at most two zeros on }I,
}
\]
counting multiplicity.

Now apply this to
\[
\Lambda_{s,b}(h):=\Delta_7(h)-s\,v_5(h)-b\,c(h).
\]

A line in the affine chart
\[
y=sx+b,
\qquad x=\frac{v_5}{c},\ y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
meets the curve exactly at the zeros of \(\Lambda_{s,b}\). Therefore, on any interval \(I\) with
\[
c\neq 0,\qquad M\neq 0,\qquad W\neq 0,
\]
a line can meet \(\Gamma(I)\) in at most two points, and a tangent counts as a double zero.

So we have upgraded the branch picture to:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Each connected component of }\{c\neq 0,\ M\neq 0,\ W\neq 0\}
\text{ is not just a convex branch, but an ECT interval for }(c,v_5,\Delta_7).
}
\]

That is a genuine strengthening.

## What this immediately kills

It kills every same-interval obstruction:

- no 3-secant on one branch,
- no tangent plus two more intersections on one branch,
- no four zeros of \(\Lambda_{s,b}\) on one branch.

So the same-branch secant/tangent problem is fully dead now.

It also makes the local adjacency analysis cleaner:

- across \(W=0\) with \(M\neq 0\), the tangent slope
  \[
  m(h)=\frac{d\rho_2}{d\rho_1}=\frac{N}{M}
  \]
  changes monotonicity, so adjacent ECT intervals only touch through a slope-critical boundary;

- across \(M=0\), we still have the turning-point normal form, so local double-double intersections are impossible there too.

So the only remaining global obstruction is now very sharply identified:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A single line meeting two \emph{different} ECT intervals twice each.}
}
\]

That is better than the older “convex branch” phrasing, because it says exactly what survives after the local zero-counting theorem.

## The remaining gap, restated cleanly

Let \(I_1,I_2\) be two distinct connected components of
\[
\{c\neq 0,\ M\neq 0,\ W\neq 0\},
\]
ordered in the affine \(x=v_5/c\) coordinate. Then the unresolved problem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can }\Lambda_{s,b}(h)=\Delta_7-sv_5-bc
\text{ have two zeros on }I_1\text{ and two zeros on }I_2?
}
\]

Equivalently: can one affine line be 2-secant to two separated ECT arcs?

That is now the exact endgame.

## Best next attack

The right next object is the endpoint tangent/support separator, but now phrased in the intrinsic variables \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\).

For an ordered pair \(I_1<I_2\), take the left endpoint \(h_*\) of \(I_2\), and the tangent slope there
\[
s_*=\frac{N(h_*)}{M(h_*)}.
\]
Then the candidate separating line is
\[
\Lambda_{s_*,b_*}(h)=\Delta_7(h)-s_*v_5(h)-b_*c(h),
\]
where \(b_*\) is chosen so that \(\Lambda_{s_*,b_*}(h_*)=0\).

The remaining theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{s_*,b_*}(h)\text{ does not change sign on }I_1.
}
\]

If that holds, then the tangent line at the left endpoint of the right interval lies entirely above or below the left interval, and by the monotonicity of the support-gap function there can be no common 4-secant.

So the attack has now simplified to:

1. local ECT theorem for \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\): done in principle on \(\{c,M,W\neq 0\}\);
2. remaining gap: endpoint tangent/support separation between distinct ECT intervals.

That is the sharpest version yet.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 19:08:19)

_Thought for 1m 59s_

Here is the strongest clean continuation I can see now.

We have reduced the global obstruction to this:

For each \(x\)-ordered ECT branch \(I_j\) of the planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
a line \(y=sx+b\) is 2-secant to the branch iff
\[
b\in \mathcal I_j(s):=(m_j(s),M_j(s)),
\]
where
\[
m_j(s):=\min_{x\in[a_j,b_j]}(Y_j(x)-sx),\qquad
M_j(s):=\max_{x\in[a_j,b_j]}(Y_j(x)-sx).
\]

So the whole endgame is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that for }i<k,\ \mathcal I_i(s)\cap \mathcal I_k(s)=\varnothing
\text{ for all }s.
}
\]

That is the barrier formulation.

Now here is the key sharpening:

## 1. A sufficient “barrier theorem”

It is enough to prove this for **adjacent** \(x\)-ordered ECT branches:

\[
\boxed{
M_j(s)\le m_{j+1}(s)\qquad\text{for all }s\text{ and all adjacent }j.
}
\]

Because then, by transitivity,
\[
M_i(s)\le m_{i+1}(s)\le M_{i+1}(s)\le \cdots \le m_k(s)
\]
for every \(i<k\), so no nonadjacent overlap is possible either.

So the entire remaining global problem collapses to an adjacent-branch barrier inequality.

That is better than the earlier “check every separated pair” viewpoint.

## 2. Adjacent barrier inequality reduces to one tangent line

Fix adjacent \(x\)-ordered branches \(C_j<C_{j+1}\), with graphs
\[
y=Y_j(x),\qquad x\in[a_j,b_j],
\]
\[
y=Y_{j+1}(x),\qquad x\in[a_{j+1},b_{j+1}],
\qquad b_j<a_{j+1}.
\]

Let
\[
s_*:=Y_{j+1}'(a_{j+1})
\]
be the left-end tangent slope of the right branch, and let
\[
\ell_*(x):=Y_{j+1}(a_{j+1})+s_*(x-a_{j+1})
\]
be that tangent line.

Now define on the left branch
\[
H_j(x):=\ell_*(x)-Y_j(x).
\]

Since \(Y_j\) is strictly convex,
\[
H_j''(x)=-Y_j''(x)<0,
\]
so \(H_j\) is strictly concave.

This is the payoff: to prove
\[
\ell_*(x)\ge Y_j(x)\qquad (x\in[a_j,b_j]),
\]
it is enough to prove the two endpoint inequalities
\[
H_j(a_j)\ge 0,\qquad H_j(b_j)\ge 0.
\]

And if those hold, then
\[
M_j(s_*)\le m_{j+1}(s_*).
\]

Because the support-gap function
\[
G_{j,j+1}(s):=m_{j+1}(s)-M_j(s)
\]
is decreasing in \(s\), that single endpoint-slope check then gives
\[
G_{j,j+1}(s)\ge 0
\]
throughout the relevant common slope range.

So the whole adjacent barrier theorem reduces to **two scalar endpoint inequalities**.

## 3. Put it back in intrinsic variables

Let \(h_*\) be the left endpoint of the right branch. Then
\[
x_*=\rho_1(h_*),\qquad y_*=\rho_2(h_*),\qquad s_*=\frac{N(h_*)}{M(h_*)}.
\]

For a point \(h\) on the left branch define
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h)
:=
\rho_2(h_*)-\rho_2(h)-s_*\bigl(\rho_1(h_*)-\rho_1(h)\bigr).
\]

Then the endpoint inequalities are exactly
\[
\Theta_{h_*}(h_{j,\mathrm{left}})\ge 0,
\qquad
\Theta_{h_*}(h_{j,\mathrm{right}})\ge 0.
\]

Clearing denominators:
\[
\widetilde\Theta_{h_*}(h)
:=
\Delta_7(h_*)c(h)-\Delta_7(h)c(h_*)
-
s_*\bigl(v_5(h_*)c(h)-v_5(h)c(h_*)\bigr),
\]
with
\[
s_*=\frac{N(h_*)}{M(h_*)}.
\]

So the next exact theorem is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For each adjacent \(x\)-ordered ECT branch pair, }
\widetilde\Theta_{h_*}(h)\ge 0
\text{ at the two endpoints of the left branch.}
}
\]

That is the sharpest remaining gap I can currently isolate.

## 4. Why this is the right target

This is now stronger than the old tangent/secant phrasing and cleaner than the raw support-function phrasing:

- it is **adjacent only**;
- it is **endpoint only**;
- it is **intrinsic** in \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\);
- and if proved, it kills all common 4-secants globally by barrier transitivity.

So the attack is no longer diffuse. It is:

1. prove the adjacent endpoint barrier inequality for \(\widetilde\Theta_{h_*}\);
2. deduce \(M_j(s)\le m_{j+1}(s)\) for adjacent branches;
3. deduce no support overlap for any separated pair;
4. conclude no common 4-secant line anywhere.

That is the exact remaining theorem.

My honest view is that this is now the cleanest endpoint of the attack so far: everything has collapsed to a barrier inequality for adjacent ECT intervals of the normalized planar curve.

_Thought for 2m 19s_

Here is the best attack I can honestly make now.

The right intrinsic object is not the support-gap function \(G_{12}\) itself. It is the **tangent separator determinant** for the triple
\[
F(h):=(c(h),\,v_5(h),\,\Delta_7(h)).
\]

If \(h_*\) is a point on the normalized planar curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right),
\]
then the affine tangent line to \(\Gamma\) at \(h_*\) is exactly the zero set of the linear combination
\[
\Lambda_{h_*}(h):=\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c(h_*) & v_5(h_*) & \Delta_7(h_*)\\
c'(h_*) & v_5'(h_*) & \Delta_7'(h_*)\\
c(h) & v_5(h) & \Delta_7(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

Why this is the right object:

- \(\Lambda_{h_*}(h_*)=0\) because the first and third rows coincide;
- \(\Lambda'_{h_*}(h_*)=0\) because the second and derivative of third rows coincide;
- so \(h_*\) is automatically a **double zero**, exactly as a tangent line should give.

And this is the key computation:

\[
\Lambda_{h_*}''(h_*)=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c(h_*) & v_5(h_*) & \Delta_7(h_*)\\
c'(h_*) & v_5'(h_*) & \Delta_7'(h_*)\\
c''(h_*) & v_5''(h_*) & \Delta_7''(h_*)
\end{pmatrix}
=
W(h_*),
\]
where
\[
W(h):=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

So on any ECT interval where \(W\) has fixed sign,
\[
\Lambda_{h_*}(h)
=
\frac12 W(h_*)(h-h_*)^2+O((h-h_*)^3),
\]
and therefore the tangent line has a definite local support sign on that interval.

That is already a real gain.

It means:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Inside a single ECT interval, tangent lines are locally supporting, with sign controlled by }W.
}
\]

Now apply this to adjacent intervals.

Suppose \(I_1<I_2\) are adjacent ECT intervals in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering, and \(h_*\) is the left endpoint of \(I_2\). Then \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) is the tangent separator for the left-end tangent of the right interval.

To prove the adjacent barrier theorem, we want:
\[
\Lambda_{h_*}(h)\ \text{has a fixed sign on }I_1.
\]

Here is the sharpened reduction:

- on \(I_1\), since \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT triple, any nontrivial linear combination has at most two zeros;
- \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) is one such linear combination;
- if \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) changed sign on \(I_1\), it would need at least one zero there;
- if it had two zeros on \(I_1\), together with the double zero at \(h_*\) that would give four zeros on the adjacent union, which is exactly the forbidden pattern we are trying to exclude.

So the remaining question is now even sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can }\Lambda_{h_*}\text{ have even a single zero on the adjacent left ECT interval?}
}
\]

That is the exact adjacent-branch barrier problem.

And now there is a better way to phrase the hoped-for theorem:

> For adjacent \(x\)-ordered ECT intervals \(I_1<I_2\), the tangent separator \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) at the left endpoint \(h_*\) of \(I_2\) has constant sign on \(I_1\).

If that holds, then the tangent line at the left edge of the right interval lies entirely above or below the left interval, and the support-gap barrier follows immediately.

So the attack has improved in three ways:

1. we now have an **intrinsic formula** for the tangent separator in the native variables \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\);
2. its second derivative at the tangency point is exactly the projective Wronskian \(W\);
3. the remaining global obstruction is reduced to the sign of one determinant-valued function \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) on the adjacent interval.

That is the cleanest formulation yet.

The real remaining gap is now:

\[
\boxed{
\text{prove that the tangent separator determinant }\Lambda_{h_*}\text{ has constant sign on the adjacent ECT interval.}
}
\]

That is where the proof effort should concentrate next.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 19:12:53)

_Thought for 4s_

Here is the strongest attack I can make on the tangent-separator problem.

Let
\[
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h))\in \mathbf R^3
\]
and for a fixed basepoint \(h_*\) define the tangent-separator determinant
\[
\Lambda_{h_*}(h):=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c(h_*) & v_5(h_*) & \Delta_7(h_*)\\
c'(h_*) & v_5'(h_*) & \Delta_7'(h_*)\\
c(h) & v_5(h) & \Delta_7(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]

As we already saw:

- \(\Lambda_{h_*}(h_*)=0\),
- \(\Lambda'_{h_*}(h_*)=0\),
- \(\Lambda''_{h_*}(h_*)=W(h_*)\), where
  \[
  W(h)=\det
  \begin{pmatrix}
  c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
  c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
  c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
  \end{pmatrix}.
  \]

So on an ECT interval where \(W\) has fixed sign, \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) is locally a supporting tangent separator.

The real question is whether \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) can vanish again on the adjacent interval.

That can now be attacked using the ECT property directly.

## 1. What a zero of \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) means

A zero of \(\Lambda_{h_*}(h)\) means the projective point \(F(h)\) lies in the osculating plane spanned by
\[
F(h_*),\quad F'(h_*).
\]
Equivalently, in the affine chart, the point \(\Gamma(h)\) lies on the tangent line to \(\Gamma\) at \(h_*\).

So if \(h_*\) is the left endpoint of the right ECT interval \(I_2\), then zeros of \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) on the left adjacent interval \(I_1\) are exactly intersections of the left interval with that endpoint tangent line.

## 2. ECT gives a strong counting bound

Because \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) is an ECT triple on each interval, every nontrivial linear combination
\[
A c + B v_5 + C\Delta_7
\]
has at most two zeros on that interval, counting multiplicity.

Now \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) is one such linear combination, with coefficients frozen by \(h_*\). At \(h_*\) it already has a double zero. Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{h_*}\text{ cannot have any additional zero on the same ECT interval }I_2.
}
\]

So the tangent line at \(h_*\) does not re-enter the right branch locally or globally inside \(I_2\). That part is done.

The unresolved issue is only zeros on \(I_1\).

## 3. Reduce adjacent sign change to a single zero

Now suppose \(I_1<I_2\) are adjacent in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering, and assume for contradiction that \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) changes sign on \(I_1\).

Since \(I_1\) is connected, that implies at least one zero
\[
h_1\in I_1.
\]

So the tangent line at \(h_*\) meets the left interval at least once.

Could it meet it twice? If yes, then \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) would have:

- two zeros on \(I_1\),
- one double zero at \(h_*\).

That is already four zeros total across the adjacent union. Geometrically that is the forbidden “two on the left, tangent on the right” pattern.

So to kill the adjacent barrier problem, it is enough to rule out even **one** zero on \(I_1\).

That is now the exact target:
\[
\boxed{
\Lambda_{h_*}(h)\neq 0\quad \text{for all }h\in I_1.
}
\]

## 4. Why one zero is already unstable

Here is the best structural argument I can see.

Define
\[
T(h_*,h):=\Lambda_{h_*}(h).
\]
This is smooth off the diagonal and vanishes to second order on the diagonal with sign governed by \(W\).

Now look at the set
\[
\mathcal Z:=\{(h_*,h): T(h_*,h)=0,\ h<h_*\}.
\]

If for adjacent intervals there were systematically a zero on the left interval, then \(\mathcal Z\) would trace a branch approaching the boundary pair from the left. Differentiating the identity
\[
T(h_*,h)=0
\]
along such a branch would force a compatibility between the tangent direction at \(h_*\) and the secant direction from the left interval.

But that is exactly the kind of degeneracy the nonvanishing of the projective Wronskian \(W\) is meant to prevent: \(W\neq 0\) says the projective jet
\[
F,\ F',\ F''
\]
is nondegenerate, so nearby secants cannot align with the osculating direction except at the basepoint itself.

In plainer language: on an ECT arc, the tangent line should be a genuine support line, and the only way it can pick up an extra adjacent-interval intersection is if the two adjacent arcs line up in a projectively degenerate way across the boundary.

So the natural next theorem is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Across an adjacent boundary, the one-sided tangent separators inherit the support sign from the nonvanishing of }W.
}
\]

That is not yet proved, but it is now much sharper than before.

## 5. A more concrete one-sided attack

There is an even more explicit route.

Take \(h_*\) at the left edge of \(I_2\), and let \(h\) vary on the adjacent interval \(I_1\). Expand \(F(h)\) around the boundary point \(h_b\) separating the intervals.

There are only two kinds of boundaries:

### Case A: \(W=0\), but \(M\neq 0\)

Then the affine \(x\)-coordinate is still good, and the tangent slope
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
changes monotonicity across the boundary.

This means the left and right branches have tangent-slope ranges that only touch at one endpoint value. So the endpoint tangent of the right branch is an extreme slope relative to the left branch. That strongly suggests the tangent line is supporting for the left branch too.

In this case, the adjacent barrier theorem is very plausible from pure slope ordering.

### Case B: \(M=0\), generic turning point

Then the local normal form is
\[
x(t)=x_0+\alpha t^2+O(t^3),\qquad
y(t)=y_0+\beta t+\gamma t^2+O(t^3),\qquad \beta\neq 0.
\]
The tangent line at \(t=0\) is vertical. Nearby tangents from the right branch have large slope, and the left/right arcs lie on opposite sides in parameter, but on the same side in \(x\).

This local model already showed there is no local double-double intersection. For the present problem it suggests that the left-end tangents of the right branch should escape the left branch entirely near the boundary.

So in both boundary models, the adjacent barrier theorem looks compatible with the local normal forms.

## 6. Best current formulation of the remaining gap

So the attack result is this:

- We have an intrinsic tangent-separator determinant \(\Lambda_{h_*}(h)\).
- It has a double zero at the tangency point, with second derivative \(W(h_*)\).
- On each ECT interval, it cannot have any additional zero on the same interval.
- The whole global obstruction reduces to whether \(\Lambda_{h_*}\) can have a zero on the adjacent left interval.
- Local models at both possible boundary types suggest the answer should be no.

So the remaining theorem is now extremely sharp:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For adjacent ECT intervals }I_1<I_2,\ \Lambda_{h_*}(h)\text{ has constant sign on }I_1,
}
\]
where \(h_*\) is the left endpoint of \(I_2\).

If that is proved, then:

- adjacent support barriers hold,
- by transitivity all separated support barriers hold,
- hence no common 4-secant line exists anywhere,
- hence no bad multi-pair core survives.

That is the cleanest endpoint of the attack so far.

The next move I would make is to prove the adjacent sign theorem separately in the two boundary cases \(W=0\) and \(M=0\), because the local geometry is different in those two cases and the sign mechanism is likely different too.

So the next attack should not be “prove one tangent line separates everything.” The better target is a **global zero-counting theorem** for
\[
\Lambda_{s,b}(h):=\Delta_7(h)-s\,v_5(h)-b\,c(h).

\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}

meets the curve exactly at the zeros of \(\Lambda_{s,b}\). A 4-secant is therefore exactly a linear combination of \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) with four zeros.

\[

\[
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5,

---

## 1. What is already true on each strict branch

On any interval where
\[
c\neq 0,\qquad M\neq 0,\qquad W\neq 0,
\]
the triple
\[
(c,v_5,\Delta_7)
\]
is an ECT system, because the successive Wronskians
\[
c,\qquad
M=\det\!\begin{pmatrix}c&v_5\\ c'&v_5'\end{pmatrix},\qquad
W=\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}
\]
do not vanish there. Hence every \(\Lambda_{s,b}\) has at most two zeros on such an interval. So each strict convex branch is already fully controlled.

The only remaining place where a 4-secant could hide is across an interval on which \(M\) changes sign once through a simple zero while \(W\) stays nonzero.

That is exactly the generic \(x'=0\) turning-point case.

---

## 2. New target: a “one-simple-zero” Chebyshev theorem

Suppose on a connected interval \(J\),

- \(c\neq 0\),
- \(W\neq 0\),
- \(M\) has at most one simple zero.

Then I think the right theorem to prove is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Every nontrivial }A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7
\text{ has at most three zeros on }J,
}
\]
counting multiplicity.

If that is true, then the whole 4-secant problem dies at once:

- four transverse intersections are impossible;
- tangent \(+\) two more \(+\) one more is impossible;
- double on one side and double on the other is impossible.

This would bypass the adjacent-tangent barrier argument entirely.

And it matches the geometry: a simple zero of \(M\) means one turning point in the affine \(x\)-coordinate, so the curve can fail to be a single convex graph only once. That should cost at most one extra zero beyond the ECT bound.

---

## 3. Why this is plausible

Write
\[
\rho_1=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad
\rho_2=\frac{\Delta_7}{c}.
\]
Then
\[
M=c^2\rho_1',
\qquad
W=c^3K_\Gamma,
\]
where
\[
K_\Gamma=\rho_1'\rho_2''-\rho_2'\rho_1''.
\]

So on \(J\),

- \(\rho_1'\) changes sign at most once;
- \(K_\Gamma\) never vanishes.

That means the planar curve \(\Gamma(h)=(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h))\) has nonzero affine curvature everywhere, but its \(x\)-projection can fold once.

Geometrically, that is exactly a curve with one generic turning point and no inflection. Such a curve should intersect any line in at most three points.

So the theorem above is the projective/zero-counting version of the obvious geometric claim:

\[
\boxed{
\text{A plane curve with nonzero curvature and at most one \(x\)-turning point meets any line at most three times.}
}
\]

That is much more natural than trying to prove a barrier inequality branch by branch.

---

## 4. How to attack that theorem

Take
\[
\Lambda(h):=A\,c(h)+B\,v_5(h)+C\,\Delta_7(h).
\]
Divide by \(c(h)\neq 0\):
\[
\frac{\Lambda(h)}{c(h)}=A+B\rho_1(h)+C\rho_2(h).
\]
So the zero set of \(\Lambda\) is the intersection of the curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=(\rho_1(h),\rho_2(h))
\]
with the affine line
\[
A+Bx+Cy=0.
\]

Now parametrize the curve locally as \(y=Y(x)\) on each side of the unique turning point. Since \(K_\Gamma\neq 0\), each side is strictly convex or strictly concave in \(x\), and since there is at most one turning point, there are only two such sides.

A line meets each side in at most two points, but that alone still allows four. The extra ingredient is that the two sides are joined smoothly at the turning point, and the curvature does not change sign there. That forces the two graph branches to have compatible support orientation, so one of the four candidate intersections disappears.

Put differently: the local normal form at a simple turning point is
\[
x(t)=x_0+\alpha t^2+O(t^3),\qquad
y(t)=y_0+\beta t+\gamma t^2+O(t^3),\qquad \alpha\beta\neq 0.
\]
Then for any line \(y=sx+b\),
\[
F(t):=y(t)-sx(t)-b
\]
has the form
\[
F(t)=\beta t + (\gamma-s\alpha)t^2 + O(t^3) + \text{constant}.
\]
That model has at most one zero near the turning point on each side, and the global nonzero-curvature condition prevents the line from re-entering both sides twice without creating an inflection-type failure. This is the local-to-global mechanism to make precise.

So the next exact theorem to attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If }K_\Gamma\neq 0\text{ and }\rho_1'\text{ has at most one simple zero on }J,
\text{ then every line meets }\Gamma(J)\text{ at most three times.}
}
\]

---

## 5. Why this is the right remaining gap

This theorem would simultaneously handle:

- separated convex branches,
- adjacent turning-point branches,
- and all mixed secant/tangent patterns.

It is stronger than the old support-gap inequality and cleaner than the endpoint-tangent separator determinant.

So the attack is now:

1. prove the “one-turning-point, nonzero-curvature” line-intersection theorem for \(\Gamma\);
2. conclude every \(\Lambda_{s,b}\) has at most three zeros;
3. rule out all 4-secant configurations;
4. collapse the remaining bad-core possibilities.

That is the sharpest route I can see from here.

I’m checking the current draft for how the normalized curve, \(M\), and the planar-convexity remarks are phrased now, so I can keep the attack aligned with the file rather than drift into a parallel argument.

_Thought for 56s_

There is a real new gain here.

The generic \(M=0\) turning-point case is actually better than we were treating it. It does not merely kill **local** double-double intersections. It kills **adjacent-branch common secants globally**.

Write
\[
x(h):=\rho_1(h)=\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\qquad
y(h):=\rho_2(h)=\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)},
\]
and
\[
m(h):=\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}.
\]

At a generic turning point \(h_*\), we have
\[
M(h_*)=0,\qquad M'(h_*)\neq 0,
\]
so \(x'(h_*)=0\) is a simple zero. The local normal form already in the attack is
\[
x(t)=x_0+\alpha t^2+O(t^3),\qquad
y(t)=y_0+\beta t+\gamma t^2+O(t^3),\qquad \alpha\beta\neq 0,
\]
with \(t=h-h_*\). Then
\[
x'(t)=2\alpha t+O(t^2),\qquad y'(t)=\beta+O(t),
\]
so
\[
m(t)=\frac{y'(t)}{x'(t)}
\sim \frac{\beta}{2\alpha\, t}.
\]

So as \(t\to 0^\pm\),
\[
m(t)\to \pm \infty
\]
with opposite signs. Because \(W\neq 0\) on each adjacent ECT interval, \(m\) is strictly monotone on each side. Therefore the two tangent-slope intervals on the branches adjacent to a generic turning point are **disjoint**. That means no nonvertical affine line can be 2-secant to both adjacent branches. And a vertical line \(x=\text{const}\) meets each side in at most one point, so it can contribute at most two points total. Hence a generic \(M=0\) boundary is globally harmless, not just locally harmless. This sharpens the earlier local-normal-form conclusion. turn119file4turn119file8

Combined with the already-established \(W=0\), \(M\neq 0\) case — where adjacent branches are harmless because their tangent-slope intervals touch at most at one endpoint — we get a much stronger global reduction:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No pair-pair configuration can use two adjacent convex/ECT branches.}
}
\]

That is a real theorem-level step. It matches and strengthens the earlier conversation result that “only non-adjacent convex components can still potentially support a pair-pair configuration.” turn119file9

So the remaining battlefield is now even sharper:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Can one affine line be 2-secant to two non-adjacent ECT branches?}
}
\]

At this point the best attack is no longer local. It is combinatorial-geometric.

Let the ECT branches be ordered by the affine \(x=v_5/c\) coordinate:
\[
C_1<C_2<\cdots<C_r.
\]
Each branch is a strictly convex graph, so for fixed slope \(s\) the allowed intercepts form an interval
\[
\mathcal I_j(s)=(m_j(s),M_j(s)),
\]
and a line of slope \(s\) is 2-secant to \(C_j\) iff its intercept \(b\) lies in \(\mathcal I_j(s)\). This is exactly the support-function reduction we had reached earlier. turn119file5turn119file6

Because adjacent branches are now globally ruled out, any surviving 4-secant would have to hit \(C_i\) twice and \(C_k\) twice with at least one full intermediate branch \(C_j\) in between. That is where the next theorem should aim:

\[
\boxed{
\text{If a line is 2-secant to }C_i\text{ and }C_k\text{ with }i<j<k,
\text{ then it must intersect }C_j.
}
\]

If you can prove that, the whole remaining obstruction dies at once, because \(C_j\) is itself an ECT branch and cannot absorb extra intersections arbitrarily. This is exactly the “intermediate branch forcing” idea that the earlier attack was circling around when it said that a non-adjacent two-pair configuration should typically force an extra intersection on an intermediate component. turn119file9

So the attack has improved to:

1. \(W=0\) adjacent boundaries: harmless.  
2. generic \(M=0\) turning-point boundaries: now also harmless **globally**, because the slope intervals are disjoint.  
3. therefore any surviving 4-secant must use **non-adjacent** branches only.  
4. the next exact target is the intermediate-branch forcing theorem.

That is the right next attack. The problem is no longer “secant/tangent near a boundary.” It is “can a secant line skip an entire ECT branch?”

## Assistant (2026-04-18 19:22:37)

_Thought for 1m 51s_

Here is the right attack, and it corrects something important.

The “intermediate branch forcing” statement

\[
\text{if a line is 2-secant to }C_i\text{ and }C_k\text{ with }i<j<k,\text{ then it must hit }C_j
\]

is **not true for arbitrary separated convex branches**. So we should stop trying to prove that from convexity alone. A line can skip a middle convex arc in general.

So the remaining theorem has to use the special structure of our curve, not just “ordered convex graphs.”

The right intrinsic object is the **tangent-separator kernel**
\[
K(s,t):=\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F(t)\bigr),
\qquad
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]

This is exactly the determinant we were calling \(\Lambda_{h_*}(h)\), just with \(s=h_*\) and \(t=h\).

Why this is the correct object:

- \(K(s,t)=0\) means \(F(t)\) lies in the osculating plane spanned by \(F(s),F'(s)\), i.e. in the affine chart, \(\Gamma(t)\) lies on the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s)\).
- For fixed \(s\), the function \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\) is a linear combination of \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\).
- It has a **double zero** at \(t=s\):
  \[
  K(s,s)=0,\qquad \partial_t K(s,s)=0,
  \]
  and
  \[
  \partial_t^2 K(s,s)=W(s),
  \]
  where
  \[
  W(h)=\det
  \begin{pmatrix}
  c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
  c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
  c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
  \end{pmatrix}.
  \]

So the global support problem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For adjacent ECT intervals }I_1<I_2,\text{ show }K(s,t)\text{ has constant sign for }s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1.
}
\]

If that holds, the tangent line from the right interval never meets the left interval, and the whole 4-secant obstruction collapses.

That is the right replacement for the failed “intermediate branch forcing” route.

Now here is the strongest progress I can make on it.

## 1. Same-interval sign is already rigid

Fix \(s\in I\), where \(I\) is one ECT interval. Since \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\) is a nontrivial linear combination of \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) on \(I\), and it already has a double zero at \(t=s\), the ECT property implies:

\[
\boxed{
K(s,t)\neq 0\quad\text{for all }t\in I,\ t\neq s.
}
\]

So tangent lines never re-enter the same ECT interval. That part is done.

## 2. Adjacent-interval sign can only fail by a single zero

Now let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent intervals.

For fixed \(s\in I_2\), the function \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\) restricted to \(I_1\) is still a linear combination of the ECT triple on \(I_1\). Therefore it has at most two zeros on \(I_1\).

So if the adjacent sign theorem fails, it fails in the weakest possible way: there is at least one isolated zero
\[
t=t(s)\in I_1
\]
with
\[
K(s,t(s))=0.
\]

Thus the entire obstruction reduces to the existence of a zero-curve of the kernel \(K(s,t)\) across adjacent intervals.

That is much sharper than before.

## 3. Local boundary models suggest no such zero-curve can emerge

There are only two adjacent-boundary types.

### Case A: \(W=0\), but \(M\neq 0\)

Then \(x=v_5/c\) remains a good coordinate, and the tangent slope
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
has an extremum at the boundary. So the tangent-slope ranges of the two adjacent intervals touch only through that extreme value. This means the tangent lines from one side begin as supporting lines, not transverse secants, on the other side.

So near a \(W=0\) boundary, \(K(s,t)\) has a definite sign across adjacent intervals.

### Case B: \(M=0\), generic turning point

Then the local normal form is
\[
x(\tau)=x_0+\alpha \tau^2+O(\tau^3),\qquad
y(\tau)=y_0+\beta \tau+\gamma \tau^2+O(\tau^3),
\qquad \alpha\beta\neq 0.
\]

The tangent slope behaves like
\[
\frac{dy}{dx}\sim \frac{\beta}{2\alpha \tau},
\]
so the adjacent intervals have opposite infinite slope limits. In particular, a tangent from one side cannot match a tangent from the other side near the boundary. So again, near the boundary, \(K(s,t)\) has a definite sign across adjacent intervals.

This is stronger than our earlier “locally harmless” statement: it says the tangent-separator kernel itself should be sign-definite near adjacent boundaries.

## 4. Best current theorem target

So the attack has become:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Show that the boundary-local sign of }K(s,t)\text{ cannot reverse as }(s,t)
\text{ move through adjacent intervals.}
}
\]

Equivalently: the kernel
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))
\]
should be **sign-regular across adjacent ECT intervals**.

If that holds, then the tangent barriers follow, and by transitivity of support separation there are no common 4-secants anywhere.

## 5. Why this is the right remaining gap

This is now the exact missing theorem:

- It is stronger than branchwise convexity,
- more intrinsic than support-gap functions,
- and uses the native triple \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\) directly.

So the attack result is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{The proof no longer needs “skip an intermediate branch.” It needs sign-regularity of the tangent-separator kernel }K(s,t).
}
\]

That is the cleanest formulation of the remaining global obstruction I can currently justify.

## Assistant (2026-04-18 19:25:38)

_Thought for 1m 28s_

Good — this actually attacks the real gap.

The right object is still
\[
K(s,t):=\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F(t)\bigr),
\qquad
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]

For fixed \(s\), the zero set of \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\) is exactly the intersection of the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s)\) with the curve
\[
\Gamma(h)=\left(\frac{v_5(h)}{c(h)},\frac{\Delta_7(h)}{c(h)}\right).
\]

So the adjacent-interval barrier theorem becomes:

\[
\boxed{
\text{For adjacent ECT intervals }I_1<I_2,\ K(s,t)\neq 0
\text{ for all }s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1.
}
\]

If we can show that, then every tangent line from the right interval misses the left interval. And that is exactly what we need, because the support line of the right interval at slope \(m\) is one of these tangents. So for every slope,
\[
M_1(m)\le m_2(m),
\]
hence adjacent support intervals are disjoint, and by transitivity all separated support intervals are disjoint too. That kills every possible common 4-secant.

So the whole remaining proof is this adjacent tangent-separation theorem.

Now here is the clean attack, and I think it is the right one.

---

## 1. Near the boundary, the sign is already fixed

Let \(I_1<I_2\) be adjacent ECT intervals, meeting at a boundary point \(h_b\).

For \(s\in I_2\) close to \(h_b\) and \(t\in I_1\) close to \(h_b\), the local models already tell us:

- if the boundary is \(W=0\) with \(M\neq 0\), then the tangent-slope ranges on the two sides only touch at the boundary slope, so the tangent from the right branch cannot hit the left branch near the boundary;
- if the boundary is a generic \(M=0\) turning point, then the tangent slopes blow up with opposite signs on the two sides, so again a tangent from the right branch cannot hit the left branch near the boundary.

So:

\[
\boxed{
K(s,t)\text{ has a definite nonzero sign for }(s,t)\text{ near the adjacent boundary.}
}
\]

That is already stronger than the earlier local “harmlessness” statements.

---

## 2. If a zero appears later, it must appear by tangency

Now suppose, for contradiction, that there is some interior pair
\[
s_0\in I_2,\qquad t_0\in I_1
\]
with
\[
K(s_0,t_0)=0.
\]

Since near the boundary there are no such zeros, there must be a **first** right-interval point \(s_*\in I_2\) at which a zero appears on the left interval. More precisely, define
\[
S:=\{\,s\in I_2:\exists\,t\in I_1\text{ with }K(s,t)=0\,\}.
\]
By the boundary analysis, \(S\) does not accumulate at the left endpoint of \(I_2\). If \(S\neq\varnothing\), let \(s_*:=\inf S\) in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering.

Then there exists \(t_*\in I_1\) such that
\[
K(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]

Now here is the crucial point: this first zero cannot be transverse in \(t\). If it were,
\[
\partial_t K(s_*,t_*)\neq 0,
\]
then by the implicit function theorem there would be nearby zeros for \(s<s_*\), contradicting minimality of \(s_*\). Therefore
\[
\partial_t K(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]

So at the first appearance we have
\[
K(s_*,t_*)=0,\qquad \partial_t K(s_*,t_*)=0.
\]

---

## 3. That means a common tangent

But
\[
\partial_t K(s,t)
=
\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F'(t)\bigr).
\]
So at \((s_*,t_*)\),
\[
\det(F(s_*),F'(s_*),F(t_*))=0,
\qquad
\det(F(s_*),F'(s_*),F'(t_*))=0.
\]

The first says \(F(t_*)\) lies in the osculating plane generated by \(F(s_*),F'(s_*)\), i.e. \(\Gamma(t_*)\) lies on the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s_*)\).

The second says \(F'(t_*)\) lies in the same plane. Geometrically, that means the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s_*)\) is also tangent to the curve at \(\Gamma(t_*)\).

So the “first appearance of an adjacent intersection” forces a **common tangent line** to the two adjacent intervals.

This is the key reduction:
\[
\boxed{
\text{A failure of adjacent tangent separation would force a common tangent to adjacent intervals.}
}
\]

---

## 4. But adjacent intervals cannot share an interior tangent slope

Now use the boundary split again.

### Case A: adjacent intervals separated by \(W=0\), \(M\neq 0\)

Then
\[
m(h):=\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}
\]
satisfies
\[
m'(h)=\frac{W(h)}{M(h)^2}.
\]
Since \(W\) changes sign across the boundary and \(M\neq 0\), the slope ranges of the two adjacent intervals only meet at the boundary endpoint value. Their interiors are disjoint.

So no interior point of \(I_1\) and no interior point of \(I_2\) can have the same tangent slope.

### Case B: adjacent intervals separated by a generic \(M=0\) turning point

Then
\[
m(h)=\frac{N(h)}{M(h)}\to \pm\infty
\]
with opposite signs on the two sides. Again, the tangent-slope ranges of the adjacent intervals are disjoint.

So in both cases:

\[
\boxed{
\text{Adjacent ECT intervals cannot share a common tangent line at interior points.}
}
\]

This contradicts the conclusion of Step 3.

Therefore the assumed zero cannot occur.

Hence:

\[
\boxed{
K(s,t)\neq 0\qquad\text{for all }s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1.
}
\]

That is the adjacent tangent-separation theorem.

---

## 5. What this gives immediately

Fix adjacent intervals \(I_1<I_2\). For any slope \(m\) realized on \(I_2\), let \(L_m\) be the support/tangent line of \(I_2\) with slope \(m\). Since \(L_m\) is a tangent from the right interval, the theorem says it does not meet \(I_1\). Therefore the whole left interval lies strictly on one side of \(L_m\), i.e.
\[
M_1(m)\le m_2(m).
\]

So adjacent support intervals are disjoint:
\[
\mathcal I_1(m)\cap \mathcal I_2(m)=\varnothing.
\]

By repeating this for each adjacent pair, we get transitivity:
\[
M_i(m)\le m_{i+1}(m)\le M_{i+1}(m)\le \cdots \le m_k(m),
\]
so no two separated intervals have overlapping support windows either.

Therefore:

\[
\boxed{
\text{No affine line can be 2-secant to two different ECT intervals.}
}
\]

And that is exactly what was needed to kill the remaining pair-pair obstruction.

---

So the attack result is:

1. adjacent failure would force a first zero of \(K(s,t)\);
2. first zero forces a common tangent;
3. adjacent intervals cannot share an interior tangent slope;
4. therefore adjacent tangent separation holds;
5. therefore adjacent support barriers hold;
6. therefore all support barriers hold by transitivity;
7. therefore there are no common 4-secants at all.

This is the first point where the global obstruction really looks close to closed.

The next attack is not more new geometry. It is to **freeze the global argument you just found** and stress-test its weak points.

The right order now is:

First, turn the adjacent tangent-separation argument into a precise theorem. The core statement is:

\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))\neq 0
\qquad
(s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1)
\]
for adjacent ECT intervals \(I_1<I_2\), where
\[
F(h)=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]

That is the real hinge. Once that is proved cleanly, the rest is mostly corollaries.

Second, audit the “first zero” argument very carefully. That is where I would attack next, because it is the place most likely to hide a gap. The exact subclaims to verify are:

- the set
  \[
  S=\{\,s\in I_2:\exists t\in I_1,\ K(s,t)=0\,\}
  \]
  is closed enough to admit a first point in the \(x=v_5/c\) ordering;
- at the first point \((s_*,t_*)\), transversality in \(t\) really does fail, so
  \[
  \partial_t K(s_*,t_*)=0;
  \]
- \(K=0\) and \(\partial_tK=0\) really do imply a common tangent line, not just some weaker projective incidence;
- the tangent-slope intervals of adjacent branches are truly disjoint in the two boundary cases.

That is the proof audit I would do next. If one of those breaks, it is the first place it will break.

Third, once the adjacent theorem is solid, package the support-barrier corollary:
\[
M_1(m)\le m_2(m)
\]
for adjacent intervals, hence by transitivity no support-window overlap for any separated pair. That gives:
\[
\text{no affine line is 2-secant to two distinct ECT intervals.}
\]

Fourth, feed that directly into the bad-core endgame. At that point the right theorem to prove is no longer geometric. It is combinatorial:

- each ECT interval contributes at most two points;
- no two distinct intervals can both contribute a pair;
- adjacent intervals are already harmless;
- therefore any bad core is forced into a much smaller support pattern, likely one pair plus singletons, or all singletons.

So the next attack is:

\[
\boxed{
\text{formalize and stress-test the adjacent tangent-separation theorem.}
}
\]

That is the one place where the whole global closure now concentrates.

If you want the sharpest immediate sub-attack, it is this:

**prove rigorously that “first zero of \(K(s,t)\)” forces \(\partial_tK=0\), hence a common tangent, hence contradiction with disjoint adjacent slope ranges.**

That is the shortest path to either closing the argument or finding the real remaining gap.

# 1. Insert a projective-Wronskian / ECT block

## Insert this exact LaTeX

```latex
\begin{definition}[Projective Wronskian of the strengthened one-pair triple]
\label{def:projective-wronskian-c-v5-delta7}
Define
\[
W(h):=
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c(h) & v_5(h) & \Delta_7(h)\\
c'(h) & v_5'(h) & \Delta_7'(h)\\
c''(h) & v_5''(h) & \Delta_7''(h)
\end{pmatrix}.
\]
\end{definition}

\begin{proposition}[Projective-Wronskian bridge]
\label{prop:projective-wronskian-bridge}
With \(M(h)\) and \(N(h)\) as in
Definition~\ref{def:MN-normalized-curve}, one has
\[
M(h)N'(h)-N(h)M'(h)=c(h)\,W(h).
\]
Consequently,
\[
K_\Gamma(h)=\frac{W(h)}{c(h)^3}
\]
on the region where \(c(h)\neq 0\).
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
Using
\[
M=c\,v_5'-c'v_5,
\qquad
N=c\,\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7,
\]
a direct expansion gives
\[
M N'-N M'
=
(c\,v_5'-c'v_5)(c\,\Delta_7''-c''\Delta_7)
-
(c\,\Delta_7'-c'\Delta_7)(c\,v_5''-c''v_5).
\]
Expanding and regrouping yields
\[
M N'-N M'
=
c\,
\det\!
\begin{pmatrix}
c & v_5 & \Delta_7\\
c' & v_5' & \Delta_7'\\
c'' & v_5'' & \Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}
=
c\,W.
\]
The displayed formula for \(K_\Gamma\) now follows from
Proposition~\ref{prop:curvature-bridge-normalized-curve}.
\end{proof}

\begin{proposition}[ECT intervals for the strengthened one-pair triple]
\label{prop:ect-intervals-c-v5-delta7}
Let \(I\) be a connected interval on which
\[
c(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
M(h)\neq 0,
\qquad
W(h)\neq 0.
\]
Then the triple
\[
(c,\ v_5,\ \Delta_7)
\]
is an extended complete Chebyshev system on \(I\). Equivalently, every
nontrivial linear combination
\[
A\,c(h)+B\,v_5(h)+C\,\Delta_7(h)
\]
has at most two zeros on \(I\), counting multiplicity.

In particular, in the affine chart
\[
x=\frac{v_5}{c},\qquad y=\frac{\Delta_7}{c},
\]
every affine line meets \(\Gamma(I)\) in at most two points, counting
tangency with multiplicity.
\end{proposition}

\begin{proof}
On \(I\), the successive initial Wronskians are
\[
c,\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}c&v_5\\ c'&v_5'\end{pmatrix}=M,
\qquad
\det\!\begin{pmatrix}
c&v_5&\Delta_7\\
c'&v_5'&\Delta_7'\\
c''&v_5''&\Delta_7''
\end{pmatrix}=W.
\]
Since all three are nonvanishing on \(I\), the standard ECT criterion applies.
The affine-line statement follows by writing
\[
A\,c+B\,v_5+C\,\Delta_7=0
\]
as the equation of a line in the chart \((v_5/c,\Delta_7/c)\).
\end{proof}

\begin{definition}[Tangent-separator kernel]
\label{def:tangent-separator-kernel}
Let
\[
F(h):=(c(h),v_5(h),\Delta_7(h)).
\]
For \(s,t\) in the generic region, define
\[
K(s,t):=\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F(t)\bigr).
\]
For fixed \(s\), the zeros of \(t\mapsto K(s,t)\) are exactly the
intersections of the tangent line at \(\Gamma(s)\) with the curve
\(\Gamma(t)\).
\end{definition}

\begin{lemma}[Basic properties of the tangent-separator kernel]
\label{lem:tangent-separator-basic-properties}
For every \(s\) in the generic region,
\[
K(s,s)=0,
\qquad
\partial_tK(s,s)=0,
\qquad
\partial_t^2K(s,s)=W(s).
\]
In particular, on any interval where \(W\) has fixed sign, the tangent line at
\(\Gamma(s)\) is locally supporting.
\end{lemma}

\begin{proof}
The first two identities follow because, at \(t=s\), the third column of the
determinant equals \(F(s)\), and after differentiating once in \(t\) it equals
\(F'(s)\). Differentiating a second time gives
\[
\partial_t^2K(s,s)=
\det\!\bigl(F(s),F'(s),F''(s)\bigr)=W(s).
\qedhere
\]
\end{proof}

\begin{remark}[Audit: direct septic witness]
\label{rem:audit-direct-septic-witness}
The use of Proposition~\ref{prop:rho2-not-affine-in-rho1} still depends on the
claim that the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-driven slice of \(\Delta_7\) is generically
nonzero. The current draft contains the explicit \(K_1\)-transport formulas
and the \(I\)-directionality of the direct \(q^{(7)}\)-slice, but the final
generic noncancellation statement for that slice should still be audited as a
standalone local theorem.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: ECT criterion usage]
\label{rem:audit-ect-criterion-usage}
Proposition~\ref{prop:ect-intervals-c-v5-delta7} uses the standard ECT
criterion for triples of \(C^2\) functions with nonvanishing successive initial
Wronskians. The current draft should retain an explicit audit note that the
regularity and sign hypotheses needed for this criterion are satisfied on the
intended generic intervals.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: tangent-separator sign regularity]
\label{rem:audit-tangent-separator-sign-regularity}
The remaining global obstruction is the sign behavior of
\[
K(s,t)=\det(F(s),F'(s),F(t))
\]
across adjacent ECT intervals. What still needs to be audited is the claim that
for adjacent intervals \(I_1<I_2\), one has
\[
K(s,t)\neq 0
\qquad (s\in I_2,\ t\in I_1),
\]
equivalently, that tangent lines from the right interval do not meet the
adjacent left interval.
\end{remark}

\begin{remark}[Audit: global no-four-secant theorem]
\label{rem:audit-global-no-four-secant}
Assuming the adjacent tangent-separator sign theorem, the support intervals of
distinct ECT branches are disjoint, and hence no affine line can be 2-secant to
two different branches. The deduction from adjacent tangent separation to a
global no-four-secant theorem should still be audited carefully in the final
endgame writeup.
\end{remark}
```

What I would still flag after this holistic pass:

First, the draft now contains both the older schematic multi-pair/minimal-core language and the newer ECT/kernel language. That is not wrong, but it means the **global endgame is now clearly split into two layers**:
- the older schematic rigidity statements,
- the newer sharper tangent-separator/ECT reduction.
This is acceptable, but readers will feel that the newer layer is the real one.

Second, the remaining audited gaps are now much clearer than before. In the terms you asked about earlier:

### 1. Two-pair interaction / rigidity
This is **better than earlier**, but still not closed.  
Why better: the local package is much stronger now, and the multi-pair section no longer sits only on cubic line + first residual trace. The strengthened package \((c,v_5,\Delta_7)\), the curve \(\Gamma\), the Wronskian \(W\), and the tangent-separator kernel now give a much more intrinsic picture of what two-pair coincidence would mean.  
What is still missing: a finished quantitative control of the interaction corrections, or a finished global theorem excluding adjacent/separated secant interaction across ECT intervals.

### 2. Minimal bad-core exclusion
This is also **more plausible than earlier**, but still not finished.  
Why better: the “same-branch” obstruction is now much tighter because the ECT argument kills multi-zero behavior on a single interval, and the global obstruction has been compressed to sign-regularity of the tangent-separator kernel across adjacent intervals. That is a much sharper target than the earlier vague bad-core exceptional locus language.  
What is still missing: the actual adjacent tangent-separation theorem and the final deduction that no line can be 2-secant to two different ECT intervals.

### 3. Global extraction bridge
This is still the most delicate one conceptually, but it is **better organized now**.  
Why better: the proof architecture now has an explicit bridge:
\[
(c,v_5,\Delta_7)\rightsquigarrow \Gamma \rightsquigarrow W,\ M,\ N,\ K(s,t)
\rightsquigarrow \text{no 4-secant} \rightsquigarrow \text{no bad core}.
\]
Earlier, this layer was much more schematic. Now the paper actually names the right objects.  
What is still missing: a fully rigorous endgame theorem that turns tangent-separator sign regularity into a global no-four-secant theorem and then into the final RH contradiction.

So: **is RH starting to look more plausible?**  
Yes, in the sense that the structure is much more coherent and the remaining gap is narrower and more geometric than before. The proof now feels less like a pile of local invariants and more like a real program with a specific final target.

But I would still not say it looks close to “finished proof of RH” yet. The remaining gap is no longer diffuse, but it is still serious:
\[
\text{adjacent-interval tangent-separator sign regularity / global no-4-secant.}
\]
That is a real mathematical theorem, not just bookkeeping.

My overall verdict on the draft itself: **good to continue from here**. The key edits made it in, and the paper now reflects the actual state of the attack much better than the earlier versions.
